**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how TEFR is used as a screening metric in the conceptual design phase of a chemical plant project. Discuss the role of TEFR in technology selection between two competing licensor processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A refinery processes 2 million tonnes/year of crude oil (LHV = 44 MJ/kg). Annual energy inputs are: fuel oil 80,000 tonnes/year (LHV = 40 MJ/kg), grid electricity 30,000 MWh/year, and steam turbine work 100 TJ/year. Compute (i) the operational TEFR (excluding feedstock LHV), and (ii) the *primary* TEFR (including feedstock LHV). Comment on which is the more appropriate metric for *greenhouse-gas accounting*. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline:

**Part (a):** Discuss the project life cycle → screening stage → TEFR computation → benchmark comparison → go/no-go decision → pinch analysis. Use the block diagram from Section 4.2 above.

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Thermal energy from fuel oil:

$$
E_{th} = 80{,}000 \times 10^3 \times 40 = 3.2 \times 10^9 \text{ MJ} = 3{,}200 \text{ TJ}
$$

Step 2 — Electrical energy:

$$
E_e = 30{,}000 \text{ MWh} = 30{,}000 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} = 108 \text{ TJ}
$$

Step 3 — Mechanical energy (given): $E_m = 100 \text{ TJ}$.

Step 4 — Operational TEFR:

$$
\text{TEFR}_{\text{op}} = \frac{3{,}200 + 108 + 100}{2{,}000{,}000} \text{ GJ/tonne} = \frac{3{,}408 \text{ TJ}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ t}} = 1.704 \text{ GJ/tonne}
$$

Step 5 — Feedstock chemical energy:

$$
E_c = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ t} \times 44 \text{ MJ/kg} = 8.8 \times 10^{10} \text{ MJ} = 88{,}000 \text{ TJ}
$$

Step 6 — Primary TEFR:

$$
\text{TEFR}_{\text{primary}} = \frac{3{,}408 + 88{,}000}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{91{,}408 \text{ TJ}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ t}} = 45.704 \text{ GJ/tonne}
$$

Step 7 — For **greenhouse-gas accounting**, the *operational TEFR* is more appropriate, because the CO₂ released from the *feedstock's* own chemical energy is not emitted by the *plant* (it is released downstream when the fuel is combusted in the end-use application, e.g., a car's engine). The plant's responsibility is its *own* combustion emissions, captured by $\text{TEFR}_{\text{op}}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often include $E_c$ without realizing it double-counts emissions if the same feed is also being sold as fuel. Always clarify whether you are computing **operational** or **primary** TEFR. Marks lost: typically **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **TEFR** = Total Energy for Feed Ratio, units **GJ/tonne feed**.
- ✅ Four energy vectors: **Thermal, Electrical, Mechanical, Chemical (feedstock LHV)**.
- ✅ Two conventions: **Operational TEFR** (excludes $E_c$) vs. **Primary TEFR** (includes $E_c$).
- ✅ **SEC** is the *product-basis* sibling metric; conversion factor = $m_{feed}/m_{product}$.
- ✅ Key conversion: **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3.6 × 10⁻³ GJ**.
- ✅ Boiler/furnace **efficiency** must be applied to fuel energy to get *useful* thermal energy.
- ✅ TEFR is a **screening metric** in Module 1, not a final design target — full optimization comes in Pinch Analysis (Module 2/3).
- ✅ **Higher TEFR ≠ bad process**; always interpret in context of feed-to-product ratio.
- ✅ For **CO₂ accounting**, use **operational TEFR**; for **thermodynamic benchmarking**, use **primary TEFR**.
- ✅ KTU convention: energy reported on **LHV basis** unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ Always express TEFR with **3 significant figures** in KTU numerical answers.
- ✅ Annual energy cost = $\text{TEFR}_{\text{annual}} \times p_{\text{fuel}}$; remember to use the **correct price unit** (₹/GJ for thermal, ₹/kWh for electricity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, *always* compute TEFR in **two ways** (operational and primary) and *comment* on the difference. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for the comparative discussion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Laboratory Development of New Process - Bench Scale Experiments - Pilot Plant Studies - Semi-Commercial Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Importance of Laboratory Development of New Process - Bench Scale Experiments - Pilot Plant Studies - Semi-Commercial Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Process Development – The Journey from Idea to Industry

In **Project Engineering** and **Chemical Process Technology**, no new chemical process is ever directly built at industrial scale. The development of any new process follows a strictly hierarchical path — from a test-tube in a research lab, to a bench-top apparatus, to a small outdoor unit, to a semi-commercial plant, and finally to a full-fledged commercial plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Process Development** is the systematic, scientifically controlled sequence of investigations — Laboratory Research → Bench Scale → Pilot Plant → Semi-Commercial → Commercial Plant — performed to acquire reliable engineering data (kinetics, thermodynamics, mass/heat transfer coefficients, yields) and to verify technical, economic, and operational feasibility before committing the multi-crore capital required for a full-scale industrial plant.

The fundamental purpose of this staged development is to **minimize technical and financial risk**. The larger the scale, the higher the cost of failure. A wrong decision in a 50 mL beaker costs a few hundred rupees; a wrong decision in a 5000-tonne plant costs crores and possibly human lives.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Cooking Recipe Trial"

Imagine a chef inventing a new dish for a 5-star hotel menu:

| Chef's Stage | Engineering Equivalent | Scale |
|---|---|---|
| Tries recipe in a small bowl for 1 person | **Laboratory / Bench Scale** | Grams, mL |
| Cooks in full kitchen utensils for 5–10 people | **Pilot Plant** | Kilograms, Litres |
| Runs a special counter at the hotel for 50 guests | **Semi-Commercial Plant** | Hundreds of kg |
| Adds to the full buffet for 500 guests | **Commercial Plant** | Tonnes |

Just as the chef does NOT directly cook 500 portions without testing taste, yield, cooking time, and cost — the chemical engineer does NOT build a 50,000 TPA plant without first verifying the chemistry at lab scale, engineering at pilot scale, and economics at semi-commercial scale.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Hierarchy (must memorize for KTU):**
> **Lab → Bench → Pilot → Semi-Commercial → Commercial**
> Each step answers a specific question:
> - Lab/Bench → *CAN it be done?* (Chemistry)
> - Pilot → *HOW can it be done reliably?* (Engineering)
> - Semi-Commercial → *IS it economically viable?* (Economics)
> - Commercial → *Produce at market scale* (Profit)

## 1.3 The Scale-Up Multiplier (Typical Engineering Magnitude)

| Stage | Typical Capacity | Approximate Volume |
|---|---|---|
| Laboratory / Bench | 0.1 – 10 kg/day | 0.001 – 0.1 m³ |
| Pilot Plant | 50 – 5000 kg/day | 0.5 – 10 m³ |
| Semi-Commercial | 5 – 500 tonnes/day | 10 – 100 m³ |
| Commercial Plant | > 1000 tonnes/day | > 100 m³ |

The scale-up factor between successive stages is typically **10× to 100×** in capacity. This gradual increase is intentional — it allows engineers to detect and correct problems before they become catastrophic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Structured Notes

## 2.1 Stage 1 — Laboratory / Bench Scale Experiments

### 2.1.1 Definition
A **bench scale experiment** is performed on a laboratory workbench using **glassware and small reactors of volume typically less than 10 litres**. It is the first step where the proposed chemistry is translated into a measurable, reproducible process.

### 2.1.2 Objectives (Why we do it)
- **Establish reaction feasibility** — does the reaction occur? What are the products?
- **Determine optimum conditions** — temperature, pressure, pH, catalyst concentration, solvent ratio.
- **Measure equilibrium & kinetic data** — rate equations, activation energy, equilibrium conversion.
- **Identify by-products and side reactions** — selectivity analysis.
- **Screen catalysts** — evaluate activity, deactivation, regeneration cycles.
- **Generate mass & energy balance data** for preliminary design.

### 2.1.3 Typical Equipment
- **Round-bottom flasks**, **stirred glass reactors** (0.5 – 5 L)
- **Autoclaves** for high-pressure reactions
- **Chromatographs (GC, HPLC)** for product analysis
- **Spectrophotometers, titration setups**
- **Calorimeters** for heat of reaction measurement

### 2.1.4 Deliverables from Bench Scale
- Mass balance with **material yield > 90%** (typical target)
- Thermodynamic data: $\Delta H_r$, $\Delta G_r$, $K_{eq}$
- Kinetic parameters: rate constant $k$, activation energy $E_a$, reaction order
- Preliminary catalyst screening report
- Identification of **corrosive, hazardous, or toxic intermediates**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Bench scale is the *cheapest* stage to fail. Roughly **60–70%** of proposed processes are rejected at this stage itself, saving enormous downstream cost.

## 2.2 Stage 2 — Pilot Plant Studies

### 2.2.1 Definition
A **pilot plant** is an **integrated, small-scale replica of the proposed commercial process** that operates continuously (or semi-continuously) and uses actual process equipment — reactors, separators, heat exchangers, pumps — but at a much smaller capacity (typically 50 – 5000 kg/day).

### 2.2.2 Objectives (Why we need it even after bench scale)
Bench scale uses *ideal* lab conditions. A pilot plant introduces **real engineering problems**:
- **Continuous operation** vs. batch in lab
- **Real material of construction** (stainless steel vs. glass)
- **Heat removal / addition** at industrial rates
- **Mixing and residence time distribution** in large vessels
- **Phase separation** under real flow conditions
- **Long-term catalyst stability** (hundreds of hours)
- **Wastewater and off-gas characterization**

### 2.2.3 Scale-Up Criteria (Critical KTU Concept)

When scaling up, certain dimensionless numbers are kept constant to preserve hydrodynamic and transport similarity:

$$\text{Reynolds Number (Re)} = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \text{constant}$$

$$\text{Froude Number (Fr)} = \frac{v^2}{g \, D} = \text{constant}$$

$$\text{Power Number (Po)} = \frac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5} = \text{constant}$$

$$\text{Peclet Number (Pe)} = \frac{v \, L}{D_{ax}} = \text{constant}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density, $v$ = velocity, $D$ = impeller diameter
- $\mu$ = viscosity, $g$ = gravitational acceleration
- $P$ = power input, $N$ = impeller rotational speed
- $D_{ax}$ = axial dispersion coefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality (KTU favourite):** Geometric similarity (same shape, ratios) is **mandatory**, kinematic similarity (same velocity ratios) is **desirable**, and dynamic similarity (same force ratios) is the **ultimate goal** — but impossible to achieve perfectly. This is why pilot plant data is so crucial.

### 2.2.4 Information Generated
- **Heat transfer coefficients (U)** for actual industrial fluids
- **Pressure drop** across reactors, pipes, packed columns
- **Actual energy consumption** (kWh/tonne of product)
- **Operator skill requirements and safety protocols**
- **Preliminary P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)**
- **Equipment sizing data** — final multiplier for commercial design

## 2.3 Stage 3 — Semi-Commercial (Demonstration) Plant

### 2.3.1 Definition
A **semi-commercial plant** (also called a **demonstration plant** or **pre-commercial plant**) is a **partial-scale, revenue-generating version of the final commercial plant**, typically producing **5–25%** of the proposed commercial capacity. It uses full-scale equipment (or at least industrial-grade equipment) and operates long enough to generate **bankable economic data**.

### 2.3.2 Objectives
- **Validate long-term operability** over months/years (vs. days/weeks at pilot).
- **Confirm product quality, purity, and specifications** meet market demands.
- **Test multiple product grades** by varying operating conditions.
- **Train operators** for the future commercial plant.
- **Generate market samples** for customer trials and acceptance.
- **Provide bankable cost data** — actual CAPEX and OPEX for the commercial plant.
- **De-risk the technology** for investors and lenders.

### 2.3.3 Semi-Commercial vs. Pilot Plant (Exam-Ready Comparison)

| Parameter | Pilot Plant | Semi-Commercial Plant |
|---|---|---|
| Scale | 50 – 5000 kg/day | 5 – 500 tonnes/day |
| Equipment | Lab/small-scale, sometimes glass | Full industrial-grade |
| Operation duration | Days to weeks | Months to years |
| Purpose | Engineering data | Economic + market data |
| Products sold? | Rarely | Yes, to test markets |
| Operator training? | Limited | Full-scale |

## 2.4 Stage 4 — Commercial Plant

Only after successful semi-commercial operation does the company sanction the **full-scale commercial plant**. The design is now based on **proven data, real cost figures, and demonstrated market acceptance**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Capacity scale-up ratio | $S_R = \dfrac{V_{large}}{V_{small}}$ | Compare plant capacities |
| Linear scale-up (geometry) | $D_{large} = D_{small} \cdot S_R^{1/3}$ | Maintain cube/sphere similarity |
| Constant power per unit volume | $\dfrac{P_1}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{V_2}$ | For mixing similarity |
| Surface-to-volume ratio | $\dfrac{A}{V} \propto \dfrac{1}{D}$ | Heat transfer challenge at large scale |
| Pilot-to-commercial factor | Typical: 10× to 50× | Rough estimate for design |
| Material balance | $\sum F_{in} = \sum F_{out} + \text{Accumulation}$ | Valid at every scale |
| Scale-up safety factor | Design margin: **+10% to +25%** | Conservative design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Scale-Up Insight (Very High Yield for KTU):**
> Heat transfer area scales as $D^2$, but volume scales as $D^3$.
> Therefore, **surface-to-volume ratio decreases with scale**:
> $$\frac{A}{V} \propto \frac{1}{D}$$
> This is why **exothermic reactions become harder to control at large scale** — pilot data on heat removal is essential.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Scale-Up Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why Heat Transfer Becomes a Bottleneck at Large Scale

We want to show mathematically why pilot plant heat-transfer data is indispensable.

**Step 1: Define heat generation rate per unit volume**
For an exothermic reaction with rate $r$ (mol/m³·s) and heat of reaction $\Delta H_r$ (J/mol):
$$q_{gen} = (-\Delta H_r) \cdot r \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^3}\right]$$

This is **volumetric** — it scales with $V$, i.e., with $D^3$.

**Step 2: Define heat removal rate per unit volume**
For a vessel with surface area $A$ and overall heat transfer coefficient $U$:
$$q_{rem} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{V} \quad \left[\frac{W}{m^3}\right]$$

For a sphere (worst case for surface-to-volume ratio), $A = \pi D^2$ and $V = \frac{\pi D^3}{6}$:
$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{\pi D^2}{\pi D^3 / 6} = \frac{6}{D}$$

**Step 3: Express $q_{rem}$ in terms of diameter**
$$q_{rem} = U \cdot \frac{6}{D} \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 4: Compare generation vs. removal at different scales**
Heat generation is independent of $D$ (per unit volume).
Heat removal **decreases with $D$** as $\frac{1}{D}$.

**Step 5: Form the scale-up ratio**
$$\frac{q_{rem,\, large}}{q_{rem,\, small}} = \frac{U_{large} \cdot (6/D_{large}) \cdot \Delta T_{large}}{U_{small} \cdot (6/D_{small}) \cdot \Delta T_{small}}$$

If $U$ and $\Delta T$ are kept approximately constant:
$$\frac{q_{rem,\, large}}{q_{rem,\, small}} = \frac{D_{small}}{D_{large}}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**
For a 10× scale-up in diameter (i.e., 1000× volume), heat removal capability per unit volume drops to **1/10th**.
This is why a reaction perfectly controlled at lab scale can **run away thermally** at commercial scale.
**Pilot plant studies are therefore essential to measure real $U$ values at intermediate scale.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Geometric Similarity and Linear Scale-Up

If two reactors (small and large) are **geometrically similar** (same shape, all linear dimensions scaled by the same factor $k$):

$$D_{large} = k \cdot D_{small}$$
$$H_{large} = k \cdot H_{small}$$

**Step 1: Volume ratio**
$$\frac{V_{large}}{V_{small}} = \frac{(\pi/4) D_{large}^2 H_{large}}{(\pi/4) D_{small}^2 H_{small}} = k^3$$

**Step 2: Surface area ratio**
$$\frac{A_{large}}{A_{small}} = \frac{D_{large} H_{large}}{D_{small} H_{small}} = k^2$$

**Step 3: Solving for $k$ in terms of capacity ratio $S_R$**
$$k = S_R^{1/3} = \left(\frac{V_{large}}{V_{small}}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4: Example numerical evaluation**
Let pilot reactor volume = 0.1 m³, target commercial volume = 1000 m³.
$$k = \left(\frac{1000}{0.1}\right)^{1/3} = 10000^{1/3} = 21.54$$

So every linear dimension in the commercial reactor is **21.54 times** larger than in the pilot.

**Step 5: Cross-check area scaling**
$$\frac{A_{large}}{A_{small}} = k^2 = 21.54^2 \approx 464$$

The commercial reactor has 464× more cooling surface — but its volume is 10,000× more. Hence the **per-unit-volume cooling capacity drops**:
$$\frac{(A/V)_{large}}{(A/V)_{small}} = \frac{k^2}{k^3} = \frac{1}{k} = \frac{1}{21.54} \approx 0.046$$

Only **4.6%** of the pilot's cooling capacity per unit volume is available at commercial scale — confirming Derivation 1.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Constant Power-Per-Volume for Mixing Scale-Up

To maintain similar mixing intensity when scaling up an agitated tank:

**Step 1: Power Number definition**
$$Po = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}$$

In the **turbulent regime**, $Po$ is essentially constant (independent of Re).

**Step 2: For geometrically similar tanks, if $Po$ is constant:**
$$P_1 = P_2 \cdot \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^5$$

**Step 3: Imposing constant Power per unit Volume**
$$\frac{P_1}{V_1} = \frac{P_2}{V_2}$$
$$P_1 = P_2 \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2} = P_2 \cdot k^3 \cdot \frac{1}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$
(since $V_1/V_2 = k^3$)

**Step 4: Equate the two expressions for $P_1$**
$$P_2 k^3 = P_2 N^3 D^5$$
$$N^3 D^5 = k^3$$
$$N^3 = \frac{k^3}{D^5}$$

**Step 5: Solve for new impeller speed $N_2$ (subscript 2 = large scale)**
$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot k^{-2/3}$$

**Step 6: Numerical example**
If $k = 21.54$ (from previous derivation):
$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot (21.54)^{-2/3} = N_1 \cdot 0.131$$

So the commercial-scale impeller must rotate at only **13.1%** of the pilot speed to maintain the same power per unit volume — otherwise mixing intensity would be too aggressive (excessive shear, foaming, or mechanical damage).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete Process Development Flow (KTU High-Yield Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STAGE 1 BASIC RESEARCH\nLiterature survey, theoretical studies\nIdentify target molecules"]
    nodeB["STAGE 2 LABORATORY BENCH SCALE\nGlassware, 0.1 to 10 L\nFeasibility, kinetics, catalyst screening"]
    nodeC["STAGE 3 PILOT PLANT\n50 to 5000 kg per day\nEngineering data, continuous operation"]
    nodeD["STAGE 4 SEMI COMMERCIAL PLANT\n5 to 500 tonnes per day\nEconomic data, market samples"]
    nodeE["STAGE 5 COMMERCIAL PLANT\nGreater than 1000 tonnes per day\nFull scale production"]
    nodeF["REJECT DECISION\nProcess abandoned at this stage"]
    nodeG["FEEDBACK LOOP\nData sent back to refine earlier stages"]
    nodeH["INVESTMENT DECISION\nCAPEX and OPEX confirmed by banker"]
    nodeI["DESIGN ENGINEERING\nP and ID, equipment sizing"]
    nodeJ["MARKET LAUNCH\nProduct sold to customers"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeB -.->|60 to 70 percent rejected| nodeF
    nodeC -.->|data refinement| nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeJ
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic at Each Scale (Examiner's Favourite Question)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Bench Scale Experiment\nCompleted in 2 to 6 months"]
    nodeB{"Does reaction proceed\nwith acceptable yield?"}
    nodeC["Proceed to Pilot Plant"]
    nodeD["Stop: Reject Process"]
    nodeE["Pilot Plant Study\n6 to 18 months operation"]
    nodeF{"Can process run\ncontinuously and safely?"}
    nodeG["Proceed to Semi Commercial"]
    nodeH["Return to Bench Scale\nfor further research"]
    nodeI["Semi Commercial Plant\n12 to 36 months operation"]
    nodeJ{"Is product quality\nand economics viable?"}
    nodeK["Approve Commercial Plant"]
    nodeL["Return to Pilot for\nprocess modification"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|Yes yield above 80 percent| nodeC
    nodeB -->|No| nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|Yes safe continuous| nodeG
    nodeF -->|No instability detected| nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|Yes profitable| nodeK
    nodeJ -->|No quality or cost issue| nodeL
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram – Information Flow Between Scales

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["LABORATORY\nKinetic constants\nEquilibrium data\nCatalyst activity"]
    nodeB["PILOT PLANT\nHeat transfer coefficients\nMass transfer rates\nReactor residence time"]
    nodeC["SEMI COMMERCIAL\nActual operating cost\nProduct specifications\nMarket acceptance data"]
    nodeD["COMMERCIAL PLANT\nFinal optimized design\nBankable feasibility report"]

    nodeA ==>|"Scaled by 10x to 100x"| nodeB
    nodeB ==>|"Scaled by 10x to 50x"| nodeC
    nodeC ==>|"Scaled by 5x to 25x"| nodeD
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Why Each Stage is Needed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SQ1["QUESTIONS ANSWERED AT EACH STAGE"]
        direction TB
        nodeA1["Bench Scale:\nCAN the chemistry work?"]
        nodeA2["Pilot Plant:\nHOW does the engineering work?"]
        nodeA3["Semi Commercial:\nIS it economically viable?"]
        nodeA4["Commercial:\nPRODUCE at market scale"]
    end

    subgraph SQ2["DATA GENERATED AT EACH STAGE"]
        direction TB
        nodeB1["Yield, selectivity, kinetics\nThermodynamic data\nCatalyst life short term"]
        nodeB2["Continuous operation data\nReal heat and mass transfer\nEquipment sizing curves"]
        nodeB3["Long term catalyst stability\nOperator training data\nActual CAPEX and OPEX\nProduct samples for market"]
        nodeB4["Bankable cost data\nProven P and ID\nTrained operators"]
    end

    subgraph SQ3["RISK REDUCTION AT EACH STAGE"]
        direction TB
        nodeC1["Low investment\nHigh rejection possible"]
        nodeC2["Medium investment\nTechnical de risking"]
        nodeC3["High investment\nFinancial de risking"]
        nodeC4["Very high investment\nProduction proven"]
    end

    nodeA1 --- nodeB1
    nodeA1 --- nodeC1
    nodeA2 --- nodeB2
    nodeA2 --- nodeC2
    nodeA3 --- nodeB3
    nodeA3 --- nodeC3
    nodeA4 --- nodeB4
    nodeA4 --- nodeC4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is laboratory development of a new process considered the most critical stage in process engineering?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Laboratory development is the most critical stage because:
1. **(1 mark)** It is the **cheapest stage** to fail — a failed experiment costs only a few thousand rupees, whereas a failed commercial plant costs crores.
2. **(1 mark)** It establishes the **fundamental chemistry** — reaction feasibility, yield, selectivity, catalyst activity, kinetics, and thermodynamics.
3. **(1 mark)** Roughly **60–70% of proposed processes are rejected at the bench scale itself**, preventing huge downstream losses. Data generated here forms the foundation for all pilot plant and commercial design.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between a pilot plant and a semi-commercial plant."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Pilot Plant | Semi-Commercial Plant |
|---|---|---|
| **(1 mark)** Capacity | 50 – 5000 kg/day | 5 – 500 tonnes/day |
| **(1 mark)** Operation duration | Days to weeks | Months to years |
| **(1 mark)** Primary purpose | Engineering & design data | Economic & market data |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **stages involved in the development of a new chemical process** from laboratory to commercial scale. State the objectives of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **importance of bench scale and pilot plant studies** with suitable examples. Why is data from the pilot plant considered indispensable for commercial plant design? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The development of a new chemical process follows a systematic, hierarchical sequence:

1. **(1 mark) Basic / Fundamental Research:** Literature survey, identification of target molecules, theoretical studies, thermodynamic analysis to check reaction feasibility.

2. **(1.5 marks) Laboratory / Bench Scale Experiments:** Conducted in glassware and small reactors (0.1 – 10 L). Objectives are:
   - Verify the chemical reaction
   - Determine optimum conditions (T, P, pH, catalyst, solvent)
   - Measure yield, selectivity, kinetic parameters ($k$, $E_a$), and thermodynamic data ($\Delta H_r$, $K_{eq}$)
   - Identify by-products, hazardous intermediates
   - Perform initial catalyst screening

3. **(1.5 marks) Pilot Plant Studies:** Integrated small-scale replica at **50 – 5000 kg/day**. Objectives are:
   - Test **continuous operation** vs. batch in lab
   - Use **real materials of construction** (SS, CS, alloys)
   - Measure **real heat transfer coefficients, mass transfer rates, pressure drops**
   - Verify long-term catalyst stability
   - Generate data for **equipment sizing and P&ID preparation**

4. **(1.5 marks) Semi-Commercial Plant:** 5–25% of commercial capacity (5–500 tonnes/day). Objectives are:
   - Validate **long-term operability** (months/years)
   - Generate **bankable economic data** — actual CAPEX and OPEX
   - Produce **market samples** for customer trials
   - **Train operators** for the commercial plant
   - **De-risk the technology** for investors

5. **(1.5 marks) Commercial Plant:** Full-scale production > 1000 tonnes/day. Design is now based on **proven data** with confidence in chemistry, engineering, and economics.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Importance of Bench Scale Studies (3 marks):**
- **Cheapest stage to test and reject** — saves enormous downstream cost
- Determines **reaction pathway, kinetics, and optimum conditions**
- Identifies **toxic, corrosive, or hazardous materials** early
- Provides **preliminary mass and energy balance** for the process
- **Example:** In the development of the **Monsanto acetic acid process** (methanol carbonylation), bench-scale studies identified the optimal rhodium-based catalyst and iodide co-catalyst concentrations before pilot work.

**Importance of Pilot Plant Studies (4 marks):**
- Provides **engineering data impossible to obtain at bench scale**:
  - Real heat transfer coefficients $U$ for industrial fluids
  - Actual pressure drops in reactors and columns
  - Continuous operation behaviour, start-up, and shut-down procedures
- Tests **material of construction** — corrosion, erosion, fouling
- Generates **operator skill requirements and safety protocols**
- Provides **equipment sizing data** for commercial design
- **Example:** In the **Fischer-Tropsch process** for synthetic fuels, pilot plants at Sasol (South Africa) operated for years before commercial scale-up, providing critical data on catalyst deactivation, heat removal, and product distribution.

> **Conclusion:** Pilot plant data is indispensable because the **scale-up of heat transfer, mixing, and residence time cannot be reliably predicted from bench-scale correlations alone** — a pilot plant is the only place where real industrial-grade equipment and continuous operating conditions can be tested at manageable cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is meant by **"scale-up"** in process engineering? Explain the **important scale-up criteria** used in the transition from pilot plant to commercial plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **complete flow of process development** from laboratory to commercial plant. Why is the **semi-commercial plant** considered a bridge between pilot and commercial plants? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Scale-up is the systematic methodology of **translating process data obtained at a smaller scale (bench or pilot) to design a larger, economically optimal commercial plant**, while preserving critical hydrodynamic, thermal, and chemical performance.

**Scale-up Criteria (6 marks):**

1. **(1.5 marks) Geometric Similarity:** All linear dimensions scaled by the same factor $k = (V_{large}/V_{small})^{1/3}$. The same shape is preserved.

2. **(1.5 marks) Kinematic Similarity:** Velocity ratios between corresponding points are kept constant. Achieved by maintaining equal **impeller tip speed**: $N_1 D_1 = N_2 D_2$.

3. **(1.5 marks) Dynamic Similarity:** Ratios of all relevant forces (inertial, viscous, gravitational) are preserved. Implemented by keeping **dimensionless numbers constant**:
   - Reynolds number: $Re = \rho v D / \mu$
   - Froude number: $Fr = v^2 / (g D)$
   - Power number: $Po = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$
   - Peclet number for residence time: $Pe = v L / D_{ax}$

4. **(1 mark) Constant Power per Unit Volume:** For mixing, $P/V$ is kept constant, which gives $N_2 = N_1 \cdot k^{-2/3}$.

5. **(0.5 mark) Constant Heat Transfer Coefficient per Unit Volume:** Particularly critical for exothermic reactions because $A/V \propto 1/D$ decreases with scale.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Refer to the flow diagram in Section 4.1.

The flow of process development is:
**Basic Research → Bench Scale → Pilot Plant → Semi-Commercial → Commercial Plant**

**Semi-Commercial Plant as a Bridge (4 marks):**
- It operates at **5–25% of commercial capacity** using industrial-grade equipment.
- It provides **long-term operational data (months to years)** which the pilot plant cannot generate.
- It produces **actual saleable product samples** for **market testing and customer acceptance**.
- It generates **bankable CAPEX and OPEX data** required for **financial closure** of the commercial project.
- It allows **full operator training** under near-real conditions.
- It is the **final de-risking step** before committing the large capital of a commercial plant — hence it acts as a bridge.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "pilot plant" with "semi-commercial plant".** Many students write the same answer for both. The key difference is **economic validation** — semi-commercial is for *profit* and *market* testing, pilot is for *engineering data*.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the bench scale** and write directly about the pilot plant. KTU specifically asks about **laboratory development importance** — the first 60–70% rejection rate is a guaranteed mark.
> 3. **Do NOT forget the formulas** $A/V \propto 1/D$ and $N_2 = N_1 k^{-2/3}$. These are the most-asked scale-up relations and skipping them loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Do NOT write generic "process development" without stages.** Always write the **specific objective** of each stage (chemistry / engineering / economics / production).
> 5. **Do NOT ignore dimensionless numbers** (Re, Fr, Po, Pe) in scale-up answers — KTU examiners specifically look for them.
> 6. **Avoid one-word answers** like "Yield is calculated" — always quantify ("Yield > 80%", "Scale-up factor 10–50×", "Capacity 50–5000 kg/day").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this 1 hour before KTU exam:**

- ✅ **Process Development Hierarchy:** *Basic Research → Bench → Pilot → Semi-Commercial → Commercial*
- ✅ **Bench Scale:** Glassware, **0.1 – 10 L**, answers *"CAN it be done?"* (chemistry)
- ✅ **Pilot Plant:** **50 – 5000 kg/day**, answers *"HOW can it be done?"* (engineering, continuous)
- ✅ **Semi-Commercial Plant:** **5 – 500 tonnes/day**, answers *"IS it viable?"* (economics + market)
- ✅ **Commercial Plant:** **> 1000 tonnes/day**, full production
- ✅ **60–70% processes rejected at bench scale** — saves downstream cost
- ✅ **Scale-up ratio per stage:** typically **10× to 100×**
- ✅ **Critical scale-up relations:**
  - Linear dimension: $D_{large} = D_{small} \cdot k$ where $k = S_R^{1/3}$
  - Area: $A_{large} = A_{small} \cdot k^2$
  - Volume: $V_{large} = V_{small} \cdot k^3$
  - Power per volume: $P/V$ = constant → $N_2 = N_1 \cdot k^{-2/3}$
- ✅ **$A/V \propto 1/D$** — heat transfer is the biggest challenge in scale-up
- ✅ **Key dimensionless numbers for scale-up:** **Re, Fr, Po, Pe** (must be constant)
- ✅ **Pilot plant is indispensable** because it gives real $U$, real $\Delta P$, real catalyst life data
- ✅ **Semi-commercial plant is the "bridge"** — it provides bankable data and trains operators
- ✅ **Operations of pilot plant:** days to weeks; **semi-commercial:** months to years
- ✅ **Capital risk per stage (low → very high):** Lab → Pilot → Semi-Commercial → Commercial
- ✅ **KTU must-mention keywords:** *geometric similarity, dimensionless numbers, scale-up, P&ID, material of construction, residence time, heat transfer coefficient, mass transfer coefficient, de-risking, bankable data*
- ✅ **Final exam tip:** Always end your answer with a **single-line conclusion** like *"Thus, the staged development from bench to commercial ensures technical, economic, and operational feasibility before large capital commitment."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Engineering: Conceptual Process Design, Preliminary Process Design, and Detailed Process Design (FEED)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Engineering: Conceptual Process Design, Preliminary Process Design, and Detailed Process Design (FEED)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Process Engineering** is the discipline of designing, developing, optimizing, and operating industrial chemical, physical, and biological processes. In the context of **Project Engineering**, Process Engineering encompasses the systematic translation of a laboratory-proven chemical transformation into a fully engineered, safe, and economically viable industrial plant. It forms the *technical backbone* of any capital (CAPEX) project and directly dictates equipment sizing, material/energy balances, capital cost, and operability.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT524 – Project Engineering)**, Process Engineering is executed across **three sequential, gated phases**:

1. **Conceptual Process Design (CPD)** – The "Idea-to-Feasibility" stage.
2. **Preliminary Process Design (PPD)** – The "Feasibility-to-Basic-Engineering" stage.
3. **Detailed Process Design (DPD) / Front-End Engineering Design (FEED)** – The "Basic-to-Detailed-Engineering" stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** FEED (Front-End Engineering Design) is the *Basic Engineering* phase that precedes Detailed Design. It is the most critical deliverable of a Process Engineering team because it fixes >80% of the project capital cost and is the basis for EPC tendering.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to build a **house** on a 10-cent plot in Kerala:

| Design Stage | Engineering Equivalent | What You Decide |
|---|---|---|
| **Conceptual** | "Should I build a 2BHK or 3BHK?" | Plot area, number of rooms, rough budget (₹40L vs ₹80L) |
| **Preliminary** | Architectural Plan Submission | Room dimensions, structural type (RCC frame), water tank capacity, septic tank size |
| **Detailed (FEED)** | Construction Drawings | Exact rebar diameter, concrete M20/M25 grade, wiring gauge, plumbing trap sizes |

**The key insight:** In a house, you cannot change the *plot orientation* after construction. In a chemical plant, you cannot change the *reactor type* or *major equipment metallurgy* after procurement. The earlier the error is caught, the cheaper it is to fix. This is the **"Rule of Ten"** in Process Engineering — an error costing **₹1** to fix at the conceptual stage costs **₹10** at preliminary, **₹100** at detailed, and **₹1,000+** during plant operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical/Business Metric:** Industry-standard cost-estimate accuracy at the end of each phase (per AACE Class):
> - **Conceptual:** Class 4 → accuracy **± 30% to ± 50%**
> - **Preliminary:** Class 3 → accuracy **± 20% to ± 30%**
> - **Detailed (FEED):** Class 2 → accuracy **± 10% to ± 15%**
> - **Procurement/Construction:** Class 1 → accuracy **± 5% to ± 10%**

The standard engineering effort distribution is roughly **10% (Conceptual) : 30% (Preliminary) : 60% (Detailed/FEED)** of total process hours, but the **cost-fixation power** is nearly inverse: **80% of CAPEX is fixed by the end of FEED**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phase 1 — Conceptual Process Design (CPD)

**Objective:** Establish *whether* the project is technically and economically viable.

**Inputs:** Market study, feed-stock availability, product specification, regulatory framework, plant location.

**Engineering Activities:**
- Selection of the **process route / chemistry** (e.g., methanol via steam reforming vs. biomass gasification).
- Preliminary **block flow diagram (BFD)** — only major unit operations.
- First-pass **material and energy balance** using short-cut methods (stoichiometric, rule-of-thumb yields).
- Identification of **utility systems** (steam, cooling water, electricity, nitrogen).
- Preparation of an **Order-of-Magnitude (OOM) capital cost estimate**.
- Initial **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** brainstorming — *not* a formal study yet.

**Key Deliverables:**
- Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
- Process Concept Document
- Class 4 / OOM cost estimate
- Feasibility Report

**KTU High-Yield Point:** At this stage, **no P&ID, no equipment datasheets, no control philosophy** exists. Equipment is sized in terms of *order-of-volume* using heuristic rules (e.g., reflux ratio ≈ 1.2 × minimum reflux for distillation).

### 2.2 Phase 2 — Preliminary Process Design (PPD)

**Objective:** Develop the process to a level sufficient to define the project scope, plot plan, and major equipment list (MEL).

**Engineering Activities:**
- Rigorous **material and energy balance** using simulation software (ASPEN PLUS, HYSYS, PROMAX, DWSIM).
- Generation of **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)** with full stream data (T, P, composition, phase, flow rate).
- Preliminary **equipment sizing** (columns, vessels, pumps, compressors, heat exchangers) using shortcut and rating methods.
- Preliminary **Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)** — usually not released for construction, only for review.
- Development of a **Process Control Philosophy** (regulatory, override, and interlock strategy at a narrative level).
- Preparation of a **Class 3 cost estimate** with factored/parametric approach.
- **HAZOP Study 1** with full PFD-level nodes.
- Identification of **long-lead items** (e.g., large compressors, special metallurgy reactors, licensed columns).

**Key Deliverables:**
- Process Simulation Report
- PFDs with stream tables
- Preliminary Equipment List (MEL)
- Preliminary P&IDs (for design review, not construction)
- Class 3 Capital Cost Estimate
- HAZOP Register 1

### 2.3 Phase 3 — Detailed Process Design (DPD) / FEED

**Objective:** Produce a complete, unambiguous engineering package that an EPC contractor can build, procure, and commission *without* further process engineering input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern KTU/industry terminology, the words **FEED** and **Detailed Process Design** are used interchangeably for the *basic engineering* package. True "Detailed Design" / "Engineering for Fabrication" is the EPC phase that uses the FEED package.

**Engineering Activities:**
- **Hydraulic and rating calculations** for every pipe, control valve, and relief device.
- Finalization of **P&IDs** — every valve, instrument, and line numbered.
- **Equipment Data Sheets** for every tagged item (vessels, exchangers, pumps, columns, reactors).
- **Process Control Narrative** converted into Cause-&-Effect matrices and logic diagrams.
- **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** definition per **IEC 61511** (SIL targets).
- **Relief and flare system** sizing (API 520/521).
- **Utility and off-spec system** sizing.
- **Class 2 / Class 1 cost estimate** ready for FID (Final Investment Decision).
- **HAZOP Study 2** at P&ID level (mandatory for KTU projects in real practice).
- **3D model review** with piping and structural teams.

**Key Deliverables:**
- Final P&IDs (Issued for Design / IFD, then Issued for Construction / IFC)
- Full Equipment Data Sheets
- Line List, Valve List, Instrument Index
- Process Control Philosophy & Cause/Effect Charts
- Relief Sizing Report
- Class 2 Cost Estimate
- HAZOP Register 2 (closed-out actions)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Value / Range** | **Used In Phase** |
|---|---|---|---|
| AACE Cost Estimate Accuracy | $\sigma$ | **Class 4: ±50%**, Class 3: ±30%, Class 2: ±15%, Class 1: ±5% | All 3 |
| Engineering Hours Split | $H_{eng}$ | 10% : 30% : 60% (CPD : PPD : DPD) | Project Planning |
| Minimum Reflux Ratio ( Underwood) | $R_{m}$ | $R_m = \sum \frac{\alpha_i x_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta}$ | PPD/DPD |
| Actual Reflux Ratio | $R$ | $R \approx 1.2 \, R_m$ to $1.5 \, R_m$ | PPD/DPD |
| Pressure Drop Design (process lines) | $\Delta P$ | **0.3 to 0.5 psi/100 ft** for liquid, **0.1 to 0.5 psi/100 ft** for gas | DPD |
| Control Valve Authority | $\beta$ | $\beta \geq 0.5$ (typically **0.5–0.7**) | DPD |
| SIS Safety Integrity Level | SIL | 1, 2, 3 (PFD $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$) | DPD |
| Capital Cost Factor (Lang Factor) | $f_L$ | $C_{plant} = f_L \cdot C_{equipment}$ ; $f_L = 3.10$ (solid), **4.74** (fluid) | CPD |
| Power Law Scaling (Six-Tenths Rule) | $C_B$ | $C_B = C_A \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^n$, $n \approx 0.6$ to **0.8** | CPD/PPD |
| Relief Valve Orifice Area (API 520) | $A$ | $A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot K_b \cdot K_c} \sqrt{\frac{T \cdot Z}{M}}$ | DPD |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Lang Factor** and **Six-Tenths Rule** are the *two most-tested* equations in KTU Module 1 viva and Part A questions.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** FEED packages are issued by licensors (UOP, Shell, Lummus) and form the legal basis of LSTK (Lump-Sum Turn-Key) contracts.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Process Engineering defines the cGMP-compliant equipment train, which determines FDA/EMA audit pass-rate.
- **Green Hydrogen & Ammonia:** Modern FEED packages incorporate carbon-capture tie-ins, electrolyzer ramp-rate modeling, and dynamic simulation — none of which existed in the 1990s syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Six-Tenths Power Law (Cost Scaling)

The **Six-Tenths Rule** is the single most important concept in **Conceptual Process Design** because it allows rapid capital cost estimation before *any* equipment is fully sized.

**Step 1 — Empirical Starting Point**
From historical cost data of chemical plants, it has been observed that when the *capacity* of a process unit is doubled, the cost does **not** double. Instead, it follows a power law because of **economies of scale** in fabrication, installation, and instrumentation.

$$C = k \cdot S^{n}$$

where:
- $C$ = purchased cost of the equipment (in base-year currency)
- $S$ = characteristic size parameter (e.g., volume $V$, heat-transfer area $A$, flow rate $Q$)
- $k$ = cost constant (depends on material, pressure, and year)
- $n$ = scale exponent (typically 0.6 for complete plants, 0.8 for individual equipment)

**Step 2 — Taking the Ratio for Two Similar Units (A and B)**
To eliminate the constant $k$, divide the cost of unit B by the cost of unit A:

$$\frac{C_B}{C_A} = \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Solving for the Unknown Cost $C_B$**
This gives the final working form of the rule, the **Six-Tenths Power Law**:

$$C_B = C_A \cdot \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)**
A heat exchanger costs **₹ 8,00,000** for a heat-transfer area of **50 m²**. Estimate the cost of a similar exchanger with area **120 m²**. Take $n = 0.65$.

$$C_B = 8{,}00{,}000 \times \left( \frac{120}{50} \right)^{0.65}$$

$$C_B = 8{,}00{,}000 \times (2.4)^{0.65}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Exponent using Logarithms**

$$\ln(2.4) = 0.8755$$

$$0.8755 \times 0.65 = 0.5691$$

$$e^{0.5691} = 1.7667$$

$$C_B = 8{,}00{,}000 \times 1.7667 = \mathbf{₹ \, 14{,}13{,}360}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the capacity **increased by 2.4×** (140% rise), but the cost **increased by only 1.77×** (77% rise). This is the **economy of scale** that justifies building a single 1,00,000 TPA plant instead of ten 10,000 TPA plants.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Phase Comparison Table

| **Activity / Deliverable** | **Conceptual (CPD)** | **Preliminary (PPD)** | **Detailed (FEED / DPD)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Flow Diagram | ✔️ (sketch) | ✖️ | ✖️ |
| Process Flow Diagram (PFD) | ✖️ | ✔️ (with stream tables) | ✔️ (final) |
| P&ID | ✖️ | Preliminary (review) | Final (Issued for Construction) |
| Material Balance | Order-of-magnitude | Rigorous (simulator) | Rigorous + utility streams |
| Energy Balance | ✖️ / rough | ✔️ | ✔️ + integration study |
| Equipment Datasheet | ✖️ | Outline | Full datasheet |
| Cost Estimate Class | **Class 4 / OOM (±50%)** | **Class 3 (±30%)** | **Class 2 (±15%)** |
| HAZOP | Brainstorm | Study 1 (PFD-level) | Study 2 (P&ID-level) |
| Control Philosophy | ✖️ | Narrative | Cause/Effect + Logic diagrams |
| Relief / Flare Sizing | ✖️ | ✖️ | **Full API 520/521 calculation** |
| 3D Model Review | ✖️ | ✖️ | ✔️ (Piping, Structure clash) |
| % of CAPEX fixed at gate | ~ **30%** | ~ **60%** | ~ **80–90%** |
| Typical Duration | 2–4 months | 4–8 months | 6–12 months |
| Tools Used | Excel, heuristics | ASPEN/HYSYS, HTRI, hydraulics | Same + SmartPlant, COMOS, AVEVA |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Three Phases of Process Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Need + Lab Data] --> B[Phase 1: Conceptual Process Design]
    B --> C{Feasibility Gate: +/- 50% Accuracy}
    C -->|Approved| D[Phase 2: Preliminary Process Design]
    C -->|Rejected| Z1[Project Halted / Re-scoped]
    D --> E{Basic Engineering Gate: +/- 30% Accuracy}
    E -->|Approved| F[Phase 3: Detailed Process Design FEED]
    E -->|Rejected| Z2[Optimize and Re-enter PPD]
    F --> G{FID Gate: +/- 15% Accuracy}
    G -->|Approved| H[EPC Phase: Procurement + Construction + Commissioning]
    G -->|Rejected| Z3[Re-cycle Detailed Design]

    B --> B1[Outputs: BFD, Class 4 Cost, Feasibility Report]
    D --> D1[Outputs: PFD, MEL, Class 3 Cost, Preliminary P and ID]
    F --> F1[Outputs: Final P and ID, Datasheets, Class 2 Cost, HAZOP 2, Cause Effect]

    classDef gate fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    class C,E,G gate
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — FEED Deliverable Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph I[Inputs]
        I1[Approved PFD from PPD]
        I2[Site Data and Utilities]
        I3[Client Specifications]
    end

    subgraph P[Phase 3 FEED Activities]
        P1[Process Simulation Steady State + Dynamic]
        P2[Equipment Sizing and Rating]
        P3[P and ID Development]
        P4[Safety and Relief Sizing]
        P5[Control and Safeguarding]
    end

    subgraph O[Outputs FEED Package]
        O1[Final P and ID Issued for Design]
        O2[Equipment Data Sheets]
        O3[Line List and Valve List]
        O4[Cause and Effect Matrix]
        O5[SIL Classification Report]
        O6[Class 2 Cost Estimate]
        O7[HAZOP 2 Register Closed]
    end

    subgraph E[EPC Phase]
        E1[Procurement and Fabrication]
        E2[Construction and Commissioning]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P3
    P1 --> P3
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O2
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O3
    P3 --> O4
    P3 --> O5
    P4 --> O5
    P5 --> O4
    P1 --> O6
    P3 --> O7

    O1 --> E1
    O2 --> E1
    O3 --> E1
    O4 --> E1
    O5 --> E1
    O7 --> E1
    E1 --> E2

    classDef phase fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1.5px
    classDef out fill:#E8FFE8,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1.5px
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 phase
    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5,O6,O7 out
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Flow Diagram (BFD) — Sample Ammonia Plant (KTU-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Natural Gas Feed] --> M1[Reforming: Produce H2 and CO]
    A2[Steam + Air] --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Water Gas Shift: CO to CO2]
    M2 --> M3[CO2 Removal: Amine Absorber]
    M3 --> M4[Methanation: Purge CO and CO2]
    M4 --> A3[Liquid Ammonia Product]
    M4 --> S1[Recycle Loop: Purge and Recompress]

    classDef unit fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:1.5px
    class M1,M2,M3,M4 unit
```
> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly the level of detail expected in a **Conceptual BFD** — no temperatures, no pressures, no stream numbers. Anything more belongs to a PFD (Preliminary Phase).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "FEED" in the context of Process Engineering. Why is it considered the most critical phase of a capital project?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
FEED stands for **Front-End Engineering Design**. It is the Basic Engineering phase that follows Preliminary Design and precedes Detailed EPC Engineering. (1 mark)
It is critical because:
- It freezes >80% of the project's CAPEX. (1 mark)
- It produces the engineering package (P&ID, datasheets, HAZOP, Class 2 cost) used for EPC tendering and the Final Investment Decision (FID). (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Six-Tenths Rule and mention two of its limitations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Six-Tenths (Power Law) Rule relates the cost of similar process equipment at two different sizes:
$$C_B = C_A \cdot \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{n}, \quad n \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8$$
(1.5 marks for the equation and definition)

**Limitations (any two, 0.75 each):**
1. Valid only for **similar equipment** built in the **same year, same material, same pressure rating**.
2. Cannot be used if the *technology* or *materials of construction* change between A and B.
3. Fails at extreme size ratios ($S_B/S_A > 5$) due to constructability limits.
4. Does not account for inflation between the base year of $C_A$ and the target year.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three sequential phases of Process Engineering in a chemical plant project, clearly stating the objective, key activities, deliverables, and cost-estimate accuracy of each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flash drum for a refinery off-gas costs **₹ 12,00,000** for a vessel volume of **5 m³**. Estimate the cost of a similar drum with a volume of **20 m³**, using a scale exponent of **$n = 0.68$**. Comment on the result in the context of "economy of scale." **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Phase | Objective | Key Activities | Main Deliverable | Cost Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conceptual | Test feasibility | Route selection, BFD, OOM cost, market study | Feasibility Report, BFD, Class 4 Estimate | **± 30–50%** |
| Preliminary | Define project scope | Rigorous M&E balance, simulation, PFD, preliminary P&ID, HAZOP 1 | PFD with stream tables, MEL, Class 3 Estimate, Preliminary P&ID | **± 20–30%** |
| Detailed (FEED) | Produce build-ready package | Final P&ID, datasheets, hydraulics, relief sizing, SIL, HAZOP 2, Cause/Effect | Issued-for-Construction P&ID, Data Sheets, Class 2 Cost | **± 10–15%** |

(1 mark per row for clear comparison, 1 mark for the engineering effort split 10:30:60, 1 mark for stating that ~80% of CAPEX is fixed by end of FEED, 1 mark for HAZOP/SIL/relief being FEED-only activities.)

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Given:
- $C_A = 12{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $S_A = 5 \, \text{m}^3$
- $S_B = 20 \, \text{m}^3$
- $n = 0.68$

Using Six-Tenths Power Law:

$$C_B = C_A \cdot \left( \frac{S_B}{S_A} \right)^{n}$$

$$C_B = 12{,}00{,}000 \times \left( \frac{20}{5} \right)^{0.68}$$

$$C_B = 12{,}00{,}000 \times (4)^{0.68}$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$\ln(4) = 1.3863$$

$$1.3863 \times 0.68 = 0.9427$$

$$e^{0.9427} = 2.567$$

$$C_B = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 2.567 = \mathbf{₹ \, 30{,}80{,}400}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
The vessel volume increased **4×**, but the cost increased only **~2.57×**. This is the **economy of scale**: a single larger unit is *not* four times as expensive as a smaller one because fabrication labor, foundation cost, instrumentation, and indirect costs scale sub-linearly. Hence, single-train large plants are preferred over multiple smaller trains.

(Marks distribution: equation 2, log evaluation 2, final answer 1, comment 1, and 1 mark for clarity.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between a **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)**, a **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**, and a **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**. State at which design phase each is generated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fertilizer plant requires a new **Class 3** capital cost estimate. The total purchased equipment cost is **₹ 50 Crore** for a **fluid-processing plant**. Estimate the total plant capital cost using the **Lang Factor** method. Also calculate the revised cost if the plant is **solid-processing** instead. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

| Diagram | Purpose | Key Features | Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BFD** | Shows overall process chemistry | Rectangular blocks for unit ops, single connecting lines, no stream data | Conceptual |
| **PFD** | Shows material & energy flow | All major equipment symbols (columns, vessels, pumps, exchangers), full stream tables (T, P, flow, composition), main control loops shown | Preliminary |
| **P&ID** | Shows how to build and operate | Every pipe, valve, instrument, fitting, line number, insulation, tracing; no process stream data | Detailed/FEED |

(5 marks — 2 for correct features, 1 for ordering, 2 for phase tagging)

**Sketch (2 marks):** A neat, hand-drawn or schematic representation of any one example (e.g., distillation column on PFD with reboiler/condenser; same column on P&ID with level control loop LIC, reflux pump, and every valve tagged).

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $C_{equipment} = ₹ 50 \, \text{Crore}$, fluid-processing plant.

For a **fluid-processing plant**, Lang Factor $f_L = 4.74$.

$$C_{plant, fluid} = f_L \times C_{equipment}$$

$$C_{plant, fluid} = 4.74 \times 50 = \mathbf{₹ \, 237 \, Crore}$$

For a **solid-processing plant**, Lang Factor $f_L = 3.10$.

$$C_{plant, solid} = 3.10 \times 50 = \mathbf{₹ \, 155 \, Crore}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
The fluid plant costs **~53% more** than the solid plant of the same equipment cost. This is because fluid processes require a much higher density of piping, instrumentation, and structural steel per rupee of equipment.

(Marks: Lang factor selection 1 mark × 2, equation 1 mark × 2, final answer 1 mark × 2, interpretation 1 mark.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse PFD with P&ID** — the single most common mistake. PFD has *stream data*; P&ID has *pipe sizes and instrument tags*. Writing P&ID in place of PFD costs 2 full marks.
> 2. **Do NOT use $n = 0.6$ blindly** — the exponent for *individual equipment* (compressors, vessels) is often **0.8**, while only *complete plants* use 0.6. KTU examiners award a separate mark for selecting the correct exponent.
> 3. **Lang Factor values must be memorized** — $f_L = 4.74$ (fluid), **3.10** (solid), **3.63** (mixed). Forgetting them loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Cost-estimate Class accuracy numbers are mandatory** — Class 4 (±50%), Class 3 (±30%), Class 2 (±15%), Class 1 (±5%). Writing "approximately" or leaving blank loses the mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Process Engineering):**

- ✅ Process Engineering = translation of chemistry into a buildable, operable plant.
- ✅ Three phases: **CPD → PPD → DPD (FEED)** — strictly sequential, gated.
- ✅ Engineering effort split: **10 : 30 : 60** (CPD : PPD : DPD).
- ✅ CAPEX fixation by end of each phase: **30% / 60% / 80–90%**.
- ✅ Cost estimate classes (AACE): **4 (CPD) / 3 (PPD) / 2 (DPD-FEED) / 1 (EPC)**.
- ✅ Six-Tenths Rule: $C_B = C_A (S_B/S_A)^n$, $n = 0.6$ (plant) or **0.8** (equipment).
- ✅ Lang Factors: **4.74 (fluid) / 3.10 (solid) / 3.63 (mixed)**.
- ✅ BFD = Conceptual; PFD = Preliminary; P&ID = Detailed/FEED.
- ✅ HAZOP 1 is on PFD; HAZOP 2 is on P&ID (mandatory for KTU real-world projects).
- ✅ Relief sizing, SIL classification, and Cause/Effect matrices are **FEED-only** deliverables.
- ✅ **Rule of Ten:** Cost of fixing an error rises 10× at each successive phase.
- ✅ FEED is the *contractual* handover document to the EPC contractor and the basis of the **Final Investment Decision (FID)**.

> **End of Module 1 — Process Engineering Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Diagrams, Plot Plans, and Engineering Design and Drafting (Detailed Engineering)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Diagrams, Plot Plans, and Engineering Design and Drafting (Detailed Engineering)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Flow Diagrams in Project Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Diagrams** are the graphical language of chemical/process engineering projects. They serve as the primary communication tool between designers, operators, contractors, and regulatory bodies throughout the entire project lifecycle — from conceptualization to commissioning.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme Project Engineering (PECHT524)**, flow diagrams are classified as the backbone of **Detailed Engineering**, which is the third major phase in a chemical plant's project execution (after Conceptual/Feasibility and Front-End Engineering Design — FEED).

### 1.1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

**1. Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**
A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is a schematic representation of the overall process that shows the major equipment, process streams, mass and energy balances, operating conditions, and principal control loops. It is essentially a "process engineer's drawing" — emphasizing *what happens* and *in what quantities*, without piping details.

**2. Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**
A **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed schematic that shows *every* pipe, valve, instrument, control loop, fitting, and piece of equipment in the process, complete with line numbers, sizes, materials, and specifications. It is the *spine* of detailed engineering and is used for construction, operations, and safety studies (HAZOP).

**3. Plot Plan**
A **Plot Plan** is a scaled, two-dimensional layout drawing that shows the geographical arrangement of all major process equipment, storage tanks, buildings, roads, pipe racks, and utility lines *within* the plant boundary, viewed from above.

**4. Detailed Engineering**
**Detailed Engineering** is the phase of project execution where every engineering discipline (process, mechanical, piping, civil, electrical, instrumentation, safety) prepares construction-ready deliverables, including P&IDs, plot plans, equipment datasheets, isometrics, and procurement specifications.

### 1.1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "House Construction" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of designing a chemical plant like designing and building a house:**
> - The **PFD** is the *architect's concept sketch* — it shows the rooms, the flow of people, doors, and key dimensions, but not the wiring or plumbing.
> - The **P&ID** is the *complete electrical and plumbing blueprint* — every wire, every switch, every pipe, every valve, fully detailed.
> - The **Plot Plan** is the *site map* of the house and garden — showing where the house sits on the plot, where the driveway is, where the septic tank goes, where trees are.
> - **Detailed Engineering** is the *foreman's working drawings* — everything the carpenter, plumber, and electrician need to actually build the house.

### 1.1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions (KTU 2024)

The following **industry-standard codes** govern flow diagram preparation:

| Standard | Issuing Body | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **ISA-5.1** | International Society of Automation | Instrumentation symbols |
| **ISA-5.3** | ISA | Flow diagram symbols |
| **PIP** | Process Industry Practices | Plant layout |
| **ASME Y14.5** | ASME | Drafting standards |
| **API RP 505** | American Petroleum Institute | Hazardous area classification |

Key visual conventions:
- **Process streams** are shown as **solid black thick lines** with arrows for flow direction.
- **Utility streams** (steam, water, air) are shown as **dashed lines** or different colors (red = steam, blue = water, green = air, brown = oil).
- **Equipment** is drawn using **standard symbols** (circles for pumps/vessels, horizontal cylinders for horizontal vessels, vertical cylinders for columns).
- **All dimensions** in SI units: **pressure in kPa or bar**, **temperature in °C**, **flow rate in kg/h or m³/h**, **length in mm or m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy mapping, students must be able to *Create* (Level 6) a PFD and *Analyze* (Level 4) a given P&ID, with full understanding of every symbol.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Flow Diagrams — The Hierarchy

Detailed engineering in a process plant requires multiple levels of flow diagrams, each with increasing detail.

### 2.1.1 Block Flow Diagram (BFD)

- **Purpose:** Earliest conceptual overview, typically for non-technical stakeholders.
- **Content:** Major process sections as simple blocks; major material streams.
- **When used:** Feasibility/Conceptual stage.
- **Information shown:** Material balance, but not energy balance.

### 2.1.2 Process Flow Diagram (PFD)

This is the **central document** of process engineering and is heavily emphasized in **KTU 2024 Module 1**.

**Key contents of a PFD:**
1. **All major equipment** (vessels, columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, reactors, filters, dryers) drawn using standard symbols.
2. **All process streams** (feed, product, recycle, vent) with flow direction arrows.
3. **Stream data table** containing:
   - Stream number
   - Mass flow rate (kg/h)
   - Composition (wt% or mol%)
   - Temperature (°C)
   - Pressure (bar)
   - Enthalpy (kJ/kg)
   - Phase (V/L/S)
4. **Material balance** summary table.
5. **Energy balance** summary (duty of heat exchangers, reboilers, condensers in kW or MW).
6. **Major control loops** (typically only the critical regulatory loops; not every loop).
7. **Operating conditions** for major equipment (T, P, hold-up).
8. **Utility summary** at the bottom.
9. **Title block, revision history, legend**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Examined in KTU):**
> - A **PFD does NOT show** every pipe, every valve, every instrument tag, line numbers, pipe specs, or instrument loop details.
> - A **PFD DOES show** the *process intent* — what the plant does, the major equipment, and the mass/energy balance.
> - The PFD is essentially a *mass and energy balance made visual*.

### 2.1.3 Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)

The P&ID is the *construction-grade* document and the most detailed of all flow diagrams.

**Key contents of a P&ID:**
1. **Every piece of process equipment** with unique tag number (e.g., `V-101`, `E-201`, `P-301A/B`).
2. **Every pipe**, with:
   - Line number (e.g., `6"-P-1001-A2-P`)
   - Size (nominal diameter, inches or mm)
   - Service code (process = blank, utility = specific letter)
   - Material class (e.g., carbon steel, SS-304)
   - Insulation class
3. **Every valve** (gate, globe, ball, check, control, safety/relief).
4. **Every instrument** with ISA tag (e.g., `FT-101`, `FCV-102`, `TT-205`).
5. **All control loops** with signal lines (dashed) and pneumatic/electronic designations.
6. **Safety devices** (PSV, Rupture discs, flare connections).
7. **Interlock and ESD logic** (where shown).
8. **Special notes** (e.g., "all hot taps require work permit," "weld procedure per ASME IX").

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** P&IDs are the *single most important document* for plant safety. They are the basis for **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) studies**, **SIL (Safety Integrity Level) classification**, and **operator training simulators (OTS)**.

### 2.1.4 Plot Plan — The Spatial Story

The plot plan is a *scaled plan view* of the entire plant, showing:

- **Process units** (e.g., reactor block, distillation block, utility block)
- **Storage tank farm** (raw material, intermediate, product)
- **Loading/unloading gantries** (for trucks, railcars, marine)
- **Pipe racks** (the "spine" connecting all units)
- **Roads** (for access, fire trucks, maintenance vehicles)
- **Buildings** (control room, substation, warehouse, admin)
- **Sewers and drains** (process sewer, oily water sewer, storm water)
- **Fire water network** and hydrants
- **Flare stack** location
- **Perimeter fencing and access gates**

**Design codes governing plot plans:**
- **OISD-118** (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) for oil & gas
- **NFPA-30** for flammable liquid storage
- **API RP 752** for building spacing
- **TAC-India** for Indian installations

### 2.2 The Detailed Engineering Phase — Stage-Gate Logic

Detailed Engineering is typically executed in **four sequential sub-phases**:

| Sub-Phase | Key Activities | Duration (% of project) |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Engineering** | Develop PFDs into P&IDs, equipment datasheets, line lists | ~15-20% |
| **Mechanical/Static** | Vessel, heat exchanger, pump, compressor specs | ~15% |
| **Piping Engineering** | Isometrics, support design, stress analysis | ~20-25% |
| **Instrumentation & Electrical** | I/O lists, loop diagrams, MCC, cable schedules | ~10-15% |
| **Civil/Structural** | Foundation design, building design, roads | ~10-15% |
| **3D Modeling & Review** | Plant 3D model (PDMS/E3D), 30/60/90% model reviews | Continuous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 30/60/90% Model Review Rule (High-Yield KTU Concept):**
> - **30% Review** — After major equipment layout, no piping details yet. Focus: plot plan, equipment placement.
> - **60% Review** — After most piping is modeled, equipment datasheets 80% complete. Focus: operability, maintenance access, safety.
> - **90% Review** — Construction-ready model. Final HAZOP closeout. Issued for construction (IFC).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Engineering Relations

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for this module. All formulas appear in numerical problems in **KTU past papers**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 2 | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{M}$ | Molar flow rate | kmol/s |
| 3 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat duty of heat exchanger | kW |
| 4 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$ | Heat transfer rate (LMTD method) | kW |
| 5 | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | °C |
| 6 | $A = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{C_D \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}$ | Orifice/control valve sizing | m² |
| 7 | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{suction}}{\rho g} - \dfrac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - h_{friction}$ | Net positive suction head available | m |
| 8 | $\Delta P_{pipe} = \dfrac{4f \cdot L \cdot \rho v^2}{2D}$ | Frictional pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach) | Pa |
| 9 | $v_{optimum} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \cdot A_{pipe}}$ | Optimum pipe velocity | m/s |
| 10 | $W_{pump} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot \eta_{pump}}$ | Pump power requirement | kW |
| 11 | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot t_{residence}}{\rho \cdot \phi}$ | Storage tank volume | m³ |
| 12 | $P_{design} = P_{operating} \times SF$ | Design pressure (with safety factor) | bar |
| 13 | $t_{design} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{SE - 0.6P}$ | Cylindrical shell thickness (ASME VIII) | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Typographic Rule for KTU 2024:**
> - In any markdown table, the **vertical pipe** `|` symbol must NEVER be used inside a cell (e.g., for absolute value). Use **`\vert`** or **`\mid`** instead. For instance, write `$\vert x \vert$` rather than `$\vert x \vert$`.

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these diagrams so important in the field?**
> - **Regulatory Compliance:** Statutory bodies (PESO, OISD, MoEFCC) require P&IDs for plant approval.
> - **Construction:** Pipefitters, welders, and instrument technicians read P&IDs daily.
> - **Operations:** DCS graphics, alarm rationalization, and operator round checks are derived from P&IDs.
> - **Safety:** Emergency response plans, fire water hydraulics, and evacuation maps all originate from plot plans.
> - **Procurement:** Equipment and bulk material take-offs (MTO) are extracted from P&IDs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Heat Exchanger Sizing from PFD Data (14-Mark Type)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This problem integrates flow diagram data with mechanical design — a typical **14-mark KTU Module 1 question**.

### Problem Statement
A PFD shows a feed stream to a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with the following data:
- Cold stream inlet: $T_{c,in} = 30$ °C, $T_{c,out} = 90$ °C, $\dot{m}_c = 20{,}000$ kg/h, $C_{p,c} = 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Hot stream inlet: $T_{h,in} = 200$ °C, $T_{h,out} = 120$ °C, $\dot{m}_h = 8{,}000$ kg/h, $C_{p,h} = 2.2$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 800$ W/(m²·K)

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the heat duty in **kW**.
(b) Calculate the LMTD.
(c) Calculate the heat transfer area in **m²**.
(d) Specify the equipment tag for the heat exchanger using the standard KKS/numerical system.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a): Heat Duty Calculation

First, verify energy balance across the heat exchanger.

**Hot stream heat released:**

$$\dot{Q}_h = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\dot{Q}_h = \frac{8000}{3600} \cdot 2.2 \cdot (200 - 120)$$

$$\dot{Q}_h = 2.2222 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 80$$

$$\dot{Q}_h = 391.11 \text{ kW}$$

**Cold stream heat absorbed:**

$$\dot{Q}_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

$$\dot{Q}_c = \frac{20000}{3600} \cdot 3.5 \cdot (90 - 30)$$

$$\dot{Q}_c = 5.5556 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 60$$

$$\dot{Q}_c = 1166.67 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy balance check (10% tolerance for exam):** The two values should be equal. In real design, this discrepancy is due to rounding in stream data; the designer's responsibility is to reconcile. We will use the **hot stream value** $\dot{Q} = 391.11$ kW as conservative.

**Reconciled heat duty:**

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} = 391.11 \text{ kW}}$$

#### Part (b): LMTD Calculation

For a shell-and-tube exchanger with no phase change:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 200 - 90 = 110 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 120 - 30 = 90 \text{ °C}$$

LMTD formula:

$$LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{110 - 90}{\ln(110/90)}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{20}{\ln(1.2222)}$$

$$LMTD = \frac{20}{0.20067}$$

$$\boxed{LMTD = 99.67 \text{ °C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-flow check:** If we used pure counter-current, $\Delta T_1 = 170$ and $\Delta T_2 = 90$, giving $LMTD = 125.5$ °C. In a real shell-and-tube, correction factor $F_T$ is applied. For this exam, $F_T \approx 1$ is assumed.

#### Part (c): Heat Transfer Area

From $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$:

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot LMTD}$$

Converting units:
- $\dot{Q} = 391.11 \text{ kW} = 391{,}110 \text{ W}$
- $U = 800 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$
- $LMTD = 99.67 \text{ K}$

$$A = \frac{391110}{800 \cdot 99.67}$$

$$A = \frac{391110}{79736}$$

$$\boxed{A = 4.905 \text{ m}^2}$$

#### Part (d): Equipment Tag Numbering (KTU/KKS Convention)

Standard numerical tagging follows:

$$\text{E} - \underbrace{2}_{\text{Plant Area}} \underbrace{01}_{\text{Unit No.}} \underbrace{A}_{\text{Suffix}}$$

For our heat exchanger in Plant Area 2, Unit 01, the tag is:

$$\boxed{\text{E-201A}}$$

Where:
- **E** = Heat Exchanger
- **2** = Plant area code (e.g., 1 = Raw material, 2 = Reaction, 3 = Separation, 4 = Utilities)
- **01** = Unit number within area
- **A** = Suffix for spares/parallel units

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pipe Sizing from P&ID (7-Mark Type)

### Problem Statement
A P&ID shows a process line carrying liquid acetone:
- Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 15{,}000$ kg/h
- Density $\rho = 791$ kg/m³
- Viscosity $\mu = 0.32$ cP = $0.32 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- Recommended velocity for liquids in process lines: $v = 1$ to $2$ m/s

**Required:** Select the optimum nominal pipe size (in inches) using Schedule 40 steel pipe data.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate volumetric flow rate:**

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{15000/3600}{791} = \frac{4.1667}{791}$$

$$\dot{V} = 5.268 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Assume trial pipe size (3-inch, Sch 40)**

Inner diameter of 3-inch Sch 40 pipe = **77.93 mm = 0.07793 m**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.07793)^2 = 4.769 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Velocity:

$$v = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{5.268 \times 10^{-3}}{4.769 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.105 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity check:**

Since $v = 1.105$ m/s lies within the recommended 1 to 2 m/s range:

$$\boxed{\text{Select 3-inch (DN 80) Schedule 40 steel pipe}}$$

**Step 4 — Line numbering per KTU/ISA convention:**

For a 3-inch process line in Plant Area 1, Unit 02:

$$\text{Line Tag} = 3"-\text{P}-1021-\text{A1-CS}$$

Where:
- **3"** = Nominal diameter
- **P** = Process service
- **1021** = Line sequence number (Area 1, Unit 02, line 1)
- **A1** = Material class (Carbon Steel, low temp)
- **CS** = Corrosion allowance (carbon steel standard)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table of Diagram Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU 2-mark and short-answer questions.

| Feature | BFD | PFD | P&ID | Plot Plan |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Project Phase** | Concept/Feasibility | Basic/Detail Eng. | Detailed Eng. | All phases |
| **Equipment Shown** | Major blocks | All major + many minor | All equipment | All major |
| **Piping Detail** | None | Major only | Every line + valve | Pipe racks only |
| **Instrumentation** | None | Critical loops only | Every instrument | None |
| **Stream Data** | Material only | Material + Energy + Op. Cond. | Service + line class | None |
| **Scale** | Not to scale | Not to scale | Not to scale | **To scale** |
| **Number per project** | 1-2 | 5-20 | 50-500+ | 2-5 |
| **Reader** | Management | Engineers | Construction + Operations | Layout team |
| **Used for HAZOP?** | No | Preliminary | **Yes (primary)** | No |
| **Drawing Time** | Days | Weeks | Months | Weeks-Months |
| **Revision Frequency** | Low | Medium | **Very High** | Low |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure — Creating a PFD (KTU Practicum)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 OBE framework**, students should be able to *Create* a PFD. The systematic procedure is:

**Step 1 — Gather process information** from the BFD, mass balance, and energy balance.

**Step 2 — Identify all major process equipment** (reactors, columns, heat exchangers, pumps).

**Step 3 — Determine flow connections** between equipment based on the process scheme.

**Step 4 — Assign stream numbers** (sequentially 1, 2, 3... around the process).

**Step 5 — Populate stream data table** with $\dot{m}, T, P, \text{composition}, \text{phase}, \text{enthalpy}$ for every stream.

**Step 6 — Show utility connections** (steam, cooling water, refrigerant) to each heat exchanger.

**Step 7 — Identify and add major control loops** (typically FC for flow, LC for level, PC for pressure, TC for temperature on critical equipment).

**Step 8 — Add title block, legend, revision history, and key notes.**

**Step 9 — Perform internal design review** (cold eyes review).

**Step 10 — Issue for client approval** (Issued for Approval, IFA).

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Standard PFD Symbol Library (KTU Frequently Tested)

The following schematic shows the most frequently tested PFD symbols in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Recognition Tip (KTU Exam):** Always remember that in a PFD, equipment is drawn using simplified symbols, and the **equipment tag** is shown *inside or adjacent* to the symbol. The tag is the key identifier for cross-referencing between the PFD, equipment list, datasheets, and P&ID.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Plot Plan Design Logic (Step-by-Step)

The plot plan is not a free-hand drawing; it follows a rigorous decision sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with PFD] --> S2[Identify all major equipment and their sizes]
    S2 --> S3[Group equipment into process units]
    S3 --> S4[Apply minimum spacing rules NFPA API]
    S4 --> S5[Locate hazardous equipment far from control room]
    S5 --> S6[Place storage tanks on windward side]
    S6 --> S7[Designate pipe rack as main spine]
    S7 --> S8[Locate flare at safe distance per API 521]
    S8 --> S9[Position control room centrally with safe blast distance]
    S9 --> S10[Add access roads and fire water network]
    S10 --> S11[Verify emergency egress paths]
    S11 --> S12[Issue preliminary plot plan for review]
    S12 --> S13[Incorporate review comments]
    S13 --> S14[Approved Plot Plan for Detailed Engineering]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Plot Plan Spacing Rules (for KTU 14-mark questions):**
> - Minimum distance between two process vessels = **1.5 × diameter of larger vessel** or **2 m**, whichever is greater.
> - Minimum distance from a process unit to the plant boundary = **30 m** (per OISD).
> - Minimum distance from hydrocarbon storage to control room = **30 m** to **60 m** (per OISD-118).
> - Minimum distance from flare tip to occupied buildings = per API 521 radiation contours (typically **60-150 m**).
