# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any six key contents of a Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and explain why the PFD is considered the "central process document" of detailed engineering.*

#### Model Answer (6 points, 3 marks)

The six key contents of a PFD are:
1. All major process equipment drawn using standard symbols.
2. All process streams with arrows indicating flow direction.
3. Stream data table with mass flow rate, temperature, pressure, composition, and enthalpy.
4. Material and energy balance summary.
5. Major control loops (flow, level, pressure, temperature on critical equipment).
6. Utility summary and title block with revision history.

The PFD is considered the central document because **all other engineering deliverables** — equipment datasheets, P&IDs, line lists, instrument indexes, plot plans, and even the 3D model — are derived from it. It is the first integrated document that translates the process design intent into a visual engineering form, and serves as the contract between the process licensor, the engineering contractor, and the client.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare a PFD and a P&ID with respect to (i) piping detail, (ii) instrumentation detail, and (iii) primary user (engineer, constructor, operator).*

#### Model Answer (3 × 1 mark = 3 marks)

| Aspect | PFD | P&ID |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Piping Detail** | Only major process streams are shown; pipe sizes and line numbers are not included. | Every pipe, every valve, every fitting, with line number, size, service, and material class is shown. |
| **(ii) Instrumentation Detail** | Only major control loops (typically <10% of total loops) are shown for critical equipment. | Every instrument with ISA tag, every control loop with signal lines, every interlock, every relief device is shown. |
| **(iii) Primary User** | Process engineers, project managers, clients, and licensing authorities. | Piping designers, construction crews, instrument technicians, operations team, and HAZOP study teams. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, Part B Module 1 offers internal choice. The student must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE 1]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 marks]**
A PFD for an ammonia synthesis plant shows the following data:

- **Reactor R-101:** Inlet stream at 400 °C, 250 bar, $\dot{m} = 50{,}000$ kg/h; Outlet at 500 °C, 250 bar.
- **Feed-effluent heat exchanger E-101:** Cools reactor outlet from 500 °C to 320 °C using reactor inlet (400 °C to 220 °C).
- **Waste heat boiler E-102:** Cools reactor outlet further from 320 °C to 180 °C, generating 100 bar saturated steam. Boiler feed water enters at 220 °C.
- **Condenser E-103:** Cools stream from 180 °C to 40 °C, with cooling water from 25 °C to 40 °C.

Given: $C_p$ of process gas = 2.5 kJ/(kg·K); $C_p$ of cooling water = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K); latent heat of steam at 100 bar = 1319 kJ/kg; $U$ for waste heat boiler = 1500 W/(m²·K); LMTD of WHB = 80 °C.

**(a)** [7 marks] Calculate:
- (i) The heat duty of the feed-effluent heat exchanger E-101 in **kW**.
- (ii) The mass flow rate of cooling water required in condenser E-103 in **kg/h**.
- (iii) The mass flow rate of steam generated in waste heat boiler E-102 in **kg/h**.

**(b)** [7 marks] Calculate:
- (i) The heat transfer area of the waste heat boiler E-102 in **m²**.
- (ii) The equipment tags for all three exchangers using standard KKS convention (Assume Plant Area 1).
- (iii) Sketch (describe in words and draw a simple block diagram) the PFD of this section showing all three exchangers, the reactor, and all streams with direction arrows.

---

#### Model Answer A

##### Part (a) — Calculations [7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3]

**(i) Heat duty of E-101 (Feed-Effluent Exchanger)**

$$\dot{Q}_{E-101} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_{p,gas} \cdot \Delta T_{cold side}$$

The cold side is the reactor inlet, which gets heated from 220 °C to 400 °C:

$$\dot{Q}_{E-101} = \frac{50000}{3600} \cdot 2.5 \cdot (400 - 220)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{E-101} = 13.8889 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 180$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{E-101} = 6250 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Cooling water flow rate in E-103**

Heat to be removed from process gas:

$$\dot{Q}_{E-103} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_{p,gas} \cdot (180 - 40)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{E-103} = 13.8889 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 140 = 4861.11 \text{ kW}$$

Cooling water duty:

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{E-103}}{C_{p,cw} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}} = \frac{4861.11}{4.18 \cdot (40 - 25)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \frac{4861.11}{62.7} = 77.53 \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to kg/h:

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{cw} = 77.53 \cdot 3600 = 279{,}114 \text{ kg/h} \approx 279 \text{ tonnes/h}}$$

**(iii) Steam generation rate in E-102 (Waste Heat Boiler)**

Heat absorbed by WHB:

$$\dot{Q}_{E-102} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot C_{p,gas} \cdot (320 - 180) = 13.8889 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 140 = 4861.11 \text{ kW}$$

Note: This is the same as the condenser because the stream cools from 180 °C to 40 °C; the WHB does the bulk of cooling from 320 to 180 °C, then condenser from 180 to 40 °C. Energy balance verification:

**Energy balance check (showing the conservation of energy across all three exchangers):**

- E-101: 6250 kW absorbed by cold stream
- E-102: 4861.11 kW absorbed by WHB
- E-103: 4861.11 kW absorbed by cooling water

**Total heat removed from hot stream** = 4861.11 + 4861.11 = 9722.22 kW

**Heat absorbed by cold stream (reactor inlet)** = 6250 kW

**Net heat released by reaction** = 9722.22 − 6250 = 3472.22 kW (this is the heat of reaction distributed, plus sensible heat rise in reactor from 400 to 500 °C = 13.8889 × 2.5 × 100 = 3472.22 kW ✓ Balanced!)

Mass flow rate of steam:

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{E-102}}{\lambda_{steam}} = \frac{4861.11 \text{ kJ/s}}{1319 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{steam} = 3.686 \text{ kg/s} = 13{,}267 \text{ kg/h}}$$

##### Part (b) — Design and Sketching [7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3]

**(i) Heat transfer area of E-102**

Using $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot LMTD$:

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot LMTD} = \frac{4861.11 \cdot 1000}{1500 \cdot 80}$$

(Converting $\dot{Q}$ from kW to W: 4861.11 × 1000)

$$A = \frac{4861110}{120000}$$

$$\boxed{A_{E-102} = 40.51 \text{ m}^2}$$

**(ii) Equipment tags (KKS Convention)**

All three exchangers are in Plant Area 1 (Reactor Section), Unit 01:

- E-101 → **E-101** (Feed-Effluent Exchanger)
- E-102 → **E-102** (Waste Heat Boiler)
- E-103 → **E-103** (Condenser)

With suffix for parallel units: E-101A, E-102A, E-103A.

**(iii) PFD Description**

The PFD for this section consists of:

1. **Reactor R-101** drawn as a vertical cylindrical vessel with a catalyst bed indication.
2. **Hot reactor outlet** (Stream 1) at 500 °C, 250 bar → goes to **E-101** → exits at 320 °C (Stream 2) → goes to **E-102** → exits at 180 °C (Stream 3) → goes to **E-103** → exits at 40 °C (Stream 4).
3. **Cold reactor inlet** (Stream 5) at 220 °C, 250 bar → enters **E-101** → exits at 400 °C (Stream 6) → enters **R-101**.
4. **Utility connections:** Steam out of E-102 (Stream S1, 100 bar saturated), Cooling water in/out of E-103 (Streams CW1, CW2).
5. **A level control** on the ammonia separator downstream (not in this section).
6. **A temperature control** TIC on the reactor outlet to maintain catalyst bed temperature.

(For a real KTU answer, a hand-drawn or CAD block diagram of the above would accompany the description.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[CHOICE 2]**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** [7 marks]
(i) List the **four major types of flow diagrams** used in chemical plant project engineering, in increasing order of detail. For each, state the primary user and one distinguishing feature.
(ii) Describe the **ISA convention** for the following instrument tags: FT, FCV, TT, PSV, LSL. State the meaning of each letter and the function of each instrument.

**(b)** [7 marks]
(i) A plot plan must satisfy several minimum spacing rules. State **any four** such rules from OISD-118 or API standards, with numerical values where applicable.
(ii) With the aid of a labeled sketch (describe in words), explain the typical **zoning of a process plant plot plan** into: (1) process area, (2) tank farm, (3) utility area, (4) control room, (5) flare, (6) loading/unloading, and (7) admin building. State the **dominant wind direction** consideration in laying out the tank farm relative to the control room.

---

#### Model Answer B

##### Part (a) — Diagram Types & ISA Tags [7 marks: 3 + 4]

**(i) Four Major Flow Diagram Types (in increasing detail)** [3 marks]

| # | Diagram | Primary User | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **BFD (Block Flow Diagram)** | Project managers, clients, financiers | Shows major process sections as simple blocks; only material balance. |
| 2 | **PFD (Process Flow Diagram)** | Process engineers, licensing authorities | Shows all major equipment, all process streams with mass/energy balance, and major control loops. |
| 3 | **P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)** | Construction crews, HAZOP teams, operations | Shows every pipe, every valve, every instrument; line numbers, sizes, materials. |
| 4 | **Plot Plan / Layout Drawing** | Layout engineers, civil/structural designers | To-scale plan view showing physical placement of all units within the plant boundary. |

**(ii) ISA Instrument Tag Convention** [4 marks]

The ISA-5.1 standard defines instrument tags as a **letter code** (functional) followed by a **loop number**.

| Tag | Letter Breakdown | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **FT** | **F** = Flow, **T** = Transmitter | Flow Transmitter — senses flow rate and transmits a signal (typically 4-20 mA or digital) to the control system. |
| **FCV** | **F** = Flow, **C** = Controller, **V** = Valve | Flow Control Valve — a control valve that receives a signal from the flow controller to modulate flow rate. |
| **TT** | **T** = Temperature, **T** = Transmitter | Temperature Transmitter — senses process temperature and transmits to the control system. |
| **PSV** | **P** = Pressure, **S** = Safety, **V** = Valve | Pressure Safety Valve — a spring-loaded relief valve that automatically opens when upstream pressure exceeds the set pressure, protecting equipment from overpressure. |
| **LSL** | **L** = Level, **S** = Switch, **L** = Low | Level Switch Low — an alarm/interlock switch that activates when liquid level falls below a set low point (typically used for low-level trip to shut down pumps and prevent cavitation). |

> [!NOTE]
> **First letter** = Measured variable (F, T, P, L, A, etc.). **Second letter** = Function (T = Transmitter, C = Controller, I = Indicator, V = Valve, S = Switch, R = Recorder, A = Alarm). **Third letter (if any)** = Modifier or additional function (H = High, L = Low).

##### Part (b) — Plot Plan Rules & Zoning [7 marks: 3 + 4]

**(i) Four Plot Plan Spacing Rules** [3 marks]

1. **Vessel-to-vessel spacing:** Minimum **1.5 × diameter of the larger vessel** or **2 m**, whichever is greater (per OISD-118). This ensures maintenance access, fire-fighting clearance, and heat radiation safety.

2. **Process unit to plant boundary:** Minimum **30 m** for non-hydrocarbon plants; **60 m** for hydrocarbon processing units handling flammable materials above their flash point (per OISD-118).

3. **Flare tip to occupied building distance:** Determined by radiation contours per **API-521**; typically **60 m to 150 m** depending on the heat radiation intensity limit (typically 6.31 kW/m² for personnel exposure).

4. **Storage tank to control room:** Minimum **30 m** for non-flammable liquids; **60 m** for crude/flammable liquid storage tanks of capacity >5000 m³ (per OISD-118). For very large atmospheric storage tanks (>20,000 m³), separation can exceed **90 m**.

**(ii) Plant Zoning in a Plot Plan** [4 marks]

A typical chemical plant plot plan is divided into the following zones:

1. **Process Area (centre of the plant):** Houses reactors, columns, heat exchangers, pumps, and compressors. Connected to all other areas via the central pipe rack.

2. **Tank Farm (one side, typically downwind):** Raw material, intermediate, and product storage tanks. Located on the **downwind side** of the prevailing wind direction so that in case of a leak, vapours are blown *away* from the process area and control room.

3. **Utility Area (peripheral, near process area):** Boilers, cooling towers, compressors, instrument air plants, and power sub-stations. Often placed close to the process area to minimize utility piping length.

4. **Control Room (centrally located, upwind):** The plant's "brain." Located **upwind** of the tank farm and process area, at a safe blast distance, with an unobstructed view of the process area. Designed with positive pressure ventilation and blast-resistant construction per **API RP 752**.

5. **Flare (peripheral, downwind corner):** A tall stack for emergency relief and vent disposal. Located in the **downwind corner** of the plot at maximum distance from ignition sources, occupied buildings, and the plant boundary, satisfying **API-521** thermal radiation limits.

6. **Loading/Unloading Gantry (peripheral, road/rail access):** Truck and railcar loading/unloading stations. Placed near the tank farm but separated by at least **15-30 m** to allow fire-fighting access and emergency egress.

7. **Admin Building (outside the hazardous area, near the main gate):** Offices, canteen, security, first-aid. Located **upwind**, at the plant periphery, with a minimum **100 m** separation from any hydrocarbon handling equipment.

**Wind direction rule (KTU critical concept):** The tank farm and flare must be located **downwind** of the control room and admin building. The control room must be **upwind** of the tank farm. This ensures that any vapor leak from storage is blown away from personnel areas, and any heat from the flare is also directed away from occupied buildings.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Confusing PFD with P&ID** — Many students describe the P&ID contents when asked about the PFD. Always cross-check: the PFD has stream data; the P&ID has line numbers. **Penalty: Up to 3 marks lost per question.**
> 2. **Forgetting to include units** in numerical answers. KTU strictly enforces units; missing units may cost **1 mark** per sub-part.
> 3. **Not drawing or describing a sketch** in plot plan questions. KTU requires a labeled diagram (or detailed description) for full marks on layout questions. **Penalty: Up to 4 marks lost if no diagram.**
> 4. **Mixing up P&ID with PFD when listing control loops** — The PFD shows *only major* control loops (typically <10), while the P&ID shows *every* loop.
> 5. **Using inconsistent tag numbering** — Always use the **KKS (Power Plant)** or numerical convention consistently. E.g., E-101, V-201, P-301. Mixing tag styles loses marks.
> 6. **Missing the energy balance verification step** in heat exchanger design — KTU often gives partial credit for verifying $\dot{Q}_{hot} \approx \dot{Q}_{cold}$ within ±10% tolerance.
> 7. **Confusing NPSHA with NPSHR** — NPSH_A is *available* (calculated from system), NPSH_R is *required* (from pump vendor). Pump operation requires NPSH_A > NPSH_R + 0.5 m safety margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU Module 1 examination:**

### Core Concepts (Must Remember)
- [ ] **BFD → PFD → P&ID** is the *correct sequence of flow diagram development* (increasing detail).
- [ ] **PFD** is the *central process document* — it shows mass/energy balance, major equipment, and major control loops, but **NO** pipe sizes, line numbers, or instrument tags.
- [ ] **P&ID** shows **every** pipe, valve, instrument, and interlock; it is the construction document and HAZOP basis.
- [ ] **Plot plan** is the only diagram drawn **to scale**; governed by OISD, API, and NFPA codes.
- [ ] **Detailed Engineering** is the 3rd phase of project execution, after FEED and before construction.

### ISA Tag Convention (Frequently Tested)
- [ ] **First letter** = measured variable (F, T, P, L, A, H, etc.).
- [ ] **Succeeding letters** = function (T=Transmitter, C=Controller, V=Valve, I=Indicator, S=Switch, R=Recorder, A=Alarm).
- [ ] **Suffix** (H/L) = High/Low set point for switches and alarms.
- [ ] **PSV** (Pressure Safety Valve) is the most critical safety device; sized per API 520/521.

### Equipment Tag Numbering (KKS)
- [ ] Format: **[Type][Area][Unit][Suffix]** — e.g., **E-201A** (Heat Exchanger, Area 2, Unit 01, Unit A).
- [ ] Standard prefixes: **V** (Vessel), **T** (Tower), **E** (Exchanger), **P** (Pump), **C** (Compressor), **R** (Reactor), **ST** (Storage Tank), **F** (Filter), **D** (Drum).

### Heat Exchanger Sizing (Key Formula Chain)
- [ ] $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ → Heat duty.
- [ ] $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ → LMTD.
- [ ] $A = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot LMTD}$ → Area (use $F_T$ correction in real design).
- [ ] **Always verify energy balance** between hot and cold sides.

### Pipe Sizing
- [ ] Recommended velocities: **liquids 1-2 m/s, gases 10-20 m/s, steam 20-30 m/s**.
- [ ] Use **Schedule 40 carbon steel** as default; SS-304/316 for corrosive service.
- [ ] Line numbering: **[Size]-[Service]-[Area+Unit+Seq]-[MatClass]-[CorrAllow]** (e.g., 3"-P-1021-A1-CS).

### Plot Plan Critical Rules
- [ ] **Tank farm and flare = downwind.**
- [ ] **Control room and admin = upwind.**
- [ ] **Flare to control room distance per API 521 radiation contours.**
- [ ] **Tank to control room ≥ 30 m (non-flammable), ≥ 60 m (flammable).**
- [ ] **Flare tip to plant boundary as per API 521 (typically ≥ 60 m).**

### 30/60/90% Model Review Rule
- [ ] **30%** = plot plan + major equipment placement.
- [ ] **60%** = most piping modeled, operability reviewed.
- [ ] **90%** = construction-ready, final HAZOP closeout.

### Critical Industrial Codes
- [ ] **ISA-5.1** = Instrumentation symbols.
- [ ] **ASME VIII** = Pressure vessel design.
- [ ] **ASME B31.3** = Process piping.
- [ ] **API 520/521** = Pressure relief and flare design.
- [ ] **API RP 752** = Building spacing.
- [ ] **OISD-118** = Layout for oil & gas plants.
- [ ] **NFPA-30** = Flammable liquid storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy Tip:** Always read the question carefully. If it says "PFD," do not list P&ID contents. If it says "sketch," provide a labeled diagram (even hand-drawn) — a description alone will lose marks. **Units and energy balance verification are mandatory in numerical problems.**

topics.title: Plant Location and Site Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plant Location and Site Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plant Location** is a **macro-level strategic decision** in project engineering that involves the selection of a **geographical region or area** for establishing an industrial facility. It is a long-term, capital-intensive decision that determines the *country*, *state*, and *region* where the plant will operate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Site Selection** is a **micro-level tactical decision** that follows plant location. It involves choosing the **specific plot of land** within the chosen geographical area, considering local factors such as topography, soil condition, and neighborhood.

### The Two-Tier Hierarchy of Strategic Placement

| Tier | Decision | Scope | Question Answered |
|:----:|:---------|:------|:------------------|
| Tier 1 | **Plant Location** | Macro (Regional/National) | *"Where in the country/state should the plant be built?"* |
| Tier 2 | **Site Selection** | Micro (Local) | *"Which specific plot of land is best suited?"* |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Hunting" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are a family planning to relocate.
> - **Plant Location ≈ Choosing the City:** You first decide between *Kochi, Chennai, or Bengaluru* based on job opportunities (market), cost of living (taxes/incentives), and family preferences (government policies). This is a one-time, hard-to-reverse decision.
> - **Site Selection ≈ Choosing the House in the City:** Once the city is fixed, you pick the *actual house* based on neighborhood, soil type, road access, and proximity to schools. This decision is more flexible and localized.

Just as a wrong city ruins a relocation, a wrong plant location can render a multi-crore project unviable, regardless of how good the engineering design is.

---

## 1.3 Why This Decision is Critical

The plant location decision is considered the **"most irreversible"** decision in project engineering because:

1. **High Capital Lock-in:** Plant and machinery costs (typically **60–80% of total project cost**) cannot be relocated.
2. **Long Operational Horizon:** Industrial plants operate for **15–25 years** minimum.
3. **Cascading Effect:** Wrong location affects logistics, manpower, utilities, and even corporate tax outgo for decades.
4. **Strategic Brand Image:** Location influences customer perception and B2B partnerships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Benchmark:** Studies in industrial engineering indicate that a poorly chosen plant location can increase operating costs by **15% to 30%** over the plant's lifetime, while the *engineering* design optimization typically yields only **5% to 10%** savings.

---

## 1.4 Objectives of Plant Location

A scientifically chosen plant location must satisfy these primary objectives:

- **Minimize Total Cost:** Sum of (Fixed Cost + Variable Cost + Transportation Cost).
- **Maximize Profitability:** Ensure the revenue-cost gap is sustainable.
- **Ensure Operational Efficiency:** Provide uninterrupted access to raw materials, utilities, and labor.
- **Enable Future Expansion:** Allow room for horizontal and vertical capacity augmentation.
- **Fulfill Social Responsibility:** Minimize environmental impact and maximize community welfare.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Factors Affecting Plant Location

The factors are broadly classified into two categories: **Primary Factors** (mandatory/essential) and **Secondary Factors** (decisive/optional).

### 2.1.1 Primary Factors (A-Grade — Must-Have)

These directly determine the *economic feasibility* of the plant:

1. **Availability of Raw Materials**
   - Proximity to raw material source reduces transportation cost.
   - Critical for **weight-losing industries** (e.g., sugar mills, cement, ore processing).
   - Reduces inventory holding cost (Just-In-Time supply possible).

2. **Proximity to Market**
   - Critical for **weight-gaining industries** (e.g., bottling plants, bakery, soft drinks).
   - Reduces finished-goods distribution cost.
   - Improves responsiveness to customer demand.

3. **Transportation Facilities**
   - Availability of road, rail, port, and air connectivity.
   - Transportation cost is often the **second-largest cost component** (after raw materials).
   - Multimodal access (highway + railway + seaport) is ideal.

4. **Availability of Labor**
   - Both *skilled* (engineers, technicians) and *unskilled* (operators, helpers).
   - Labor productivity, wage rates, and union culture matter.
   - Critical for labor-intensive industries (textiles, garments).

5. **Availability of Utilities**
   - **Power** (electricity supply, captive power feasibility).
   - **Water** (process water, cooling water, potable water).
   - **Steam, Compressed Air, Effluent Treatment.**

### 2.1.2 Secondary Factors (B-Grade — Tie-Breakers)

These decide *between* equally viable locations:

1. **Climatic Conditions** — Temperature, humidity, rainfall (affects HVAC design).
2. **Government Policies** — Tax holidays, subsidies, SEZ benefits.
3. **Land Cost & Topography** — Flat land is cheaper to develop.
4. **Banking & Financial Infrastructure** — Proximity to financial hubs.
5. **Community Attitude & Living Conditions** — Housing, schools, hospitals.
6. **Environmental & Legal Constraints** — Pollution norms, forest clearance.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Memory:** *"R-M-T-L-U"* → **R**aw material, **M**arket, **T**ransport, **L**abor, **U**tilities.

---

## 2.2 The Plant Location Decision Process (Five-Stage Model)

1. **Stage 1 — Preliminary Screening:** Identify the region/country using macro factors.
2. **Stage 2 — Community Analysis:** Evaluate the community, region, and state.
3. **Stage 3 — Site-Specific Analysis:** Evaluate alternative sites within the chosen region.
4. **Stage 4 — Financial Cost Analysis:** Detailed cost breakdown and break-even computation.
5. **Stage 5 — Final Selection:** Make the final decision using weighted scoring or comparative matrix.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **most frequently tested numerical content** for Module 1 in KTU ESE. Memorize the formulas and units.

| # | Method | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|:-:|:-------|:--------|:------------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Break-Even Point (BEP)** | $BEP = \dfrac{F}{S - V}$ | $F$ = Fixed Cost (₹/yr), $S$ = Selling Price/unit (₹), $V$ = Variable Cost/unit (₹) | Finding minimum production volume for profit |
| 2 | **Location Break-Even (2 sites)** | $F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q$ | $F_i$ = Fixed Cost at site *i* (₹/yr), $V_i$ = Variable Cost at site *i* (₹/unit), $Q$ = Production volume (units/yr) | Comparing two alternative locations |
| 3 | **Solved Location BEP** | $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$ | Units as above | Production volume at which both sites are equally profitable |
| 4 | **Center of Gravity (x-coordinate)** | $X = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$ | $Q_i$ = Quantity shipped from source *i* (tonnes), $x_i$ = x-coordinate of source *i* (km) | Locating a central distribution warehouse |
| 5 | **Center of Gravity (y-coordinate)** | $Y = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$ | $Q_i$ = Quantity (tonnes), $y_i$ = y-coordinate (km) | Same as above |
| 6 | **Weighted Factor Score** | $S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot R_{ij}$ | $W_i$ = Weight of factor *i* (sum = 1.0 or 100%), $R_{ij}$ = Rating of site *j* on factor *i* (1–10 scale) | Comparing multiple sites on qualitative + quantitative factors |
| 7 | **Composite Score (Normalized)** | $S_j^{norm} = \dfrac{S_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} S_j} \times 100$ | Percentage share | Final ranking of candidate sites |
| 8 | **Transportation Cost (Weber)** | $TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot d_i$ | $C_i$ = Quantity moved (tonnes), $d_i$ = Distance (km) | Total logistics cost for raw material + finished goods |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** In answer sheets, always use *consistent subscript notation*. For example, $F_1$ for fixed cost at Location 1, $F_2$ for fixed cost at Location 2. The examiners penalize inconsistent notation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic artifacts — they are used in:

- **Greenfield Project Setup:** Multi-national firms (Reliance, Tata, Adani) use **Center of Gravity** to locate refineries and mega-warehouses.
- **Site Comparison Studies:** Used in **Detailed Project Reports (DPR)** mandated by banks and government bodies.
- **EPC Tender Evaluation:** Contractors use **Break-Even Analysis** to decide whether to bid for projects in remote vs accessible locations.
- **Supply Chain Design:** **Weighted Factor Scoring** is the cornerstone of **logistics network design** in companies like Amazon and Flipkart.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The question *"Discuss the factors affecting plant location"* appears in nearly every KTU ESE cycle. The model answer should *always* begin with the **primary-secondary classification** to demonstrate structured thinking.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Location Break-Even Analysis (LBA)

The **Location Break-Even Analysis** is the most important quantitative method for KTU. Here is the full step-by-step derivation.

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

The total annual cost at any plant location is the sum of **Fixed Cost** (independent of production volume) and **Variable Cost** (proportional to production volume).

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** We are comparing exactly **two alternative plant locations** (A and B) producing the same product at the same selling price.

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the total cost equation for Location 1 (call it Site A).

$$
TC_1 = F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q
$$

*Explanation:* Total cost at Site 1 = Fixed cost (rent, salary, depreciation) + (Variable cost per unit × Number of units produced).

**Step 2:** Write the total cost equation for Location 2 (call it Site B).

$$
TC_2 = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q
$$

*Explanation:* Same logic, but with site-specific fixed and variable costs.

**Step 3:** At the **break-even volume**, the total costs at both sites are equal — neither site has a cost advantage.

$$
TC_1 = TC_2
$$

*Explanation:* This is the definition of the break-even point — the production volume at which the management is *indifferent* between the two options.

**Step 4:** Substitute the cost equations into the equality.

$$
F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q
$$

*Explanation:* Both sides now represent the total cost as a function of Q.

**Step 5:** Group the terms involving *Q* on one side.

$$
F_1 - F_2 = V_2 \cdot Q - V_1 \cdot Q
$$

*Explanation:* Subtracting $F_2$ and $V_1 \cdot Q$ from both sides keeps the equation balanced.

**Step 6:** Factor out *Q* on the right-hand side.

$$
F_1 - F_2 = Q \cdot (V_2 - V_1)
$$

*Explanation:* This uses the distributive property of multiplication over addition/subtraction.

**Step 7:** Solve for *Q* to obtain the location break-even volume.

$$
Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}
$$

*Explanation:* Dividing both sides by $(V_2 - V_1)$ gives the final formula. The unit is *units per year*.

### 3.1.3 Decision Rule for the LBA Result

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Select Site A} & \text{if } Q_{actual} < Q_{BEP} \\ \text{Select Site B} & \text{if } Q_{actual} > Q_{BEP} \\ \text{Either Site} & \text{if } Q_{actual} = Q_{BEP} \end{cases}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"If production is LOW, choose the site with LOWER fixed cost. If production is HIGH, choose the site with LOWER variable cost."* This is because high fixed cost is amortized only when production volume is large.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Full Solution)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A company is evaluating two plant locations — **Pune** and **Coimbatore** — to manufacture a specialty chemical. The annual fixed cost at Pune is **₹50,00,000** and at Coimbatore is **₹35,00,000**. The variable cost is **₹1,200 per tonne** at Pune and **₹1,500 per tonne** at Coimbatore. Determine the break-even production volume. If the expected annual demand is **12,000 tonnes**, which site should be selected?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the given data.

$$
F_1 = ₹50,00,000 \quad (\text{Pune}), \quad F_2 = ₹35,00,000 \quad (\text{Coimbatore})
$$

$$
V_1 = ₹1,200/\text{tonne} \quad (\text{Pune}), \quad V_2 = ₹1,500/\text{tonne} \quad (\text{Coimbatore})
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the location break-even formula.

$$
Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
Q_{BEP} = \frac{50,00,000 - 35,00,000}{1,500 - 1,200}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator and denominator.

$$
Q_{BEP} = \frac{15,00,000}{300}
$$

**Step 5:** Perform the division.

$$
Q_{BEP} = 5,000 \text{ tonnes per year}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare the actual demand with the break-even volume.

$$
Q_{actual} = 12,000 \text{ tonnes/yr} \quad > \quad Q_{BEP} = 5,000 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 7:** Apply the decision rule.

Since actual production exceeds the break-even volume, **Pune should be selected** because its higher fixed cost is more than offset by its lower variable cost at high production volumes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state the *decision rule* explicitly before writing the conclusion. Examiners award 2 marks for the decision rule and 2 marks for the numerical answer in a 7-mark sub-part.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Center of Gravity Method

The Center of Gravity method is a mathematical technique used to find the **optimal location for a single new facility** (typically a warehouse) that minimizes total weighted transportation distance.

### 3.3.1 Conceptual Foundation

Imagine *n* existing facilities (suppliers, depots, or demand centers) located at known coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ on a 2D map. Each facility ships a known quantity $Q_i$ to the new facility. The new facility's location $(X, Y)$ should minimize the total transportation cost, which is proportional to *distance × quantity*.

### 3.3.2 Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the total weighted distance (proxy for transportation cost) to be minimized.

$$
TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot d_i
$$

*Explanation:* Total cost is the sum over all sources of (quantity × distance).

**Step 2:** Approximate distance using the **Euclidean (straight-line) distance** formula.

$$
d_i = \sqrt{(X - x_i)^2 + (Y - y_i)^2}
$$

*Explanation:* The straight-line distance between the new facility $(X, Y)$ and the existing facility *i* at $(x_i, y_i)$.

**Step 3:** Substitute the distance formula into the cost function.

$$
TC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot \sqrt{(X - x_i)^2 + (Y - y_i)^2}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the objective function to be minimized.

**Step 4:** Differentiate *TC* with respect to *X* and set equal to zero for a minimum.

$$
\frac{\partial TC}{\partial X} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot \frac{(X - x_i)}{\sqrt{(X - x_i)^2 + (Y - y_i)^2}} = 0
$$

*Explanation:* At the optimal point, the first derivative of total cost with respect to X must vanish.

**Step 5:** Apply the classical simplification. If all distances are approximately equal (a standard assumption for short-haul logistics), the denominator becomes a common factor.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot (X - x_i) = 0
$$

*Explanation:* Under the equal-distance assumption, the square-root term becomes a common constant that cancels out.

**Step 6:** Expand and rearrange.

$$
X \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot x_i = 0
$$

*Explanation:* This separates the X-dependent term from the known coordinates and quantities.

**Step 7:** Solve for X.

$$
X = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the classic *Center of Gravity* formula for the x-coordinate.

**Step 8:** By symmetry, apply the same logic for the y-coordinate.

$$
Y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}
$$

*Explanation:* The y-coordinate is computed identically, using the y-positions of the existing facilities.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Meaning:** The Center of Gravity formula is the **quantity-weighted average** of all the source coordinates — equivalent to finding the *center of mass* of a system of point masses.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Methods

> [!NOTE]
> This comparative matrix is a high-yield KTU content piece. Examiners often ask *"Compare the various methods of plant location selection"*.

| Parameter | Break-Even Analysis | Center of Gravity | Weighted Factor Rating |
|:----------|:-------------------|:------------------|:----------------------|
| **Nature** | Quantitative | Quantitative | Qualitative + Quantitative |
| **Number of Sites Compared** | Exactly 2 | Finds 1 new site | 2 or more |
| **Data Required** | Fixed cost, Variable cost | Coordinates, Quantities | Weights, Ratings |
| **Computational Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Subjectivity** | Low (objective) | Low (objective) | High (subjective) |
| **Best Suited For** | Two equally viable regions | Locating warehouses/distribution centers | Multi-factor site selection |
| **Limitation** | Ignores qualitative factors | Assumes equal distances | Weights may be biased |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Plant Location Decision Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Engineering: Strategic Decision] --> B[Identify Project Need]
    B --> C[Macro-Level: PLANT LOCATION]
    B --> D[Micro-Level: SITE SELECTION]
    C --> C1[Country Selection]
    C1 --> C2[State / Region Selection]
    C2 --> C3[City / District Selection]
    D --> D1[Specific Plot of Land]
    D1 --> D2[Soil, Topography, Drainage]
    D1 --> D3[Neighborhood and Connectivity]
    C3 --> E[Apply Evaluation Methods]
    E --> E1[Break-Even Analysis]
    E --> E2[Center of Gravity]
    E --> E3[Weighted Factor Rating]
    E1 --> F[Final Decision: Optimal Plant Location]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    F --> G[Issue Purchase Order and Begin Construction]
```

## 4.2 Factors Affecting Plant Location — Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Factors Affecting Plant Location] --> P[Primary Factors]
    F --> S[Secondary Factors]
    P --> P1[Raw Material Availability]
    P --> P2[Market Proximity]
    P --> P3[Transportation Network]
    P --> P4[Labor Availability]
    P --> P5[Utilities: Power, Water, Steam]
    S --> S1[Climatic Conditions]
    S --> S2[Government Policies and Subsidies]
    S --> S3[Land Cost and Topography]
    S --> S4[Community Attitude]
    S --> S5[Environmental Regulations]
    S --> S6[Banking and Financial Infrastructure]
```

## 4.3 Break-Even Analysis Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin: Two Sites A and B]) --> Step1[Collect Fixed Cost F1, F2]
    Step1 --> Step2[Collect Variable Cost V1, V2]
    Step2 --> Step3[Apply Formula: QBEP = F1 - F2 divided by V2 - V1]
    Step3 --> Step4{Compare QBEP with Qactual}
    Step4 -->|Qactual greater than QBEP| PathA[Select Site with Lower Variable Cost]
    Step4 -->|Qactual less than QBEP| PathB[Select Site with Lower Fixed Cost]
    Step4 -->|Qactual equals QBEP| PathC[Both Sites Equivalent]
    PathA --> End([Decision Finalized])
    PathB --> End
    PathC --> End
```

## 4.4 Weighted Factor Rating Method — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: List All Relevant Factors] --> P2[Step 2: Assign Weight to Each Factor: Sum equals 1.0]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: List Candidate Sites]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Score Each Site on Each Factor: 1 to 10 Scale]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute Weighted Score: Sum of Weight times Rating]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Rank Sites by Total Weighted Score]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Select Top-Ranked Site]
    P7 --> P8([Final Plant Location Selected])
```

## 4.5 Plant Location vs. Site Selection — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MACRO[MACRO: PLANT LOCATION]
        M1[Country]
        M2[State or Region]
        M3[City or Industrial Hub]
    end
    subgraph MICRO[MICRO: SITE SELECTION]
        S1[Plot of Land: Acres]
        S2[Soil Type and Bearing Capacity]
        S3[Road, Rail, Port Connectivity]
        S4[Effluent Disposal Facility]
    end
    MACRO --> MICRO
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why Mermaid and not a hand-drawn diagram?** KTU ESE answer scripts must be *self-explanatory in black-and-white*. Mermaid-style flowcharts can be redrawn manually by the student in the exam using simple boxes, arrows, and labels — which is far more practical than attempting complex multi-color illustrations.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> Differentiate between **plant location** and **plant layout**. Why is plant location considered a strategic decision?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Plant Location | Plant Layout |
|:-------|:---------------|:--------------|
| **Scope** | Macro — geographical area | Micro — arrangement of equipment inside the plant |
| **Time of Decision** | Before project sanction | After plant location is finalized |
| **Reversibility** | Highly irreversible (15–25 years) | Modifiable during operation |

Plant location is a **strategic decision** because it involves huge capital commitment, is difficult to reverse, has long-term operational implications (15–25 years), and shapes the company's competitive position. It affects taxation, logistics, and labor relations for the plant's entire lifetime. *(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for strategic justification)*

---

### Question 2: Factors Classification
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> List the **primary factors** affecting plant location. Explain why **availability of raw materials** is the most critical factor for a cement plant.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Primary Factors (1.5 Marks):**
1. Availability of raw materials
2. Proximity to market
3. Transportation facilities
4. Labor availability
5. Utilities (power, water, steam)

**Cement Plant — Raw Material Criticality (1.5 Marks):**
Cement manufacturing is a **weight-losing industry** — roughly **1.6 tonnes of raw material** (limestone, clay, gypsum) is required to produce **1 tonne of cement**. Therefore:
- Transporting raw material over long distances is uneconomical.
- Cement plants are typically located within **50 km of limestone quarries**.
- Raw material proximity reduces both inbound logistics cost and inbound inventory.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Choice A: Break-Even Analysis (Quantitative)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Question A]**
> **(a)** A chemical company must choose between two plant locations — **Mumbai** and **Vadodara** — for manufacturing industrial solvents. The estimated annual fixed cost at Mumbai is **₹80,00,000** and at Vadodara is **₹55,00,000**. The variable cost per tonne is **₹2,000 at Mumbai** and **₹2,400 at Vadodara**. The expected annual demand is **15,000 tonnes**.
> - **(i)** Determine the break-even production volume. (4 marks)
> - **(ii)** Which site should be selected and why? (3 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of the break-even method for plant location decisions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a)(i): Break-Even Volume Calculation (4 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$$
F_1 = ₹80,00,000 \quad (Mumbai), \quad F_2 = ₹55,00,000 \quad (Vadodara)
$$

$$
V_1 = ₹2,000/\text{tonne} \quad (Mumbai), \quad V_2 = ₹2,400/\text{tonne} \quad (Vadodara)
$$

**Applying the Formula:**

$$
Q_{BEP} = \frac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
Q_{BEP} = \frac{80,00,000 - 55,00,000}{2,400 - 2,000}
$$

**Simplification:**

$$
Q_{BEP} = \frac{25,00,000}{400} = 6,250 \text{ tonnes per year}
$$

#### Part (a)(ii): Site Selection (3 Marks)

**Comparison:**

$$
Q_{actual} = 15,000 \text{ tonnes/yr} \quad > \quad Q_{BEP} = 6,250 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

Since the actual demand significantly exceeds the break-even volume, **Mumbai should be selected** because its higher fixed cost is justified by lower variable cost at high production volumes.

**Decision Rule Recap:**

- For $Q > Q_{BEP}$ → site with lower variable cost is preferred → **Mumbai**
- The variable cost saving per unit (₹400) outweighs the fixed cost penalty (₹25 lakh) once production exceeds 6,250 tonnes.

#### Part (b): Advantages and Limitations of Break-Even Method (7 Marks)

**Advantages (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Simplicity:** The mathematical model is straightforward and easy to apply in KTU-style problems.
2. **Quantitative Foundation:** It provides an objective, numbers-based comparison rather than relying on intuition.
3. **Visual Clarity:** When plotted on a graph (X-axis: Volume, Y-axis: Cost), the intersection point gives immediate visual feedback.
4. **Sensitivity Insight:** Management can quickly see how volume changes affect the optimal location.
5. **Useful for Initial Screening:** Excellent for shortlisting between two equally viable regions.

**Limitations (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Two-Site Restriction:** It compares only two locations; not suitable when more than two sites are being evaluated.
2. **Linear Assumption:** Assumes fixed cost is constant and variable cost is strictly linear with volume — not always true in real plants.
3. **Ignores Qualitative Factors:** It does not consider government subsidies, labor climate, climate, or community attitude.
4. **Constant Selling Price Assumption:** Assumes revenue per unit is the same at both sites, which is rarely true.
5. **No Time Value of Money:** Does not account for inflation, discount rate, or future expansion.
6. **Single-Product Limitation:** Difficult to apply for multi-product plants with shared overheads.

---

### Choice B: Weighted Factor Rating (Qualitative + Quantitative)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Question B]**
> **(a)** A pharmaceutical company is evaluating three plant locations — **Hyderabad, Bengaluru, and Chennai** — using the **Weighted Factor Rating Method**. The relevant factors and their weights are given below. The ratings (out of 10) are tabulated. Compute the weighted score for each site and recommend the best site.
>
> | Factor | Weight | Hyderabad | Bengaluru | Chennai |
> |:-------|:------:|:---------:|:---------:|:-------:|
> | Raw Material Supply | 0.25 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
> | Labor Availability | 0.20 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
> | Power and Water | 0.20 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
> | Market Access | 0.15 | 9 | 8 | 8 |
> | Transportation | 0.10 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
> | Government Policy | 0.10 | 8 | 6 | 7 |
>
> *(Sub-part (a): 7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Center of Gravity method** for plant location. What are its assumptions and limitations? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Weighted Factor Score Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Apply the Formula for Each Site**

The weighted score for site *j* is:

$$
S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i \cdot R_{ij}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Score for Hyderabad**

$$
S_{Hyd} = (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(7) + (0.20)(6) + (0.15)(9) + (0.10)(7) + (0.10)(8)
$$

**Detailed Computation:**

$$
S_{Hyd} = 2.00 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 1.35 + 0.70 + 0.80 = 7.45
$$

**Step 3: Compute Score for Bengaluru**

$$
S_{Ben} = (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.20)(8) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(8) + (0.10)(6)
$$

**Detailed Computation:**

$$
S_{Ben} = 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.60 + 1.20 + 0.80 + 0.60 = 7.75
$$

**Step 4: Compute Score for Chennai**

$$
S_{Che} = (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(8) + (0.20)(7) + (0.15)(8) + (0.10)(9) + (0.10)(7)
$$

**Detailed Computation:**

$$
S_{Che} = 1.50 + 1.60 + 1.40 + 1.20 + 0.90 + 0.70 = 7.30
$$

**Step 5: Final Ranking Table**

| Rank | Site | Weighted Score |
|:----:|:-----|:--------------:|
| **1st** | **Bengaluru** | **7.75** |
| 2nd | Hyderabad | 7.45 |
| 3rd | Chennai | 7.30 |

**Recommendation (1 mark):**

**Bengaluru should be selected** as the optimal plant location because it achieves the highest weighted composite score (7.75 out of 10). It scores particularly well on labor availability (rating 9), which has a high weight (0.20), making it the decisive factor.

---

#### Part (b): Center of Gravity Method (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Center of Gravity method is a quantitative plant location technique used to determine the **optimal coordinates of a single new facility** (typically a warehouse, distribution center, or manufacturing plant) that **minimizes the total weighted transportation distance** to a set of existing facilities (suppliers or customers).

**Mathematical Formulation (2 Marks):**

$$
X = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i} \qquad Y = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}
$$

Where $Q_i$ is the quantity shipped to/from existing facility *i*, and $(x_i, y_i)$ are its coordinates.

**Assumptions (1.5 Marks):**

1. The transportation cost is **directly proportional to distance and quantity**.
2. The **straight-line (Euclidean) distance** is a reasonable approximation of actual travel distance.
3. The map is a **flat 2D plane** (ignores Earth's curvature).
4. All existing facility locations and shipment volumes are **known and constant**.

**Limitations (1.5 Marks):**

1. Ignores **topographical barriers** (rivers, mountains, restricted zones).
2. The result is a **mathematical point**, not always a buildable site (may fall in a river or forest).
3. **Equal transportation rate** is assumed; in reality, rates differ by mode (road vs rail).
4. Cannot handle **multi-product** flows with different unit weights or rates.
5. Static — does not consider **future expansion** or **changing demand patterns**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Not stating the decision rule explicitly** in Break-Even problems (lose 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting to verify that weight sum equals 1.0** in Weighted Factor Rating (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Confusing plant location with plant layout** in Part A answers (lose 2 marks).
> 4. **Skipping the formula derivation** in 7-mark questions — write the formula *and* one line of explanation.
> 5. **Not using subscripts consistently** ($F_1$ vs $F_A$ vs $F_{Mumbai}$) — choose one and stick to it.
> 6. **Writing $100\%$ in percentage format** when the question expects the weighted score out of 10.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Plant Location and Site Selection**

- **Plant Location** = *Macro* decision (geographical region); **Site Selection** = *Micro* decision (specific plot). They are sequential, not interchangeable.
- Plant location is the **most irreversible** strategic decision in project engineering.
- **Primary (Must-Have) Factors:** Raw material, Market, Transport, Labor, Utilities — mnemonic: **"R-M-T-L-U"**.
- **Secondary (Tie-Breaker) Factors:** Climate, Government policy, Land cost, Community, Environment, Banking.
- **Weight-losing industries** (sugar, cement, ore) → locate **near raw material**.
- **Weight-gaining industries** (soft drinks, bakery) → locate **near market**.
- **Break-Even Formula:** $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$ — used for two-site comparison.
- **Decision Rule for LBA:** *Low volume → low fixed cost site; High volume → low variable cost site.*
- **Center of Gravity:** A quantity-weighted average of source coordinates — used for warehouse/distribution center placement.
- **Weighted Factor Rating:** Most flexible method — combines qualitative (ratings) and quantitative (weights) factors.
- **Weber's Industrial Location Theory:** Minimizes transportation cost; considers material index (weight of localized material ÷ weight of finished product).
- **Always check the sum of weights = 1.0** in WFR problems.
- **A bad plant location can increase lifetime operating cost by 15–30%** — a fact worth quoting in theory answers.
- **KTU ESE Focus:** Numerical problems on LBA and WFR appear in 90% of past papers — practice these two methods thoroughly.
- **Site Selection** is influenced by soil bearing capacity, drainage, flood history, and neighborhood (residential vs industrial).
- **Environmental clearance** from State Pollution Control Board is mandatory for chemical plants — often a decisive factor.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Data for Construction of Projects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Data for Construction of Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Preliminary Data for Construction of Projects** refers to the comprehensive set of physical, environmental, socio-economic, infrastructural, and regulatory information systematically collected, analysed, and documented **before** the actual design, estimation, tendering, and execution phases of a civil engineering project commence. It forms the foundational intelligence layer (the *Project Feasibility Intelligence Base*) upon which feasibility studies, preliminary designs, detailed project reports (DPR), Bill of Quantities (BoQ), and construction schedules are built.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, preliminary data collection is the **first deliverable of Module 1 (Project Initiation & Feasibility)** of *Project Engineering (PECHT524)*. It precedes the *Project Feasibility Study* and the *Technical & Financial Appraisal*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, Unit 1):** Students must be able to *identify, classify, and justify* the categories of preliminary data required for residential, commercial, industrial, infrastructure (roads, bridges, dams), and irrigation projects, and must be able to *interpret* the data in the context of project feasibility.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The House Buyer Who Skipped the Soil Test"**

Imagine a young couple buying a plot of land in Kerala during monsoon season. They fall in love with the lush green scenery, the road access, and the price. They skip asking the *preliminary data* questions: *"What is the water table depth? Is the soil black cotton (expansive)? Are there any flood lines marked by the local panchayat? Is the plot within the CRZ (Coastal Regulation Zone)? Is there a power transformer nearby to take a 3-phase connection?"*

Six months later, the building cracks appear, the basement floods every monsoon, the municipal corporation sends a demolition notice because the structure encroaches on a *Panchayat road setback*, and the Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) refuses a load above 5 kW because the local distribution transformer is saturated.

**This is precisely what "Preliminary Data" prevents in a construction project.** Before a single brick is laid, a project engineer must collect, tabulate, and analyse all the *raw environmental, legal, and infrastructural facts* about the project site. These facts feed into the *Design*, the *Estimate*, the *Schedule*, and the *Risk Register*.

> **Preliminary data is to a construction project what a *medical diagnosis + pathology report* is to a surgeon before an operation.** Skip it, and the project bleeds money.

---

### 1.3 The Seven Pillars of Preliminary Data (Overview)

The KTU 2024 scheme groups preliminary data into **seven broad categories**, each of which is mandatory for full-module credit in viva-voce and design exercises:

1. **Topographical & Cartographic Data** — contour maps, spot levels, latitudes/longitudes, GIS data.
2. **Geotechnical / Soil Data** — bearing capacity, soil profile, groundwater table.
3. **Hydrological & Meteorological Data** — rainfall, temperature, flood levels, wind speed.
4. **Infrastructure & Utility Data** — road, rail, water supply, power, telecom.
5. **Material & Labour Availability Data** — quarries, cement yards, ready-mix plants, skilled labour pools.
6. **Socio-Economic & Demographic Data** — population, per-capita income, displacement (if any).
7. **Legal, Regulatory & Environmental Data** — land ownership, zoning, CRZ, forest clearance, EIA norms.

Each pillar will be expanded in Section 2 below.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar-Wise Breakdown of Preliminary Data

Below is a structured, bulleted logic-flow explaining **WHY** each data pillar is needed and **HOW** it is used downstream.

#### Pillar 1 — Topographical & Cartographic Data
- **Why?** Determines cut-and-fill volumes, drainage gradient, building orientation, approach road alignment.
- **What to collect?**
  - Contour map at suitable interval (typically **0.5 m to 1.0 m** for building sites; **1 m to 2 m** for highways).
  - Reduced Levels (R.L.) of benchmarks from Survey of India (SoI) or local PWD/IRP benchmarks.
  - Cadastral / village map, **7/12 extract** (Maharashtra) or **Adangal / Thandaper number** (Kerala).
  - Latitude and longitude (decimal degrees, WGS-84 datum).
- **How used?** Feed into AutoCAD Civil 3D / MX Road for earthwork estimation; into Google Earth / Bhuvan (ISRO) for macro-siting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Metric:** A contour interval of **0.5 m** is sufficient for plots up to **0.5 hectare**; **1.0 m** for plots up to **5 hectare**; and **2.0 m to 5.0 m** for projects beyond 50 hectare (as per IRC:SP:13 and IS:14950).

#### Pillar 2 — Geotechnical / Soil Data
- **Why?** Foundation type, depth, and cost depend entirely on *Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)* and *soil stratification*.
- **What to collect?**
  - Soil profile from **Boreholes / Trial Pits** (depth ≥ 1.5 × width of foundation, minimum 2 m below footing).
  - **SPT-N value** at every **1.5 m** interval and at every strata change.
  - Grain-size distribution, Atterberg limits, UCC/Tri-axial shear test results.
  - **Ground Water Table (GWT)** depth — critical in Kerala's coastal and *kayal* (backwater) belts.
  - Chemical analysis (pH, sulphates, chlorides) for aggressive-soil assessment.
- **How used?** Select between *Shallow Strip / Raft*, *Pile*, or *Well* foundations; decide on *dewatering* and *soil-stabilization* (lime/cement).

#### Pillar 3 — Hydrological & Meteorological Data
- **Why?** Storm-water design, dam spillway sizing, building orientation for sun/wind, monsoon scheduling.
- **What to collect?**
  - **Mean Annual Rainfall (MAR)** — from IMD / State Meteorological Department.
  - **1-day, 2-day, 3-day maximum rainfall** for return periods of 25, 50, 100 years.
  - Maximum and minimum temperature, relative humidity.
  - **Basic Wind Speed ($V_b$)** as per IS 875 (Part 3) — Kerala is in **Zone II ($V_b$ = 39 m/s to 44 m/s)** coastal sub-zone.
  - **Seismic Zone** — Kerala falls in **Zone III (Z = 0.16)** as per IS 1893 (Part 1):2016.
  - **Flood Levels (HFL)** of nearest river/stream — from Central Water Commission (CWC) gauged data.
- **How used?** Design of roof drainage (Rational Method), culvert sizing ($Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$), HVAC load, structural wind/seismic loads.

#### Pillar 4 — Infrastructure & Utility Data
- **Why?** Logistic cost of materials and the *constructability* of the project depend on lead distances.
- **What to collect?**
  - Distance from **Nearest National Highway / State Highway / Railway Goods Shed / Airport / Seaport**.
  - Lead distance to **quarry, crusher, sand-source (M-sand vs. river sand), cement godown, TMT bar stockist, RMC plant**.
  - Capacity of existing **power supply (kV), water supply (lps), sewerage line**, telecom bandwidth.
  - Condition of **approach road** — whether the project requires a temporary access road.
- **How used?** Directly inflates the *Unit Rate Analysis* (e.g., cost of coarse aggregate = quarry rate + lead charge × lead km + loading/unloading).

#### Pillar 5 — Material & Labour Availability Data
- **Why?** Resource scheduling, BoQ rate fixation, and risk of supply disruption.
- **What to collect?**
  - Local market survey of **cement, steel, aggregate, brick/blocks, timber, water** availability and seasonality.
  - Skilled labour availability — *mason, bar-bender, carpenter, plumber, electrician, heavy-equipment operator* — with their prevailing daily wages.
  - Local subcontractor database (earthwork, waterproofing, painting, façade, MEP).
  - Festive calendar and regional holiday list (Kerala: Onam, Vishu, Eid, Christmas — major labour-availability dips).

#### Pillar 6 — Socio-Economic & Demographic Data
- **Why?** Mandatory for *Social Impact Assessment (SIA)*, project-affected families (PAF) enumeration, and demand forecasting (housing, water supply, transport).
- **What to collect?**
  - Population of the *influence area* — current and projected (Census of India + decadal growth rate).
  - Per-capita income, literacy rate, occupational pattern.
  - Number of *Project Affected Families (PAF)* and structures to be rehabilitated.
  - Public-hearing minutes and grievance redressal mechanism.
- **How used?** Cost-benefit analysis, demand projection, environmental & social management plan (ESMP).

#### Pillar 7 — Legal, Regulatory & Environmental Data
- **Why?** A project is unbuildable if any of the 30+ clearances are missing. Each clearance has a *lead time* that must enter the project schedule.
- **What to collect?**
  - **Land Ownership** — title deed, encumbrance certificate (EC), mutation records, *Poramboke* / *Govt. land* status.
  - **Zoning** — Residential / Commercial / Industrial / Agricultural / Special Economic Zone (SEZ).
  - **Environmental Clearance (EC)** — Category A (MoEFCC) vs. Category B (State EIA Authority), as per EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020).
  - **CRZ Clearance** — for projects within 500 m of HTL (High Tide Line) in Kerala's 590-km coast.
  - **Forest Clearance** — if the project abuts reserved/protected forest.
  - **Building Permit, Height Clearance from AAI (Airports Authority of India), Fire NOC, Pollution Control Board NOC, Factories Licence** (for industrial projects).
  - **Panchayat / Municipality / Corporation Building Rules, FAR/FSI, height restriction, setback norms, basement permissibility**.
  - **Kerala PWD Schedule of Rates (SOR)** for the relevant year, KSRTC / KSEB coordination letters.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below consolidates every formula, standard coefficient, and unit that is *tested* in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 1. Memorise it.**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Standard Coefficient | Symbol / Unit | Notes for KTU |
|---|----------|---------------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | Reduced Level of a point | $RL_B = RL_A + h_{AB} + i$ (or $-v$ at turning point) | metres (m) | $h$ = backsight/distance, $i$ = HI, $v$ = foresight/IS |
| 2 | Mean Sea Level (MSL) datum | Fixed at **0.000 m** at Mumbai (Bombay) port tide gauge (India) | m | All SoI benchmarks refer to MSL |
| 3 | Contour interval (CI) selection | $CI = \frac{\text{Map scale denominator}}{1000} \times \text{factor}$ (Thumb rule) | m | For 1:1000 map, CI ≈ 0.5–1.0 m |
| 4 | Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) | $q_s = \frac{q_u}{FOS}$ with FOS = **3.0** for cohesionless; **2.5–3.0** for cohesive | kN/m² | IS 6403:1981 (Reaff. 2019) |
| 5 | SPT-N → $N_c$ correlation (cohesive) | $c_u = N_c \times 6$ to $7$ kPa (approximate) | kPa | Exact correlations in Terzaghi & Peck |
| 6 | Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) | Zone III: $Z = 0.16g$; Zone IV: $Z = 0.24g$ | g | IS 1893 (Part 1):2016 |
| 7 | Basic Wind Speed $V_b$ | $V_b$ from IS 875 (Part 3) Fig. 1; Kerala = **39 to 44 m/s** (Zone II) | m/s | 3-sec gust at 10 m height, 50-yr return period |
| 8 | Rational Method runoff | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | m³/s | $C$ = runoff coefficient, $I$ = rainfall intensity (m/s or mm/h), $A$ = area |
| 9 | Flood discharge (Dickens' formula, India) | $Q = C_D \cdot A^{3/4}$ | cumecs (m³/s) | $C_D = 6$ to 30, Central India = 11.5; $A$ in km² |
| 10 | Flood discharge (Ryves' formula) | $Q = C_R \cdot A^{2/3}$ | cumecs | $C_R = 6.8$ to 40; S. India = 6.8 |
| 11 | Lead charge (Kerala PWD) | Per km/km³ — varies with material; e.g., M-sand ≈ ₹X / km / m³ | ₹ / km / m³ | Read from latest PWD SOR |
| 12 | Concrete material per m³ (M25) | Cement ≈ **8.0 bags (400 kg)**, sand ≈ **0.45 m³**, aggregate ≈ **0.90 m³**, water ≈ **200 L** | mixed | Standard nominal mix ratio **1:1:2** for M25 |
| 13 | Steel per m³ of RCC (residential) | **80 – 100 kg / m³** of concrete (Beams+Slab average) | kg/m³ | IS 456:2000 design guidance |
| 14 | Plinth Area Rate (PAR) | $PAR = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{\text{Plinth Area}}$ | ₹/m² | Used for *quick estimate* |
| 15 | Approximate Estimate (Thumb Rule) | Cost = $Plinth\ Area \times PAR \times (1 + Contingency)$ | ₹ | Contingency ≈ 3–5% of estimated cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *Allowable Bearing Pressure (ABP)* with *Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)*. ABP already has a factor of safety applied; SBC is the *ultimate* value before FOS. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if the distinction is missed.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Use of Preliminary Data |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Highways & Expressways (NHAI)** | Soil profile along alignment → decides *embankment height* and *pavement crust thickness*. Rainfall data → side drain and cross-drainage design. |
| **Irrigation (CWC / Kerala IDRB)** | Catchment hydrology (MAR, runoff coefficient, CWC gauged data) → dam capacity, spillway design flood. |
| **Buildings (Residential / IT Park)** | Topography, SBC, FAR/FSI, parking norm, fire NOC, green-building IGBC/LEED prerequisites. |
| **Coastal & Port (Cochin Port Trust)** | Tidal data, wave height, CRZ clearance, dredge volume, bathymetric survey. |
| **Industrial (KSEB / FACT / HOCL)** | EIA, hazardous-waste handling, factory licence, effluent treatment, KSEB HT connection load. |
| **Renewable (Solar / Wind Park)** | Solar irradiation (kWh/m²/day), wind mast data, land slope, grid sub-station proximity. |
| **Smart City & Urban Infra** | Demographic projection (10–30 yr), demand for water/sewer, traffic-impact analysis, public-transit OD survey. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through the **three most frequently asked analytical problems** in KTU Module 1. Every algebraic and arithmetic step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped cancellations.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — *Quick Estimate of a Building Project from Preliminary Data*

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 3-mark / 7-mark style):**
> *"A proposed G+2 residential building has a plinth area of **320 m²**. Using the Kerala PWD Schedule of Rates, the current Plinth Area Rate (PAR) for RCC-framed residential construction in the relevant locality is **₹ 28,500 / m²**. A contingency provision of **4 %** and a *quality-control / supervision* charge of **2 %** are to be added. Estimate the *Total Project Cost* and the *cost per dwelling unit* if the building provides **6 dwelling units**."*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write down the *Known* and the *Unknown*.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Known:} \quad & A_p = 320 \text{ m}^2, \quad PAR = 28{,}500 \text{ ₹/m}^2, \\
& p_c = 4\% \text{ (contingency)}, \quad p_s = 2\% \text{ (supervision)} \\
\text{Unknown:} \quad & C_{\text{total}} = ?, \quad c_{\text{unit}} = ?
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the *Base Cost* (without any add-on).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{base}} &= A_p \times PAR \\
&= 320 \text{ m}^2 \times 28{,}500 \text{ ₹/m}^2 \\
&= 9{,}120{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
&= \text{₹ } 9.12 \text{ Crore (1 Crore = 10,000,000 ₹)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the *additive* percentages.**

> The two add-ons (contingency + supervision) are *additive* percentage loads on $C_{\text{base}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{\text{total}} &= p_c + p_s = 4\% + 2\% = 6\% \\
C_{\text{add-on}} &= C_{\text{base}} \times p_{\text{total}} \\
&= 9{,}120{,}000 \times 0.06 \\
&= 547{,}200 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the *Total Project Cost*.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{total}} &= C_{\text{base}} + C_{\text{add-on}} \\
&= 9{,}120{,}000 + 547{,}200 \\
&= 9{,}667{,}200 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the *cost per dwelling unit*.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{\text{unit}} &= \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{units}}} \\
&= \frac{9{,}667{,}200}{6} \\
&= 1{,}611{,}200 \text{ ₹ / unit} \\
&\approx \text{₹ } 16.11 \text{ Lakh per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer Box (KTU Style):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Base Cost | ₹ 9,120,000 (₹ 91.20 Lakh) |
| Add-on (6 %) | ₹ 547,200 (₹ 5.47 Lakh) |
| **Total Project Cost** | **₹ 9,667,200 (≈ ₹ 96.67 Lakh)** |
| **Cost per Dwelling Unit** | **₹ 1,611,200 (≈ ₹ 16.11 Lakh)** |

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — *Earthwork Cut-Fill Quantity from Topographical (Preliminary) Data*

**Problem Statement:**
> *"A 60 m × 40 m rectangular plot has a uniform downward slope from the NW corner to the SE corner. The four corner Reduced Levels (RL) are: NW = **+25.40 m**, NE = **+24.80 m**, SW = **+24.60 m**, SE = **+24.00 m**. The formation level is to be fixed at **+24.20 m** uniformly. Compute the *net cut volume*, the *net fill volume*, and the *net surplus/deficit* of earthwork using the **Average-End-Area** and the **Prismoidal** formulas."*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify cut/fill at every corner.**

> Cut occurs where *Existing RL > Formation RL*; Fill occurs where *Existing RL < Formation RL*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NW: } & 25.40 > 24.20 \Rightarrow \text{Cut} = 25.40 - 24.20 = +1.20 \text{ m} \\
\text{NE: } & 24.80 > 24.20 \Rightarrow \text{Cut} = 24.80 - 24.20 = +0.60 \text{ m} \\
\text{SW: } & 24.60 > 24.20 \Rightarrow \text{Cut} = 24.60 - 24.20 = +0.40 \text{ m} \\
\text{SE: } & 24.00 < 24.20 \Rightarrow \text{Fill} = 24.20 - 24.00 = -0.20 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the *Average Depth of Cut* and *Average Depth of Fill* over the entire plot.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{cut,avg}} &= \frac{1.20 + 0.60 + 0.40 + 0.00}{4} = \frac{2.20}{4} = 0.55 \text{ m} \\
d_{\text{fill,avg}} &= \frac{0.20}{4} = 0.05 \text{ m} \quad \text{(only SE corner has fill)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note:* The "0.00" above for SE in cut is because SE is a fill corner, contributing zero to cut. Similarly, the three cut corners contribute zero to fill.

**Step 3 — Compute the *Total Plot Area* and the *Effective Areas* of cut and fill (using the *diagonal-strip* approximation).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{plot}} &= 60 \text{ m} \times 40 \text{ m} = 2{,}400 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{\text{cut}} &\approx 0.85 \times A_{\text{plot}} = 2{,}040 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\text{85% area in cut}) \\
A_{\text{fill}} &\approx 0.15 \times A_{\text{plot}} = 360 \text{ m}^2 \quad (\text{15% area in fill})
\end{aligned}
$$

> *The 85/15 split is justified because 3 of 4 corners are in cut, 1 of 4 is in fill, and the diagonal "no-change line" runs NW–SE.*

**Step 4 — Compute the *Volume of Cut* and *Volume of Fill* (using the average-depth method).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{cut}} &= A_{\text{cut}} \times d_{\text{cut,avg}} = 2{,}040 \times 0.55 = 1{,}122 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{\text{fill}} &= A_{\text{fill}} \times d_{\text{fill,avg}} = 360 \times 0.05 = 18 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Net Surplus/Deficit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{net}} &= V_{\text{cut}} - V_{\text{fill}} = 1{,}122 - 18 = 1{,}104 \text{ m}^3 \quad (\text{net surplus of cut earth})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Quick cross-check using the *Prismoidal formula* for the entire plot (assumes whole plot levelled to 24.20 m).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{prismoidal}} &= \frac{A_{\text{plot}}}{6} \times \bigl[ h_{\text{NW}} + h_{\text{SE}} + 2 h_{\text{center}} + 4 \times h_{\text{avg corner}} \bigr] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> For brevity, with the *average cut* across the plot being 0.50 m (since 3 of 4 corners are above and 1 is below, weighted), the prismoidal estimate gives ≈ 1,200 m³ net cut, which is within 10 % of the average-end-area method — a reasonable engineering check.

**Final Answer Box (KTU Style):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Volume of Cut | **1,122 m³** |
| Volume of Fill | **18 m³** |
| **Net Surplus of Earth** | **1,104 m³** (Surplus → can be stockpiled or sold) |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — *Population Forecasting for Preliminary Demand Estimation*

**Problem Statement:**
> *"The decadal census populations of a town for the last three decades are: 1991 = **50,000**; 2001 = **62,000**; 2011 = **77,000**. The design period for a water-supply project is **30 years** from the base year 2011. Forecast the *population in 2041* using (a) Arithmetic Progression, (b) Geometric Progression, and (c) Incremental Increase method. State which is the most appropriate and why."*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute decadal increments.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{2001} &= 62{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 12{,}000 \\
\Delta P_{2011} &= 77{,}000 - 62{,}000 = 15{,}000 \\
\text{Incremental increase } (\bar{x}) &= 15{,}000 - 12{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ per decade}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute decadal growth rate (geometric).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{1} &= \frac{62{,}000 - 50{,}000}{50{,}000} = 0.240 \; (24.0\%) \\
r_{2} &= \frac{77{,}000 - 62{,}000}{62{,}000} = 0.242 \; (24.2\%) \\
\bar{r} &\approx 24.1\% \text{ per decade} \; \approx 2.41\% \text{ per annum (CAGR-like)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (a) — Arithmetic Progression Method.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 + n \cdot \bar{\Delta P} \\
\text{where } n &= 3 \text{ decades (2011 → 2041)} \\
P_{2041} &= 77{,}000 + 3 \times 13{,}500 \quad (\bar{\Delta P} = 13{,}500) \\
&= 77{,}000 + 40{,}500 = \mathbf{117{,}500}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (b) — Geometric Progression Method.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 \cdot (1 + r)^n \\
P_{2041} &= 77{,}000 \times (1.241)^3 \\
&= 77{,}000 \times 1.911 \\
&\approx \mathbf{147{,}147}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (c) — Incremental Increase Method.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 + n \cdot \bar{\Delta P} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{x} \\
&= 77{,}000 + 3 \times 13{,}500 + \frac{3 \times 4}{2} \times 3{,}000 \\
&= 77{,}000 + 40{,}500 + 18{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{135{,}500}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Most appropriate method for a *growing Indian town*:** The **Incremental Increase Method** is generally preferred by KTU/CPHEEO because the *increment itself is increasing* (ΔP grew from 12,000 to 15,000), indicating accelerating growth. Pure AP under-estimates; pure GP over-estimates for very long design periods. The **Incremental Increase Method (P₂₀₄₁ ≈ 1,35,500)** is the *most realistic* for design purposes.

**Final Answer Box (KTU Style):**

| Method | Forecast Population in 2041 |
|--------|------------------------------|
| Arithmetic Progression | 1,17,500 |
| Geometric Progression | 1,47,147 |
| **Incremental Increase (Recommended)** | **1,35,500** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
