## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 1]**

**Q1. List any **six** categories of preliminary data that must be collected before the design of a residential building project in Kerala. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The six essential categories of preliminary data for a residential building project in Kerala are:

1. **Topographical data** — contour map, reduced levels, slope of the plot, latitude and longitude.
2. **Geotechnical data** — borehole log, SPT-N value, safe bearing capacity, groundwater table depth (especially important in Kerala's high-WT coastal zones).
3. **Hydrological and meteorological data** — annual rainfall (≈ 3000 mm in Kerala), wind zone (IS 875 Zone II), seismic zone (IS 1893 Zone III).
4. **Infrastructure data** — lead distance to quarry, RMC plant, KSEB sub-station capacity, municipal water-supply line location.
5. **Material and labour data** — availability and prevailing market rate of cement, M-sand, TMT bars, and local masons/bar-benders.
6. **Legal and regulatory data** — land title, Kerala Municipality/Panchayat building rules, FAR/FSI, height restriction, CRZ clearance (if near coast), and Environmental Clearance (if plot > 20,000 m² as per EIA Notification 2006).

> **Marking Note:** 0.5 marks for each of the six categories; full credit for naming them precisely with at least one Kerala-specific example.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 2]**

**Q2. Differentiate between *Allowable Bearing Pressure (ABP)* and *Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC)* with one numerical example. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Safe Bearing Capacity (SBC) | Allowable Bearing Pressure (ABP) |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Definition | The *maximum* bearing pressure the soil can sustain *before* failure. | The *net* bearing pressure the soil can safely support *after* applying a Factor of Safety (FOS). |
| FOS applied? | No (raw / ultimate value). | Yes (typically FOS = 2.5 to 3.0). |
| Formula | $SBC = q_u$ | $ABP = \dfrac{SBC}{FOS}$ |
| **Numerical Example** | If $q_u = 300$ kN/m², then **SBC = 300 kN/m²** | With FOS = 3, **ABP = 100 kN/m²** |

> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for correct definition, 1 mark for FOS distinction, 1 mark for the numerical example. Avoid using the term "bearing capacity" without qualifying "safe" or "allowable".

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023 – Module 1, Question Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Describe, with a real-world example, the seven categories of preliminary data required for the construction of a highway project. State at least **two specific data items** for each category. (7 Marks)**

**(b) For a 1200 m² rectangular plot with corner RLs of 32.10, 31.85, 31.50, and 31.05 m (NW, NE, SW, SE respectively), design a uniform formation level such that the **cut volume equals the fill volume** (balanced earthwork). Use the average-end-area method. Assume a uniform 4-sided slope. (7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Seven Categories for Highway Project:**

1. **Topographical Data** — (i) Contour map at 2 m CI; (ii) Longitudinal profile (L-section) along proposed centreline; (iii) Cross-sections every 50 m; (iv) Intersection list, curve data.
2. **Geotechnical Data** — (i) CBR value (for flexible pavement design as per IRC:37); (ii) Subgrade soil classification (IS:1498); (iii) SPT-N at 2 m intervals along alignment; (iv) Groundwater table depth.
3. **Hydrological & Meteorological Data** — (i) 1-day maximum rainfall of 50-year return period (for cross-drainage design); (ii) 100-year HFL of all rivers/stream crossings; (iii) Wind zone (for sign-board and high-mast design).
4. **Infrastructure Data** — (i) Lead distance from quarry to each km-stone of the alignment; (ii) Distances to existing rail/port for bitumen/cement transport; (iii) Existing utility crossings (water mains, OFC cables, gas lines) needing permission from KWA, BSNL, GAIL.
5. **Material & Labour Data** — (i) Location and capacity of hot-mix plants, crushers, and WMM plants; (ii) Local availability of unskilled labour for earthwork; (iii) Prevailing daily wages as per Kerala PWD SOR.
6. **Socio-Economic Data** — (i) Census of project-affected families; (ii) Traffic-impact assessment (OD survey) for toll-revenue forecast; (iii) Land-acquisition cost per hectare.
7. **Legal & Regulatory Data** — (i) Forest clearance if alignment passes through reserve forest (e.g., Western Ghats); (ii) CRZ clearance for coastal stretches; (iii) NOC from Railways for ROB/RUB; (iv) Environmental Clearance as per MoEFCC norms.

> **Marking Note:** 1 mark per category × 7 = 7 marks. Examiner expects at least *one quantitative* item per category.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Balanced Earthwork:**

**Step 1 — Compute the average of the four corner RLs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{RL} &= \frac{32.10 + 31.85 + 31.50 + 31.05}{4} \\
&= \frac{126.50}{4} \\
&= 31.625 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the depth of cut/fill at each corner relative to $\overline{RL} = 31.625$ m.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{NW} &= 32.10 - 31.625 = +0.475 \text{ m (cut)} \\
h_{NE} &= 31.85 - 31.625 = +0.225 \text{ m (cut)} \\
h_{SW} &= 31.50 - 31.625 = -0.125 \text{ m (fill)} \\
h_{SE} &= 31.05 - 31.625 = -0.575 \text{ m (fill)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average depth of cut and fill (areas in m² are the same fraction of the total since the plot is rectangular).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{h}_{\text{cut}} &= \frac{0.475 + 0.225}{2} = 0.350 \text{ m} \\
\bar{h}_{\text{fill}} &= \frac{0.125 + 0.575}{2} = 0.350 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification — the average cut depth equals the average fill depth → balanced earthwork automatically achieved.**

> *The formation level that balances cut = fill is exactly the **arithmetic mean of the four corner RLs**, **i.e., 31.625 m**.* This is a special property of a rectangular plot levelled uniformly with *equal* cut and fill *volumes*.

**Step 5 — Compute the cut volume (which equals the fill volume).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{plot}} &= 1200 \text{ m}^2 \quad \text{(given)} \\
\text{By symmetry of the cut/fill diagonal: } A_{\text{cut}} &\approx A_{\text{fill}} \approx 600 \text{ m}^2 \\
V_{\text{cut}} &= 600 \times 0.350 = 210 \text{ m}^3 \\
V_{\text{fill}} &= 600 \times 0.350 = 210 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Expectation:** 4 marks for the formation level (31.625 m), 2 marks for the cut/fill depth table, 1 mark for the final volume. Most students lose 2 marks by *rounding* the formation level — *do not round; retain 3 decimals*.

**Final Answer Box (KTU Style):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Formation Level for balanced cut-fill | **31.625 m** |
| Cut Volume | **210 m³** |
| Fill Volume | **210 m³** |
| Status | **Perfectly balanced earthwork** |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a) Explain the *purpose*, *data items*, and *typical sources* of preliminary data collection for a multi-storeyed commercial building in a Tier-2 city. (7 Marks)**

**(b) The decadal population of a town is given below. Forecast the population in 2031 using the (i) Arithmetic, (ii) Geometric, and (iii) Incremental-Increase methods, and state which is most appropriate. (7 Marks)**

| Year | Population |
|------|------------|
| 1991 | 40,000 |
| 2001 | 48,000 |
| 2011 | 59,000 |

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Multi-storeyed Commercial Building:**

**Purpose:** Preliminary data collection aims to *minimise design and construction risk*, *optimise cost*, *ensure regulatory compliance*, and *establish constructability* before any design or estimate is finalised. For a commercial building (e.g., IT park, mall, hospital), the stakes are *high* because the developer is making a 50–200 Crore investment.

**Data Items and Sources:**

| Data Category | Key Items | Typical Source |
|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| Topographical | Contour map, RL, slope, soil type | Licensed surveyor, Google Earth Pro, Bhuvan (ISRO), SoI topo-sheet |
| Geotechnical | Borehole log, SPT-N, SBC, chemical analysis | Geotechnical consultancy (e.g., CEG, IIT Palakkad) |
| Hydrometeorological | Rainfall, wind, seismic zone | IMD, IS 875 (Part 3), IS 1893, CWC |
| Infrastructure | Lead distance, road width, power (HT/LT) capacity | Local PWD, KSEB, KWA, BSNL, municipality |
| Material & Labour | Cement/steel rate, RMC plants, contractor list | Kerala PWD SOR, market survey, CREDAI Kerala |
| Socio-Economic | Catchment population, average IT salary, foot-fall projection | Census of India, district statistical handbook |
| Legal & Reg. | Land title, EC, FAR, parking norm, fire NOC, AAI height NOC, EC | Sub-registrar, Municipality, Fire & Rescue, AAI, MoEFCC |

> **Marking Note:** 3 marks for purpose, 2 marks for data items (any 5 listed with 1 example), 2 marks for sources (1 mark each from 2 distinct categories).

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Population Forecast:**

**Step 1 — Compute decadal increments and the incremental increase.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{2001} &= 48{,}000 - 40{,}000 = 8{,}000 \\
\Delta P_{2011} &= 59{,}000 - 48{,}000 = 11{,}000 \\
\text{Incremental Increase } (\bar{x}) &= 11{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 3{,}000 \\
\bar{\Delta P} &= \frac{8{,}000 + 11{,}000}{2} = 9{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric growth rate (per decade).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \frac{48{,}000}{40{,}000} - 1 = 0.200 \quad (20.0\%) \\
r_2 &= \frac{59{,}000}{48{,}000} - 1 = 0.229 \quad (22.9\%) \\
\bar{r} &= \frac{0.200 + 0.229}{2} = 0.2145 \quad (21.45\% \text{ per decade})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Forecast to 2031 (n = 2 decades from 2011).**

**(i) Arithmetic Progression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2031} &= P_{2011} + n \cdot \bar{\Delta P} \\
&= 59{,}000 + 2 \times 9{,}500 \\
&= 59{,}000 + 19{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{78{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Geometric Progression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2031} &= P_{2011} \cdot (1 + \bar{r})^n \\
&= 59{,}000 \times (1.2145)^2 \\
&= 59{,}000 \times 1.4750 \\
&\approx \mathbf{87{,}025}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Incremental Increase Method:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2031} &= P_{2011} + n \cdot \bar{\Delta P} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{x} \\
&= 59{,}000 + 2 \times 9{,}500 + \frac{2 \times 3}{2} \times 3{,}000 \\
&= 59{,}000 + 19{,}000 + 9{,}000 \\
&= \mathbf{87{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Recommendation:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Incremental Increase Method (87,000)** is the most realistic because the town's growth-rate is *accelerating* (each decade adds more than the previous). The AP method (78,000) *under-estimates* because it assumes a constant absolute increase, while GP (87,025) closely matches the incremental method when the per-decade rate is roughly constant — here the GP and incremental methods converge, validating both. **KTU examiners prefer the Incremental Increase Method** for Tier-2/3 Indian cities with accelerating growth.

**Final Answer Box (KTU Style):**

| Method | Forecast Population in 2031 |
|--------|------------------------------|
| Arithmetic Progression | 78,000 |
| Geometric Progression | 87,025 |
| **Incremental Increase (Recommended)** | **87,000** |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Module 1 (Preliminary Data) questions:**
> 1. **Confusing *SBC* with *ABP*** — Examiner deducts 1 mark. Always write: *SBC = ultimate; ABP = SBC / FOS.*
> 2. **Skipping Kerala-specific data items** (GWT, CRZ, IMD rainfall) — Examiner expects *local relevance* in 2024 scheme. Generic answers score ≤ 60 %.
> 3. **Wrong population-forecast formula** — The Incremental-Increase method requires the term $\frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{x}$. Students often miss the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ or write $n(n-1)$ instead of $n(n+1)$. Loss: 2 marks.
> 4. **Rounding off Reduced Levels prematurely** — Always carry **3 decimal places** for RL in contour-related problems. Premature rounding causes cumulative error of 0.01–0.05 m, which in 1200 m² plots = 12–60 m³ of error.
> 5. **Omitting the "Sources" of data** in 7-mark theory questions — KTU 2024 explicitly tests *applied* knowledge. A list of data items *without* their source is marked down by 30 %.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PECHT524 Module 1 — Preliminary Data:**

- ☐ **Seven categories** of preliminary data: Topographical, Geotechnical, Hydrometeorological, Infrastructure, Material & Labour, Socio-Economic, Legal & Regulatory. Remember all seven in order.
- ☐ **RL formula**: $RL = BS + HI$ at a station; $HI_{next} = RL_{TP} + FS_{TP}$.
- ☐ **Contour Interval** for small plots = **0.5 m**; medium = **1.0 m**; large = **2–5 m**.
- ☐ **SBC vs. ABP** — distinguish correctly; FOS = 2.5–3.0.
- ☐ **Kerala Wind Zone** = **II** ($V_b \approx 39$–$44$ m/s); **Seismic Zone** = **III** ($Z = 0.16$).
- ☐ **MAR for Kerala** ≈ **3000 mm**; **IMD** is the source; **CWC** for flood data.
- ☐ **Plinth Area Rate (PAR)** = current **Kerala PWD SOR** rate (revised yearly).
- ☐ **Quick Estimate Formula**: $C_{\text{total}} = A_p \times PAR \times (1 + \text{contingency} + \text{supervision})$.
- ☐ **Balanced Earthwork Trick**: For a rectangular plot, formation level = arithmetic mean of 4 corner RLs.
- ☐ **Population Forecast**: AP under-estimates, GP over-estimates (long term), **Incremental Increase** is best for accelerating-growth Indian towns.
- ☐ **EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020)** for environmental clearance thresholds.
- ☐ **CRZ 2019 Notification** for projects within 500 m of HTL on the Kerala coast.
- ☐ **IS 6403:1981** for bearing-capacity calculation; **IS 1498:1970** for soil classification.
- ☐ **KSRB / Kerala PWD Schedule of Rates** is the *de facto* rate reference for KTU assignments.
- ☐ **KSEB** = power utility; **KWA** = water; **PWD** = roads; **Kerala Municipality Building Rules** for FAR / setback / height.

> **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1, Topic: Preliminary Data for Construction of Projects.**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — PLANNING AND SCHEDULING OF PROJECTS

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Planning** is the systematic process of defining project objectives, identifying activities, establishing logical dependencies, estimating resource requirements, and determining the optimal sequence of execution to achieve the deliverables within the stipulated time, cost, and quality constraints.
>
> **Project Scheduling** is the operational phase that follows planning — it converts the plan into a time-bound calendar of activities by assigning start dates, finish dates, durations, and float values, often visualized using **Bar Charts (Gantt Charts)**, **Programme Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)**, and **Critical Path Method (CPM)**.

The two pillars of scheduling covered in this module are:

- **Bar Chart (Gantt Chart)** — A graphical time-vs-activity representation developed by **Henry Gantt (1917)**.
- **CPM (Critical Path Method)** — A deterministic, activity-oriented network technique developed in **1957** by DuPont and Remington Rand for plant maintenance projects.
- **PERT (Programme Evaluation and Review Technique)** — A probabilistic, event-oriented technique developed in **1958** by the U.S. Navy for the Polaris Missile project.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Planning a Wedding結婚式**
>
> Imagine you are organizing a large Indian wedding. You cannot serve the **Biryani** before the **Basmati Rice** is cooked, and you cannot call the **photographer** before the **mandap** is set up. There is a logical chain of dependencies.
>
> - A **Bar Chart** is like writing all tasks on a wall calendar with colored sticky notes — quick to see, but you cannot tell which task delays will break the wedding day.
> - **CPM** is like identifying the **chain of critical tasks** (booking hall → pandit → catering → lighting) — if any one of these slips by even one day, the entire wedding shifts.
> - **PERT** is like saying *"the pandit might take 2 hours, usually 3, but worst-case 4"* — it works on uncertainty.
>
> The **Critical Path** is the *longest* chain of dependent tasks — the minimum time your project *must* take.

### 1.1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following definitions are **must-know one-mark concepts** for KTU:
>
> - **Activity (A)** — A work element that consumes time and resources.
> - **Event / Node** — A milestone representing the start or completion of an activity; consumes **zero time** and **zero resources**.
> - **Dummy Activity** — A logical dependency with **zero duration** used to maintain correct precedence in Activity-on-Arrow (AOA) networks.
> - **EST (Earliest Start Time)** — The earliest time an activity can begin.
> - **EFT (Earliest Finish Time)** — EST + Duration.
> - **LST (Latest Start Time)** — The latest time an activity can start without delaying the project.
> - **LFT (Latest Finish Time)** — LST + Duration.
> - **Critical Path** — The longest path through the network (sum of durations is **maximum**). All activities on this path have **zero float**.

# 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1.2.1 Bar Chart (Gantt Chart) — Methodology

A Bar Chart plots activities on the **Y-axis** against **time (calendar)** on the X-axis. Each activity is represented by a horizontal bar whose length is proportional to its duration.

### Steps to Construct a Bar Chart:
1. **List all activities** of the project from the Work Breakdown Structure (WBS).
2. **Estimate duration** of each activity.
3. **Determine precedence** (which activity must precede which).
4. **Plot horizontal bars** starting from Day 1 with lengths matching durations.
5. **Mark milestones** with diamond symbols (♦).
6. **Update progress** periodically using shaded portions inside the bar.

### Limitations of Bar Charts:
- Cannot show **logical interdependencies** clearly.
- Cannot identify the **critical path**.
- Difficult to update for large projects.
- Does not handle **uncertainty** in durations.

### Advantages of Bar Charts:
- **Simple**, intuitive, and visually clear.
- Easy communication with non-technical stakeholders.
- Useful for **progress monitoring**.

## 1.2.2 Critical Path Method (CPM) — Deterministic Network

### Logic Steps in CPM (KTU Board Pattern):

**Step 1 — Draw the Network Diagram (AOA or AON)**
- Activity-on-Arrow (AOA): Arrows = activities, Nodes = events.
- Activity-on-Node (AON): Nodes = activities, Arrows = logical dependencies.

**Step 2 — Forward Pass (Calculates EST & EFT)**
Rules:
- For the **starting node**: $E_1 = 0$
- For subsequent events: $E_j = \max_{i \to j} \{E_i + D_{ij}\}$
- $EFT_{ij} = EST_{ij} + D_{ij}$

**Step 3 — Backward Pass (Calculates LST & LFT)**
Rules:
- For the **ending node**: $L_n = E_n$ (project completion time)
- For preceding events: $L_i = \min_{i \to j} \{L_j - D_{ij}\}$
- $LST_{ij} = LFT_{ij} - D_{ij}$

**Step 4 — Calculate Floats**
- **Total Float (TF)**: $TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - D_{ij} = LFT_{ij} - EFT_{ij}$
- **Free Float (FF)**: $FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - D_{ij}$
- **Independent Float (IF)**: $IF_{ij} = \max\{0, E_j - L_i - D_{ij}\}$

**Step 5 — Identify Critical Path**
- Critical activities are those with **TF = 0**.
- The **critical path** is the continuous chain of critical activities from start to end.

## 1.2.3 Programme Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)

PERT uses **three-time estimates** because real-world activity durations are uncertain:

- $a$ = **Optimistic Time** (everything goes perfectly)
- $m$ = **Most Likely Time** (mode of the distribution)
- $b$ = **Pessimistic Time** (worst-case scenario)

### Beta Distribution Assumption:
PERT assumes the activity duration follows a **Beta distribution**, which approximates a normal distribution for large projects.

### Expected Time (Mean):
$$t_e = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6}$$

### Variance of an Activity:
$$\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^2$$

### Standard Deviation:
$$\sigma = \frac{b - a}{6}$$

### Project Expected Duration:
$$T_E = \sum t_e \text{ along critical path}$$

### Project Variance (along critical path):
$$\sigma_T^2 = \sum \sigma^2 \text{ along critical path}$$

### Probability of Completion in Scheduled Time ($T_s$):
$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_T}$$

Using standard normal tables (Z-tables), find $P(Z \leq z)$.

## 1.2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Earliest Event Time | $E_j = \max\{E_i + D_{ij}\}$ | Forward Pass |
| 2 | Latest Event Time | $L_i = \min\{L_j - D_{ij}\}$ | Backward Pass |
| 3 | Earliest Finish Time | $EFT = EST + D$ | Per activity |
| 4 | Latest Start Time | $LST = LFT - D$ | Per activity |
| 5 | Total Float | $TF = LFT - EFT$ | Critical if $TF = 0$ |
| 6 | Free Float | $FF = E_j - E_i - D_{ij}$ | Independent of successor |
| 7 | Independent Float | $IF = E_j - L_i - D_{ij}$ | Rarely used |
| 8 | PERT Expected Time | $t_e = (a + 4m + b) / 6$ | Weighted mean |
| 9 | PERT Variance | $\sigma^2 = ((b-a)/6)^2$ | Per activity |
| 10 | Project Duration | $T_E = \sum t_e$ (critical path) | Deterministic |
| 11 | Z-value | $Z = (T_s - T_E) / \sigma_T$ | Probability calc |
| 12 | Crash Cost Slope | $\text{Slope} = (CC - NC) / (NT - CT)$ | For crashing |

### Real-World Engineering Utility:
- **Civil Engineering**: Scheduling of high-rise construction, dam construction, highway projects.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Plant shutdown/turnaround maintenance, shipbuilding.
- **Software**: Agile sprint planning with modified PERT (Three-Point estimation).
- **Aerospace**: NASA uses PERT variants for mission planning.

# 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1.3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — CPM Numerical (KTU Board Style)

### Problem Statement:
A small construction project has the following activities with their durations (in days) and precedence:

| Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (days) |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| A | — | 6 |
| B | — | 4 |
| C | A | 5 |
| D | A | 7 |
| E | B, C | 6 |
| F | D | 4 |
| G | E | 5 |
| H | F, G | 3 |

**Required:** Draw the network, find EST, EFT, LST, LFT, all floats, and identify the Critical Path.

---

### Step 1: Network Construction (AOA with Dummy)

Since both **B** and **C** must precede **E**, we draw them accordingly. C depends on A, D depends on A, F depends on D, G depends on E, H depends on F and G.

The network in **Event numbering** becomes:

```
(1) --A=6--> (2) --C=5--> (3) --E=6--> (5) --G=5--> (6) --H=3--> (8)
 (1) --B=4--> (4) --DUMMY(0)--> (3)
 (2) --D=7--> (7) --F=4--> (6)
 (4) -- ends at (8) via... let's recompute properly
```

**Corrected Network (AOA):**

- Node 1 (Start) → A → Node 2
- Node 1 → B → Node 3
- Node 2 → C → Node 4
- Node 2 → D → Node 5
- Node 3 → Dummy (0) → Node 4 (so E waits for both B and C)
- Node 4 → E → Node 6
- Node 5 → F → Node 7
- Node 6 → G → Node 8
- Node 7 → Dummy (0) → Node 8 (so H waits for both F and G)
- Node 8 → H → Node 9 (End)

### Step 2: Forward Pass — Compute Earliest Event Times $E_i$

We compute $E_i$ for each node using the rule:
$$E_j = \max\{E_i + D_{ij}\}$$

**Computation:**

$E_1 = 0$ (Start node)

$E_2 = E_1 + D_A = 0 + 6 = 6$

$E_3 = E_1 + D_B = 0 + 4 = 4$

$E_4 = \max\{E_2 + D_C,\ E_3 + D_{DUMMY}\} = \max\{6+5,\ 4+0\} = \max\{11, 4\} = 11$

$E_5 = E_2 + D_D = 6 + 7 = 13$

$E_6 = E_4 + D_E = 11 + 6 = 17$

$E_7 = E_5 + D_F = 13 + 4 = 17$

$E_8 = \max\{E_6 + D_G,\ E_7 + D_{DUMMY}\} = \max\{17+5,\ 17+0\} = \max\{22, 17\} = 22$

$E_9 = E_8 + D_H = 22 + 3 = 25$

**Result:** Project Duration = $T_E = \mathbf{25\ days}$

### Step 3: Backward Pass — Compute Latest Event Times $L_i$

Rule:
$$L_i = \min\{L_j - D_{ij}\}$$

**Computation:**

$L_9 = E_9 = 25$ (End node equals project duration)

$L_8 = L_9 - D_H = 25 - 3 = 22$

$L_7 = L_8 - D_{DUMMY} = 22 - 0 = 22$

$L_6 = L_8 - D_G = 22 - 5 = 17$

$L_5 = L_7 - D_F = 22 - 4 = 18$

$L_4 = L_6 - D_E = 17 - 6 = 11$

$L_3 = L_4 - D_{DUMMY} = 11 - 0 = 11$

$L_2 = \min\{L_4 - D_C,\ L_5 - D_D\} = \min\{11-5,\ 18-7\} = \min\{6, 11\} = 6$

$L_1 = \min\{L_2 - D_A,\ L_3 - D_B\} = \min\{6-6,\ 11-4\} = \min\{0, 7\} = 0$ ✓

### Step 4: Compute EST, EFT, LST, LFT, TF for Each Activity

Using formulas:
$$EST = E_i,\ EFT = EST + D,\ LFT = L_j,\ LST = LFT - D,\ TF = LFT - EFT$$

| Activity | $i \to j$ | $D$ | $E_i$ | $E_j$ | $L_i$ | $L_j$ | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF | Critical? |
|----------|-----------|-----|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|-----------|
| A | 1→2 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | **0** | ✓ |
| B | 1→3 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 11 | 0 | 4 | 7 | 11 | 7 | ✗ |
| C | 2→4 | 5 | 6 | 11 | 6 | 11 | 6 | 11 | 6 | 11 | **0** | ✓ |
| D | 2→5 | 7 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 18 | 6 | 13 | 11 | 18 | 5 | ✗ |
| Dummy | 3→4 | 0 | 4 | 11 | 11 | 11 | 4 | 4 | 11 | 11 | 7 | ✗ |
| E | 4→6 | 6 | 11 | 17 | 11 | 17 | 11 | 17 | 11 | 17 | **0** | ✓ |
| F | 5→7 | 4 | 13 | 17 | 18 | 22 | 13 | 17 | 18 | 22 | 5 | ✗ |
| G | 6→8 | 5 | 17 | 22 | 17 | 22 | 17 | 22 | 17 | 22 | **0** | ✓ |
| Dummy | 7→8 | 0 | 17 | 22 | 22 | 22 | 17 | 17 | 22 | 22 | 5 | ✗ |
| H | 8→9 | 3 | 22 | 25 | 22 | 25 | 22 | 25 | 22 | 25 | **0** | ✓ |

### Step 5: Identify Critical Path

Critical Path = activities with $TF = 0$:
$$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: } A \to C \to E \to G \to H}$$

**Path Duration Verification:**
- A + C + E + G + H = 6 + 5 + 6 + 5 + 3 = **25 days** ✓
- Compare with other paths:
  - A → D → F → H = 6 + 7 + 4 + 3 = 20 days
  - B → E → G → H = 4 + 6 + 5 + 3 = 18 days (note: needs dummy for correctness)

### Step 6: Free Float Calculation (Sample for Activity B)
$$FF_B = E_3 - E_1 - D_B = 4 - 0 - 4 = 0$$
$$FF_D = E_5 - E_2 - D_D = 13 - 6 - 7 = 0$$

---

## 1.3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — PERT Numerical (KTU Board Style)

### Problem Statement:
For a research project, the following three-time estimates (in weeks) are given for activities on the critical path:

| Activity | Optimistic $a$ | Most Likely $m$ | Pessimistic $b$ |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| P | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| Q | 1 | 3 | 11 |
| R | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| S | 2 | 4 | 6 |

**Required:** Find (i) Expected duration and variance for each activity, (ii) Expected project duration $T_E$, (iii) Probability of completing in 16 weeks.

---

### (i) Expected Time and Variance per Activity

Formula:
$$t_e = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6}, \qquad \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^2$$

**Activity P:** $a = 2,\ m = 4,\ b = 6$

$$t_e^{(P)} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\ \text{weeks}$$

$$\sigma_P^2 = \left(\frac{6 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444$$

**Activity Q:** $a = 1,\ m = 3,\ b = 11$

$$t_e^{(Q)} = \frac{1 + 4(3) + 11}{6} = \frac{1 + 12 + 11}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\ \text{weeks}$$

$$\sigma_Q^2 = \left(\frac{11 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{5}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{25}{9} \approx 2.7778$$

**Activity R:** $a = 3,\ m = 5,\ b = 7$

$$t_e^{(R)} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5\ \text{weeks}$$

$$\sigma_R^2 = \left(\frac{7 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{36} = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444$$

**Activity S:** $a = 2,\ m = 4,\ b = 6$

$$t_e^{(S)} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\ \text{weeks}$$

$$\sigma_S^2 = \left(\frac{6 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.4444$$

### (ii) Expected Project Duration

$$T_E = \sum t_e = t_e^{(P)} + t_e^{(Q)} + t_e^{(R)} + t_e^{(S)} = 4 + 4 + 5 + 4 = 17\ \text{weeks}$$

### (iii) Project Variance and Standard Deviation

$$\sigma_T^2 = \sum \sigma^2 = \frac{4}{9} + \frac{25}{9} + \frac{4}{9} + \frac{4}{9} = \frac{37}{9}$$

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{\frac{37}{9}} = \frac{\sqrt{37}}{3} \approx \frac{6.0828}{3} \approx 2.0276\ \text{weeks}$$

### (iv) Probability of Completion in 16 Weeks

Given $T_s = 16$ weeks, $T_E = 17$ weeks, $\sigma_T \approx 2.0276$:

$$Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_T} = \frac{16 - 17}{2.0276} = \frac{-1}{2.0276} \approx -0.493$$

From standard normal tables:
$$P(Z \leq -0.49) \approx 0.3121 \approx 31.21\%$$

**Conclusion:** There is approximately a **31.21% probability** of completing the project within 16 weeks.

---

## 1.3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PERT vs CPM

| Parameter | CPM | PERT |
|-----------|-----|------|
| Time Estimates | Single (deterministic) | Three (probabilistic) |
| Distribution | Not assumed | Beta distribution |
| Best For | Construction, maintenance | R&D, defense projects |
| Focus | Time-Cost trade-off | Time-uncertainty trade-off |
| Critical Path | Unique, deterministic | Probabilistic (changes with $T_E$) |
| Crashing | Yes, widely used | Less common |
| Developed | 1957, DuPont | 1958, US Navy |

# 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1.4.1 CPM Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Project Scope] --> B[List Activities from WBS]
    B --> C[Determine Activity Precedence]
    C --> D[Estimate Activity Durations]
    D --> E[Draw Network Diagram AOA or AON]
    E --> F[Forward Pass - Compute EST and EFT]
    F --> G[Backward Pass - Compute LST and LFT]
    G --> H[Calculate Floats TF, FF, IF]
    H --> I{TF equals Zero?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Mark as Critical Activity]
    I -- No --> K[Non-Critical Activity]
    J --> L[Trace Critical Path from Start to End]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Project Duration TE]
    M --> N[Generate Gantt Chart for Schedule]
    N --> O[Monitor and Control Progress]
    O --> P[End]
```

## 1.4.2 PERT Three-Time Estimate Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Collect Optimistic Time a] --> D[Compute Expected Time te]
    B[Collect Most Likely Time m] --> D
    C[Collect Pessimistic Time b] --> D
    D --> E[Compute Variance sigma squared]
    E --> F[Identify Critical Path]
    F --> G[Sum te along critical path equals TE]
    G --> H[Sum sigma squared equals sigma T squared]
    H --> I[Compute Z value for scheduled time Ts]
    I --> J[Look up Probability in Z tables]
    J --> K[Decision: P greater than 0.5?]
    K -- Yes --> L[Schedule is Achievable]
    K -- No --> M[Crashing or Replanning Needed]
```

## 1.4.3 Activity Float Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Float Calculation for Each Activity] --> B[Total Float TF]
    A --> C[Free Float FF]
    A --> D[Independent Float IF]
    B --> E[TF equals LFT minus EFT]
    C --> F[FF equals Ej minus Ei minus Dij]
    D --> G[IF equals Ej minus Li minus Dij]
    E --> H{TF equals Zero?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Critical Activity on Critical Path]
    H -- No --> J[Slack Available for Activity]
    F --> K[Can Delay Without Affecting Successor EST]
    G --> L[Rarely Used Indicator of Buffer]
```

## 1.4.4 Network Notation Reference (AOA Convention)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Node i - Event Start] -- Activity A, Duration Dij --> B[Node j - Event End]
    B --> C[Activity B starts only after A completes]
    C --> D[Parallel Activities converge at Merge Event]
    D --> E[Dummy Activity - Zero Duration Logical Link]
    E --> F[Project Completion Event]
```
