> - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipe Sizing Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pipe Sizing Technique** is a systematic engineering methodology used during the detailed design phase of a process plant to determine the most economically and operationally optimum internal diameter of a pipe for a given service. It involves a deliberate trade-off between **capital expenditure (CAPEX)** — the cost of the pipe itself (which increases with diameter) — and **operating expenditure (OPEX)** — the pumping/ compression cost required to overcome frictional pressure drop (which decreases with larger diameter).

The technique simultaneously satisfies multiple process constraints:
- **Hydraulic constraints**: Pressure drop limits and maximum permissible velocity
- **Erosion/corrosion constraints**: Maximum velocity to prevent pipe material damage
- **Two-phase flow constraints**: Avoidance of slug flow, water hammer, or flashing
- **Noise and vibration constraints**: Limiting Mach number for gases and acoustic limits
- **Operability constraints**: Minimum velocity to prevent settling of solids, fouling, or gas pockets in liquids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Pipe sizing is defined as the iterative techno-economic procedure in process engineering wherein the nominal bore of a pipe is selected such that the process fluid can be transported at a velocity and pressure drop that simultaneously meet process operability requirements (no fouling, no erosion, no flashing) and minimize the sum of annualized piping cost and pumping power cost over the plant life.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water slide in a theme park**. A slide that is too narrow makes water shoot out at dangerous speeds (high velocity, high friction loss, high pump energy) and may even damage the slide surface (erosion). A slide that is too wide makes the water trickle sluggishly, requiring constant pushing by a motor (large pipe, high material cost, low velocity but poor drainage of solids). The **"perfectly sized" slide** is the one where the construction cost of the slide PLUS the electricity cost of the pump over its lifetime is the lowest — yet still moves water fast enough to clean itself.

That is exactly what pipe sizing does — it finds the "Goldilocks diameter."

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range | Standard Unit |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Optimum liquid velocity | **1.0 – 3.0 m/s** | m/s |
| Optimum gas/vapor velocity | **15 – 30 m/s** | m/s |
| Optimum steam velocity | **30 – 60 m/s** | m/s |
| Max erosional velocity (liquids) | **< 3.0 m/s** for non-corrosive | m/s |
| Max Mach number (gases) | **< 0.3** for long lines | dimensionless |
| Economic life of plant | **10 – 15 years** | years |
| Annual operating hours | **8000 – 8400 hr/yr** | hours |
| Electricity cost (typical) | **₹ 5 – 8 / kWh** | ₹/kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of **velocity** is the master variable in pipe sizing — because diameter is back-calculated from the continuity equation once velocity and flow rate are fixed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Basis of Pipe Sizing

Pipe sizing is rooted in three governing physics principles:

1. **Continuity Equation** (conservation of mass): relates velocity, area, and flow rate
2. **Mechanical Energy Balance** (Bernoulli with losses): relates pressure drop to velocity, friction, and elevation
3. **Economic Optimization**: equates marginal CAPEX saved with marginal OPEX incurred

### Step 1 — Establish the Volumetric/Mass Flow Rate
From the process flow diagram (PFD) and material balance, the designer knows:
- Volumetric flow rate $Q$ in m³/s (liquids) or Nm³/s (gases at standard conditions)
- Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ in kg/s
- Operating density $\rho$ in kg/m³

### Step 2 — Select a Trial Velocity
Velocity is selected from empirical **velocity range tables** based on:
- Service type (liquid, gas, steam, slurry, two-phase)
- Fluid properties (viscosity, density, corrosivity, solids content)
- Operating pressure (gases — avoid sonic choking)
- Material of construction (erosion limit)

### Step 3 — Calculate Trial Diameter
Using the continuity equation, derive the diameter required.

### Step 4 — Round to Nearest Standard Pipe Size
Standard pipe sizes follow ANSI B36.10 (carbon steel) or B36.19 (stainless steel) nominal bore tables (e.g., 1", 2", 3", 4", 6", 8", 10", 12", 16", 20", 24").

### Step 5 — Validate Pressure Drop
Recalculate the actual pressure drop using the Darcy-Weisbach or Hazen-Williams equation. Check against allowable ΔP (typically 0.5 – 1.0 bar per 100 m, or per plant master limit).

### Step 6 — Validate Other Constraints
Verify erosion, noise, Mach number (gases), water hammer (transient), settling (slurries), and minimum velocity (pulsating flow).

### Step 7 — Economic Optimization (Optional but Recommended in KTU)
Determine the *optimum economic diameter* by minimizing total annualized cost.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Continuity (volumetric) | $Q = v \cdot A = v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $Q$ = m³/s, $v$ = m/s, $D$ = m | Liquid flow, single phase |
| 2 | Continuity (mass) | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $\dot{m}$ = kg/s, $\rho$ = kg/m³ | General use |
| 3 | Continuity (gases) | $Q_{std} = v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_{std}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{std}}{T}$ | $Q_{std}$ = Nm³/s | Standard conditions |
| 4 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{D v}{\nu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime check |
| 5 | Darcy-Weisbach ΔP | $\Delta P_f = \dfrac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Frictional loss |
| 6 | Friction factor (laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | dimensionless | $Re < 2100$ |
| 7 | Friction factor (turbulent smooth) | $f = \dfrac{0.0791}{Re^{0.25}}$ | dimensionless | $2100 < Re < 10^5$ (Blasius) |
| 8 | Friction factor (Colebrook) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)$ | dimensionless | Turbulent, rough pipes |
| 9 | Fanning vs Darcy | $f_{Darcy} = 4 \cdot f_{Fanning}$ | dimensionless | Unit conversion |
| 10 | Equivalent length (fitting) | $L_e = K \cdot \dfrac{D}{f}$ | m | Losses for valves, bends |
| 11 | Pump power | $W_p = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\eta_{pump}}$ | W | OPEX component |
| 12 | Annual pumping cost | $C_{op} = W_p \cdot H_{op} \cdot C_e$ | ₹/yr | Energy cost |
| 13 | Capital cost of pipe | $C_{cap} = C_m \cdot L \cdot f_i$ | ₹ | Material × length |
| 14 | Optimum velocity (Peters & Timmerhaus) | $v_{opt} = C \sqrt{Q}$ | m/s | Heuristic sizing |
| 15 | Mach number (gas) | $M = \dfrac{v}{c} = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}$ | dimensionless | Compressible flow |
| 16 | Erosional velocity (API 14E) | $v_e = \dfrac{C}{\sqrt{\rho_m}}$ | m/s | Multiphase / slurry |
| 17 | Two-phase ΔP (Lockhart-Martinelli) | $\Delta P_{TP} = \Delta P_L \cdot \phi_L^2$ | Pa | Two-phase flow |
| 18 | Equivalent sand roughness | $\varepsilon$ | m | Material dependent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where the magic happens in KTU problems:** The two most tested equations are the **Continuity Equation** (to find diameter) and the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** (to verify ΔP). Always state assumptions clearly: *steady-state, isothermal, single-phase, fully developed flow.*

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pipe sizing is the **backbone of process piping design** because:
- A wrong-sized pipe causes **excessive pumping bills** (under-size) or **capital waste plus fouling** (over-size)
- In oil & gas, undersized pipes can cause **slug flow, hydrate formation, and pipeline shut-in**
- In chemical plants, undersized pipes cause **cavitation in pumps, control valve hunting, and production loss**
- In HVAC and district cooling, undersized pipes cause **high noise and occupant discomfort**
- In power plants, improperly sized steam lines cause **erosion of turbine blades and water hammer failures**

> [!NOTE]
> The annual global market for process piping exceeds **USD 50 billion**, and even a 5% reduction in pipe diameter across a mega-plant saves crores of rupees in material and structural support costs. This is why *optimum pipe sizing* is a non-negotiable deliverable in any FEED (Front End Engineering Design) package.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Optimum Pipe Diameter (Economic Basis)

The total annualized cost of a pipeline is the sum of the **annualized capital cost** of the pipe and the **annual pumping cost** required to overcome frictional losses. The optimum diameter is the one that minimizes this sum.

### Step 1: Capital Cost Model
The capital cost of the pipe is proportional to its mass, which is proportional to its surface area and wall thickness. For a fixed schedule (SDR constant), wall thickness scales linearly with diameter, so mass per unit length is proportional to $D^2$. The annualized capital cost is:

$$C_{cap,annual} = \alpha \cdot D^2$$

where $\alpha$ is a constant that captures pipe material cost (₹/m·m²), installation cost, and the capital recovery factor (CRF).

### Step 2: Pumping Power Model
From the Darcy-Weisbach equation, the pressure drop for length $L$ is:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

Using continuity $Q = v \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$, we get $v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$. Substituting:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho}{2} \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right)^2 = \frac{32 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

The hydraulic (pumping) power required is:

$$W_p = \Delta P_f \cdot Q = \frac{32 f L \rho Q^3}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

The annual operating cost is:

$$C_{op} = W_p \cdot H_{op} \cdot C_e = \frac{32 f L \rho Q^3 H_{op} C_e}{\pi^2 D^5} = \frac{\beta}{D^5}$$

where $\beta$ groups all flow and cost parameters.

### Step 3: Total Annual Cost
$$C_{total}(D) = \alpha D^2 + \frac{\beta}{D^5}$$

### Step 4: Optimize by Setting the Derivative to Zero
$$\frac{dC_{total}}{dD} = 2\alpha D - \frac{5\beta}{D^6} = 0$$

Solving for the optimum diameter $D_{opt}$:

$$2\alpha D_{opt}^7 = 5\beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_{opt}^7 = \frac{5\beta}{2\alpha} = \frac{5}{2} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha}$$

$$D_{opt} = \left(\frac{5\beta}{2\alpha}\right)^{1/7}$$

### Step 5: Result — Optimum Velocity
Substituting back, the **optimum velocity** for a given flow rate becomes:

$$v_{opt} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D_{opt}^2}$$

Through substitution, this reduces to a power-law relationship widely attributed to **Peters & Timmerhaus, Sinnott & Towler, and Coulson & Richardson**:

$$v_{opt} = C \cdot Q^{0.43}$$

where $C$ is a constant depending on fluid density, pipe material cost, and energy cost.

> [!NOTE]
> This $1/7^{th}$ power law is why the optimum diameter is **relatively insensitive** to the assumed electricity price — a 100% error in energy cost only changes $D_{opt}$ by about 13%!

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Derivation — Friction Factor from Colebrook-White

The Colebrook-White equation is the implicit industry-standard for turbulent friction factor:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)$$

**Step A:** Collect known quantities — $D$, $\rho$, $\mu$, $v$, $\varepsilon$.
**Step B:** Calculate $Re = \rho v D / \mu$.
**Step C:** Assume a starting value of $f = 0.02$ (typical for fully turbulent).
**Step D:** Plug into RHS of Colebrook; calculate new $f$.
**Step E:** Iterate (Newton-Raphson or simple substitution) until $f$ converges to within $10^{-5}$.
**Step F:** Substitute $f$ into Darcy-Weisbach to obtain $\Delta P_f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **K-grade or commercial steel** pipes, $\varepsilon \approx 4.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m. For **drawn tubing**, $\varepsilon \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m. For **cast iron**, $\varepsilon \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Constraints vs Service Type

| Service Type | Velocity Range (m/s) | Max ΔP/100 m | Special Constraint |
|--------------|---------------------|--------------|--------------------|
| Water (process) | 1.0 – 3.0 | 0.5 bar | Avoid sedimentation < 0.6 m/s |
| Steam (saturated) | 25 – 40 | 0.3 bar | Noise, water hammer |
| Steam (superheated) | 30 – 60 | 0.3 bar | Erosion limit |
| Compressed air | 10 – 20 | 0.1 bar | Moisture dropout |
| Natural gas (long line) | 5 – 15 | 1% of P per km | $M < 0.3$ |
| Vacuum lines | 30 – 60 | — | Condensate trap reqd. |
| Viscous oils ($\mu$ > 100 cP) | 0.5 – 1.5 | 1.0 bar | Re laminar check |
| Slurries (solids) | 1.5 – 3.0 | 1.0 bar | $v > v_{settling}$ |
| Two-phase (gas-liquid) | 5 – 15 | 0.5 bar | Slug flow check |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A process plant requires 50,000 kg/hr of cooling water at 30°C ($\rho$ = 995 kg/m³, $\mu$ = 0.8 cP) to be pumped over 200 m of horizontal pipe. Maximum allowable ΔP = 2 bar. Find the optimum pipe diameter.

**Step 1: Convert Units**
$$\dot{m} = 50{,}000 \text{ kg/hr} = \frac{50{,}000}{3600} = 13.89 \text{ kg/s}$$
$$\mu = 0.8 \text{ cP} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 2: Guess Velocity from Heuristic**
For water, $v_{opt} \approx 2.0$ m/s.

**Step 3: Calculate Trial Diameter**
$$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{13.89}{995} = 0.01396 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.01396}{\pi \times 2.0}} = 0.0943 \text{ m} = 94.3 \text{ mm}$$

Round up to nearest standard: **100 mm (4" NB)**, $D_{actual} = 0.1003$ m.

**Step 4: Recalculate Velocity**
$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.01396}{\pi \times (0.1003)^2} = 1.767 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Reynolds Number**
$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{995 \times 1.767 \times 0.1003}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.20 \times 10^5$$

**Step 6: Estimate Friction Factor (Blasius, smooth pipe)**
$$f = \frac{0.0791}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.0791}{(2.20 \times 10^5)^{0.25}} = 0.00456$$

**Step 7: Calculate Pressure Drop**
$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{4 \times 0.00456 \times 200}{0.1003} \times \frac{995 \times (1.767)^2}{2}$$
$$\Delta P_f = 36.36 \times 1553.6 = 56{,}485 \text{ Pa} = 0.565 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 8: Validate Constraint**
$0.565 \text{ bar} \ll 2.0 \text{ bar}$ ✓ (Within limit, with margin)
Velocity $1.77$ m/s is in the optimum range ✓

**Step 9: Check Erosion Limit**
$v < 3.0$ m/s ✓
No solids → settling not an issue.

**Conclusion:** Use **4" NB (100 mm)** standard pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always carry **5–6 significant figures** in intermediate steps and present final ΔP rounded to **2–3 significant figures**. Always show units explicitly in each step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pipe Sizing Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Obtain PFD and Material Balance] --> B[Determine Fluid Service: Liquid, Gas, Steam, Slurry, Two-Phase]
    B --> C[Establish Volumetric Flow Rate Q from Mass Flow and Density]
    C --> D[Select Service-Specific Optimum Velocity from Heuristic Table]
    D --> E[Compute Trial Diameter D trial using Continuity Equation]
    E --> F[Round Up to Nearest Standard Nominal Bore per ANSI B36.10]
    F --> G[Compute Reynolds Number Re for Actual Diameter]
    G --> H{Re Regime?}
    H -->|Laminar Re less than 2100| I[Use f equals 64 over Re]
    H -->|Turbulent| J[Use Colebrook White or Blasius Correlation]
    I --> K[Compute Actual Pressure Drop using Darcy Weisbach]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Delta P within Allowable Limit?}
    L -->|No Increase D| E
    L -->|Yes| M{Check Erosion Limit v less than 3 m/s for Liquids}
    M -->|No Increase D| E
    M -->|Yes| N{Check Settling Limit v greater than 0.6 m/s for Liquids}
    N -->|No Decrease D| E
    N -->|Yes| O{Check Mach Number M less than 0.3 for Gases}
    O -->|No Increase D| E
    O -->|Yes| P[Check Two Phase and Water Hammer if Applicable]
    P --> Q[Finalize Pipe Size Schedule and Material Spec]
    Q --> R[End Document in Line List and P and ID]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cost Trade-off in Pipe Sizing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cost1 [Cost Components]
        A1[CAPEX: Pipe Material plus Installation] --> SUM[Total Annualized Cost]
        A2[OPEX: Pumping Power over Plant Life] --> SUM
    end
    SUM --> OPT[Optimum Diameter Dopt]
    OPT --> USAGE[Use in Line Class and Piping Spec]
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style A2 fill:#B0E0E6
    style SUM fill:#98FB98
    style OPT fill:#FFD700
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Pipe Sizing Constraints Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pipe Sizing Input: Q, rho, mu, L, epsilon] --> C1[Constraint 1: Allowable Pressure Drop]
    P --> C2[Constraint 2: Maximum Erosion Velocity]
    P --> C3[Constraint 3: Minimum Settling Velocity]
    P --> C4[Constraint 4: Maximum Mach Number for Gases]
    P --> C5[Constraint 5: Water Hammer and Surge]
    P --> C6[Constraint 6: Noise Limit 85 dBA at 1 m]
    C1 --> W[Governing Diameter equals MAX of all constraints]
    C2 --> W
    C3 --> W
    C4 --> W
    C5 --> W
    C6 --> W
    W --> OUT[Selected Standard Pipe Size]
    style W fill:#FF6347
    style OUT fill:#90EE90
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams intentionally show the **logic flow** of pipe sizing decisions rather than physical pipe geometry, which is more appropriate for KTU's process-engineering orientation. The *governing diameter* is the **largest** diameter that satisfies all constraints simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define the term "Optimum pipe diameter" with respect to process plant design. State two criteria used in its selection.**

**Model Answer:**
The optimum pipe diameter is the internal diameter of a pipe at which the sum of the annualized capital cost of the pipe (including material, fabrication, and installation) and the annual pumping cost required to overcome frictional pressure drop is at its minimum, while still satisfying all process operability constraints.

Two selection criteria:
1. **Economic criterion:** Annualized CAPEX + OPEX is minimum
2. **Hydraulic criterion:** Pressure drop ≤ allowable ΔP and velocity lies within service-specific optimum range

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark tip:** Always mention *both* an economic criterion AND a process/operability criterion for full marks.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four recommended velocity ranges for the following process services: (i) process water, (ii) saturated steam, (iii) compressed air, (iv) viscous oil.**

**Model Answer:**

| Service | Recommended Velocity |
|---------|----------------------|
| (i) Process water | 1.0 – 3.0 m/s |
| (ii) Saturated steam | 25 – 40 m/s |
| (iii) Compressed air | 10 – 20 m/s |
| (iv) Viscous oil | 0.5 – 1.5 m/s |

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark tip:** Tabulate. Examiners reward presentation.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part B question in a module offers an internal choice between two questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**A process stream of benzene (ρ = 870 kg/m³, μ = 0.65 cP) flows at a rate of 15,000 kg/hr through a horizontal pipe of length 120 m. The allowable pressure drop is 1.5 bar. The pipe material is commercial steel (ε = 4.6 × 10⁻⁵ m).**

**(a) Calculate the optimum pipe diameter and select a standard nominal bore. (7 marks)**
**(b) Verify the design by computing the actual pressure drop, Reynolds number, friction factor, and erosion velocity. Comment on the suitability of your choice. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Optimum Diameter**

Step 1: Convert units
$$\dot{m} = \frac{15{,}000}{3600} = 4.167 \text{ kg/s}$$
$$\mu = 0.65 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$

Step 2: Volumetric flow rate
$$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{4.167}{870} = 4.79 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 3: Select trial velocity (benzene, non-corrosive hydrocarbon)
$$v_{opt} = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 4: Calculate trial diameter
$$D_{trial} = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v_{opt}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 4.79 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 1.5}} = 0.0638 \text{ m} = 63.8 \text{ mm}$$

Step 5: Round up to standard NB. The next standard is **DN 65 (2½")** with $D_{actual} \approx 0.065$ m. However, checking 2" NB (60.3 mm) would under-size. Hence **choose 80 mm (3") NB** for margin, with $D_{actual} = 0.0779$ m.

**Part (b) — Verification**

Step 6: Actual velocity
$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 4.79 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (0.0779)^2} = 1.005 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 7: Reynolds Number
$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{870 \times 1.005 \times 0.0779}{0.65 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.05 \times 10^5$$

Step 8: Relative roughness
$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{4.6 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0779} = 5.9 \times 10^{-4}$$

Step 9: Colebrook-White friction factor
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{5.9 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.05 \times 10^5 \sqrt{f}}\right)$$

Iterating: $f \approx 0.0208$.

Step 10: Pressure Drop
$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{4 \times 0.0208 \times 120}{0.0779} \times \frac{870 \times (1.005)^2}{2}$$
$$\Delta P_f = 128.1 \times 439.4 = 56{,}302 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.563 \text{ bar}$$

Step 11: Validation
- $\Delta P = 0.563 \text{ bar} < 1.5 \text{ bar}$ ✓
- $v = 1.005 \text{ m/s}$ (in 1.0–3.0 m/s range) ✓
- $v < 3.0$ m/s erosion limit ✓
- $v > 0.6$ m/s settling limit ✓

**Conclusion:** **3" NB (80 mm) standard pipe is suitable** with significant pressure drop margin, indicating the line could even tolerate additional fittings or future throughput increase.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Derive the relationship for the optimum economic pipe diameter, showing that it follows a 1/7th power law. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**
**(b) A liquid hydrocarbon (ρ = 750 kg/m³, μ = 2.0 cP) is to be pumped at 200 m³/hr through 500 m of pipe. Electricity costs ₹ 7/kWh and the plant operates 8,000 hr/yr. The pipe cost is ₹ 15,000 per kg of pipe mass. Carbon steel pipe has $\varepsilon = 4.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m. Calculate the optimum economic diameter. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation**

Assumptions: Steady-state, isothermal, single-phase, fully turbulent flow, horizontal pipe, electricity cost constant, pipe cost proportional to D².

Total annualized cost:
$$C_T = C_{cap} + C_{op} = \alpha D^2 + \frac{\beta}{D^5}$$

Derivation as shown in Section 3.1 above, leading to:
$$D_{opt} = \left(\frac{5\beta}{2\alpha}\right)^{1/7}$$

This confirms the **1/7th power law** — i.e., optimum diameter is proportional to $\left(\frac{\beta}{\alpha}\right)^{1/7}$. The optimum velocity similarly follows a 0.43 power law: $v_{opt} = C \cdot Q^{0.43}$.

**Part (b) — Calculation**

Step 1: Convert
$$Q = \frac{200}{3600} = 0.0556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\mu = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$

Step 2: Empirical constant
For liquid hydrocarbons, $C \approx 1.5$ (S.I., conservative). Thus:
$$v_{opt} \approx 1.5 \times (0.0556)^{0.43} = 1.5 \times 0.306 = 0.459 \text{ m/s}$$

This is **too low** (would cause settling and laminar flow). A more practical velocity is $v \approx 1.2$ m/s.

Step 3: Diameter from economic model
Using the empirical formula with cost parameters:
$$D_{opt} = \left[\frac{0.36 \rho Q^2}{\Delta P_{allowed}}\right]^{0.2} \cdot L^{0.2}$$

Assume $\Delta P_{allowed} = 0.5$ bar per 100 m → total = 2.5 bar = 250,000 Pa.
$$D_{opt} = \left[\frac{0.36 \times 750 \times (0.0556)^2}{250{,}000}\right]^{0.2} \times (500)^{0.2}$$
$$= \left[\frac{0.36 \times 750 \times 3.09 \times 10^{-3}}{250{,}000}\right]^{0.2} \times 3.465$$
$$= \left[3.34 \times 10^{-6}\right]^{0.2} \times 3.465 = 0.20 \times 3.465 = 0.0693 \text{ m}$$

Step 4: Round up to nearest standard → **3" NB (80 mm)**

Step 5: Verify economics
Re-calculate with $D = 0.0779$ m:
$$v = \frac{0.0556 \times 4}{\pi (0.0779)^2} = 11.66 \text{ m/s}$$

This is **excessively high** (gaseous range) — indicates our velocity assumption was too low. This implies **use a larger pipe, e.g., 6" NB (150 mm)**:
$$v = \frac{0.0556 \times 4}{\pi (0.150)^2} = 3.15 \text{ m/s}$$

Slightly high — recheck with **DN 200 (8" NB)**:
$$v = \frac{0.0556 \times 4}{\pi (0.200)^2} = 1.77 \text{ m/s} \checkmark$$

**Final answer: 8" NB (200 mm) standard pipe** is the economically optimum choice with reasonable velocity and acceptable ΔP.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — mixing mm and m, or cP and Pa·s. Always convert to SI first.
> 2. **Friction factor confusion** — using Fanning $f$ as if it were Darcy $f$ (4× error!).
> 3. **Forgetting standard pipe rounding** — examiners expect a standard NB to be selected.
> 4. **Ignoring erosion velocity** — the design may pass ΔP check but fail erosion check.
> 5. **No economic check** — Part (a) derivations that skip the "annualized cost" step lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Blank assumptions box** — always state: steady state, isothermal, single-phase, fully developed, horizontal.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Pipe sizing** is a *techno-economic* activity — never purely economic, never purely hydraulic
- ✅ The **master variable** is velocity; diameter is back-calculated
- ✅ Always start with **service-specific velocity range** from heuristic tables
- ✅ Use the **continuity equation** to find trial diameter
- ✅ **Round UP** to nearest standard NB, never down
- ✅ Verify using **Darcy-Weisbach** equation with **Colebrook-White** friction factor
- ✅ Check **six constraints**: ΔP, erosion velocity, settling velocity, Mach number, water hammer, noise
- ✅ **Optimum economic diameter** follows a **1/7th power law** — relatively insensitive to cost assumptions
- ✅ The governing diameter is the **MAXIMUM** of all constraint-driven diameters
- ✅ Standard pipe tables: **ANSI B36.10 (CS) and B36.19 (SS)**
- ✅ For **two-phase flow**, use the **Lockhart-Martinelli** correlation
- ✅ For **gases**, never let $M > 0.3$ in long lines to avoid choking and noise
- ✅ For **liquids**, never let $v < 0.6$ m/s (sedimentation) or $v > 3.0$ m/s (erosion)
- ✅ Final deliverable: update the **P&ID, Line List, and Piping Material Specification (PMS)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Day Mantra for Pipe Sizing:**
> *"Know Q, choose v, find D from continuity, round to standard, verify ΔP, check ALL constraints."*
> Memorize: **Darcy-Weisbach**, **Continuity**, **Reynolds Number**, **Colebrook-White** — these four alone solve 80% of KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Codes and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Codes and Standards ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Code (Engineering Context):** A *code* is a systematically arranged body of rules, specifications, or procedures formally adopted by an authority (governmental, professional, or industrial body) that governs the design, construction, fabrication, inspection, testing, and maintenance of engineering systems. Codes are **mandatory** in nature when referenced in a contract or regulation, and violation can result in legal, safety, or commercial consequences. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard:** A *standard* is a consensus-based technical document, established by a recognized standards body, that prescribes the approved approach, methodology, dimensional criteria, material specifications, or test protocols for a given engineering activity. Standards are typically **voluntary** in nature but become **legally binding** when cited by a code, regulation, or contract. > [!NOTE] > **Regulation:** Mandatory legal requirements enacted by a governmental or statutory authority (e.g., PSSR, OSHA, EPA, CEA, OISD). Regulations override both codes and standards and are non-negotiable. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-world analogy: Building a house 🏠** > > - Imagine you and your friends decide to build a treehouse in your backyard. There is **no authority** involved — you just use "common sense" and your own experience. → This is a **Company/Proprietary Standard** (in-house practice). > - Now, your school says: *"Anyone building a treehouse must follow these specific rules — proper joints, load-bearing beams, safe height limits."* This is a **Standard** set by an educational/consensus body. > - The Municipal Corporation then passes a law: *"No treehouse may exceed 10 ft without a permit and certified engineer inspection."* This is a **Code** (mandatory rule). > - The Supreme Court issues: *"No construction is permitted on protected forest land."* This is a **Regulation** (legal mandate with statutory power). > > So, in an engineering project, **Regulations > Codes > Standards > Company Practices** in the order of legal hierarchy. ### 1.3 Key Standardization Bodies (Domain Map) | Tier | Body Type | Examples | Scope | |------|-----------|----------|-------| | **Global** | International Standards | **ISO**, **IEC**, ITU | Worldwide consensus | | **Regional** | Continental Bodies | CEN (EU), PASC (Asia-Pacific) | Regional harmonization | | **National** | Country-Level Standards | **BIS (India)**, ANSI (USA), BSI (UK), DIN (Germany), JIS (Japan) | Country-specific mandatory/voluntary | | **Industry** | Sectoral Professional Bodies | **ASME, ASTM, API, TEMA, NFPA, NACE, IEEE, AWS, ISA** | Process, mechanical, electrical, instrumentation | | **Statutory** | Government Regulations | OSHA, EPA, CEA, PSSR, OISD, MoEFCC, PESO, CCOE | Legal enforcement | | **In-house** | Corporate Specifications | Shell DEP, Bechtel, EIL, L&T | Proprietary best practices | > [!NOTE] > In the context of **Chemical Process Plant Project Engineering (PECHT524)**, the most commonly applied codes & standards are from **ASME** (mechanical/vessels/piping), **API** (tanks/process equipment), **TEMA** (heat exchangers), **NFPA** (fire safety), **IS/BIS** (Indian practice), **OISD** (oil & gas safety), and **PESO/CCOE** (statutory for explosives/pressurized systems). ### 1.4 Why Codes & Standards are Essential in Project Engineering - Ensure **Safety** of personnel, public, and environment. - Provide **interoperability** of equipment & components across global vendors. - Enable **legal compliance** and avoid penalties/project shutdowns. - Reduce **engineering rework** by providing tested design procedures. - Facilitate **procurement** with clear material/specification definitions. - Support **insurance, financing, and third-party inspection** approvals. - Allow **cross-border execution** of projects under one harmonized framework. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix ### 2.1 Hierarchical Pyramid of Engineering Rules The relationship between Regulations, Codes, Standards, and Specifications follows a strict legal hierarchy that every KTU student **must internalize** for both project engineering practice and viva questions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchy (Top → Bottom = Strongest → Weakest):** > 1. **Regulations** (Statutory Laws) — Non-negotiable > 2. **Codes** (Mandatory when adopted) — Enforced by law/contract > 3. **Standards** (Voluntary unless cited) — Consensus best practice > 4. **Company / Project Specifications** (Proprietary) — Internal rules ### 2.2 The "Code" and "Standard" Distinction (Exam Critical) | Attribute | **Code** | **Standard** | |-----------|----------|--------------| | **Legal Status** | Mandatory (when adopted) | Voluntary (unless cited) | | **Issuing Body** | Government, statutory body, or professional society with statutory recognition | Consensus standards body (ISO, BIS, ASME, etc.) | | **Content** | What **MUST** be done (rule) | What **SHOULD** be done (guideline/method) | | **Example** | ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, IS 2825 (Unfired Pressure Vessels), National Building Code of India | ISO 9001 (QMS), ASTM A106 (Pipe material), ISO 45001 (OH&S) | | **Enforcement** | Legal + Contractual | Contractual only | | **Format** | Often rule-based with "shall" language | Often performance- or test-based | ### 2.3 Application Across the Project Life-Cycle (PCECC Stages) Codes and standards are applied at **every** phase of a chemical/process plant project: | Project Phase | Typical Codes/Standards Applied | |---------------|--------------------------------| | **Concept / FEED** | ASME B31.3, API 650, TEMA, OISD-118, IS-2825 | | **Detailed Engineering** | ASME Sec VIII Div 1, ASME B16.5, IS-2062, ASTM standards | | **Procurement** | ASTM/EN material standards, vendor data sheets, IS specifications | | **Construction / Fabrication** | ASME Sec IX (Welding), AWS D1.1, IS 816, ASME B31.3 | | **Inspection / Testing** | ASME Sec V (NDE), API 510, ASME PCC-2 | | **Commissioning** | OISD-145, API RP 1110, PSSR, NFPA, ATEX directives | | **Operation & Maintenance** | API 570 (Piping), API 653 (Tanks), OSHA / Factory Act | ### 2.4 Major Codes — Chemical & Process Plant Perspective > [!NOTE] > The following table is **mandatory reference** for KTU Board Exam answers on Codes & Standards. Memorize at least 4-5 for full marks. | Code / Standard | Issuing Body | Application Area | |-----------------|--------------|------------------| | **ASME Sec I** | ASME | Power Boilers | | **ASME Sec II** | ASME | Material Specifications (Ferrous + Non-Ferrous) | | **ASME Sec V** | ASME | Non-Destructive Examination | | **ASME Sec VIII Div 1/2** | ASME | Unfired Pressure Vessels | | **ASME Sec IX** | ASME | Welding & Brazing Qualifications | | **ASME B31.1** | ASME | Power Piping | | **ASME B31.3** | ASME | Process Piping (most used in chemical plants) | | **ASME B16.5 / B16.34** | ASME | Pipe Flanges / Valves | | **API 5L** | API | Line Pipe Specification | | **API 510** | API | Pressure Vessel Inspection | | **API 570** | API | Piping Inspection Code | | **API 620 / 650** | API | Atmospheric & Low-Pressure Storage Tanks | | **API 653** | API | Tank Inspection, Repair, Alteration, Reconstruction | | **TEMA (C, B, R)** | Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Assoc. | Shell & Tube Heat Exchangers | | **NFPA 30** | NFPA | Flammable & Combustible Liquids Code | | **NFPA 13 / 15** | NFPA | Fire Sprinkler / Water Spray | | **IS 2825** | BIS | Unfired Pressure Vessels (India) | | **IS 800** | BIS | Steel Structures (General Construction) | | **OISD-118 / 138 / 145** | OISD | Oil Industry Safety (Layouts/Piping/Operations) | | **PSSR (UK)** | Statutory | Pressure Systems Safety Regulations | | **PESO / CCOE** | Statutory (India) | Explosives / Pressurized / Hazardous storage | | **ATEX (EU 94/9/EC)** | Statutory (EU) | Equipment for Explosive Atmospheres | | **PED (2014/68/EU)** | Statutory (EU) | Pressure Equipment Directive | | **OSHA 29 CFR 1910** | Statutory (USA) | Occupational Safety & Health | ### 2.5 Concept of "Code of Construction" vs "Code of Inspection" - **Code of Construction** → Governs **how** a component is designed and fabricated (e.g., ASME Sec VIII Div 1). - **Code of Inspection** → Governs **how** a component is inspected, tested, and maintained in service (e.g., API 510, API 570, API 653). - A typical **KTU answer** must distinguish these two, especially for the topic of Codes in pressure equipment. ### 2.6 Standardization Mark / Certification Symbols (Visual Recognition) A KTU examiner may test students on the **symbols** of major bodies. Memorize: - **ASME** → Hexagonal stamp with the letter "**U**, **U2**, **U3**, **S**, **NB**, **R**" (e.g., "U" stamp = ASME Sec VIII Div 1 pressure vessel). - **API** → Monogram "**API**" with specification number. - **BIS** → "**ISI**" mark in India (now replaced by "**BIS Standard Mark**"). - **CE** → European Conformity mark (mandatory in EU/EEA). - **PESO** → Indian statutory mark for pressurized systems & explosives. > [!TIP] > The **ASME "U" Stamp** is the *single most frequently asked symbol* in board exams — remember it stands for **Unfired Pressure Vessels built to ASME Sec VIII Div 1**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Selecting the Right Code for a Project Since "Codes and Standards" is a **qualitative, framework-based** topic (not a numerical derivation), KTU expects students to demonstrate a **structured decision-making process** when explaining code selection. Below is the **exhaustive analytical framework** that students should reproduce in Part B answers. ### 3.1 The 5-Step Code Selection Logic > [!NOTE] > A project engineer does **not** "pick" a code arbitrarily. Selection follows a deterministic 5-step logic. KTU examiners award 7 marks per sub-question where this logic is clearly enumerated. **Step 1 — Identify the Statutory Jurisdiction** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Country of Project} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Mandatory Local Regulations} \\ \text{Example: India (PSSR analogue) } \rightarrow \text{ Factory Act 1948, OISD, IS, PESO} \end{aligned} $$ The country of execution **always dictates the top-level regulation**. No voluntary code can override a government regulation. **Step 2 — Identify the Industry & Equipment Class** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Industry} &= \text{Oil \& Gas, Petrochemical, Refinery, Pharma, Power, etc.} \\ \text{Equipment} &= \text{Pressure Vessel, Storage Tank, Piping, Heat Exchanger, Column, Reactor} \end{aligned} $$ Once equipment class is fixed, **industry-specific codes** (e.g., API, ASME) are applied. **Step 3 — Map the Equipment to Its Applicable Construction Code** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Equipment} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Construction Code} \\ \text{Pressure Vessel} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASME Sec VIII Div 1 / Div 2, IS 2825} \\ \text{Process Piping} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASME B31.3} \\ \text{Storage Tank (Atm)} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{API 650} \\ \text{Storage Tank (Low-P)} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{API 620} \\ \text{Heat Exchanger (S\&T)} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{TEMA (Class C/B/R) + ASME Sec VIII} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Cross-Reference Supporting Standards** After the parent code is selected, supporting standards for **materials, welding, NDE, testing, fabrication, and inspection** are identified: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Material Standard} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASTM A106 (CS pipe), A240 (SS), A516 (CS plate)} \\ \text{Welding Standard} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASME Sec IX, AWS D1.1} \\ \text{NDE Standard} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASME Sec V} \\ \text{Inspection Standard} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{API 510 (Vessels), API 570 (Piping)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Apply In-house / Client Project Specifications** Finally, **client-specific or company-specific** specifications (e.g., Shell DEP, EIL, PDIL, L&T proprietary specs) override the general code **only if they are more stringent** (a universally accepted principle in EPC contracts). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Final Applicable Document Stack (Top to Bottom):} \\ \text{1. Law/Regulation (e.g., OISD, PESO, Factory Act)} \\ \text{2. Client / Company Spec (if more stringent)} \\ \text{3. Industry Code (e.g., ASME Sec VIII, API 650)} \\ \text{4. Supporting Standard (e.g., ASTM, TEMA)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Worked Example: Design of an Unfired Pressure Vessel in an Indian Refinery > [!NOTE] > This is a **classic KTU long-answer** (14 marks) — practice writing this exact framework. **Problem Statement (hypothetical exam):** *"A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel (ID = 2500 mm, design pressure = 2.5 MPa, design temp = 200 °C) is to be installed in an Indian refinery handling hydrocarbons. List and justify the applicable codes and standards."* **Solution Framework:** $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Step 1: Statutory Layer (India)} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{Factories Act 1948, Indian Boiler Regulations (if applicable), OISD-118, PESO (if hydrocarbon)} \\ \\ \textbf{Step 2: Industry} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{Refinery, hydrocarbons → OISD-118 (Layout), OISD-138 (Piping), IS standards} \\ \\ \textbf{Step 3: Construction Code for Vessel} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASME Sec VIII Div 1 (most widely used) OR IS 2825 (Indian alternative)} \\ \\ \textbf{Step 4: Supporting Standards} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{ASTM A516 Gr 70 (CS plate), ASME Sec II Pt A/B (matl),} \\ &\quad\;\text{ASME Sec IX (welding), ASME Sec V (NDE), ASME B16.5 (flanges)} \\ \\ \textbf{Step 5: Inspection / Maintenance Code} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{API 510 (in-service inspection)} \\ \\ \textbf{Step 6: Project / Client Spec} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{Client's own spec (e.g., Indian Oil, BPCL, HPCL) overlaid.} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Why Harmonization Matters in Global EPC Projects In a modern EPC project, equipment may be: - **Designed** in India (IS/ASME). - **Fabricated** in Korea (KS / KEPIC). - **Inspected** by a German TPI (TÜV / AD 2000 Merkblätter). - **Installed** in the Middle East (client uses Saudi Aramco Standards, SAES). - **Operated** under local regulation (e.g., OSHA, QAF, OSHA-Sudan). Hence the project specification **must explicitly state the hierarchy** of code reconciliation — typically a clause stating: *"In case of conflict, the most stringent requirement shall govern."* > [!TIP] > This is often the **last sub-part** of a 14-mark question: *"What happens if two codes conflict?"* — Answer: *The most stringent provision applies unless the project specification explicitly defines a hierarchy.* --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Engineering Rules (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid graph TD node1["GOVERNMENT REGULATIONS
Statutory - Highest Authority"] node2["INDUSTRY CODES
ASME - API - TEMA - NFPA"] node3["NATIONAL / INTERNATIONAL STANDARDS
ISO - BIS - ASTM - EN"] node4["CORPORATE SPECIFICATIONS
Shell DEP - EIL - Bechtel - L&T"] node5["PROJECT EXECUTION
Design - Procurement - Construction"] node6["Example Factory Act 1948 PESO CCOE OISD"] node7["Example ASME Sec VIII API 650 TEMA"] node8["Example ISO 9001 ASTM A106 IS 2825"] node9["Example Indian Oil Spec BPCL Spec"] node10["Equipment Vessels Piping Heat Exchangers"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node1 -.-> node6 node2 -.-> node7 node3 -.-> node8 node4 -.-> node9 node5 -.-> node10 ``` ### 4.2 Code & Standard Selection Workflow (Mermaid Process Map) ```mermaid graph TD start["Start Project Equipment Identification"] s1["STEP 1 Identify Statutory Jurisdiction Country of Execution"] s2["STEP 2 Identify Industry Refinery Petrochemical Power Pharma"] s3["STEP 3 Identify Equipment Type Vessel Pipe Tank Exchanger"] s4["STEP 4 Select Construction Code ASME API TEMA IS"] s5["STEP 5 Select Supporting Standards Material Welding NDE Testing"] s6["STEP 6 Overlay Client and Company Specifications"] s7["STEP 7 Resolve Code Conflicts Apply Most Stringent Rule"] s8["FINAL CODE STACK APPROVED FOR ENGINEERING"] start --> s1 s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6 s6 --> s7 s7 --> s8 ``` ### 4.3 Code Coverage by Project Phase (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR p1["CONCEPT FEED"] p2["DETAILED ENGINEERING"] p3["PROCUREMENT"] p4["CONSTRUCTION"] p5["COMMISSIONING"] p6["OPERATION MAINTENANCE"] c1["ASME B31-3 API 650 TEMA"] c2["ASME Sec VIII Sec IX Sec V"] c3["ASTM Material Standards IS EN"] c4["ASME B31-3 AWS D1-1 Sec IX"] c5["OISD 145 API RP 1110 NFPA"] c6["API 510 API 570 API 653 OSHA"] p1 --> c1 p2 --> c2 p3 --> c3 p4 --> c4 p5 --> c5 p6 --> c6 ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture of an Engineering Code (Mermaid Concept Map) ```mermaid graph TD A["ENGINEERING CODE EXAMPLE ASME SEC VIII"] B["MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS Sec II"] C["DESIGN RULES Section DIV 1"] D["FABRICATION RULES Sec IX Welding"] E["INSPECTION RULES Sec V NDE"] F["STAMP AND CERTIFICATION U Stamp"] G["IN-SERVICE INSPECTION API 510"] A --> B A --> C A --> D A --> E A --> F A --> G ``` > [!TIP] > This block diagram is a **memory aid** for viva. It shows that a code like ASME Sec VIII is **not just a single document** but a **family of sections** covering material, design, fabrication, and inspection. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** > > **Q1. Distinguish between a Code and a Standard with one example each.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Aspect | Code | Standard | > |--------|------|----------| > | **Nature** | Mandatory rule | Voluntary guideline | > | **Enforced by** | Law / contract | Consensus body | > | **Example** | ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code | ASTM A106 (material spec) | > > *(Full 3 marks require both definition + example.)* > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > > **Q2. What is the significance of the ASME "U" Stamp?** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The ASME "U" Stamp is a certification mark issued by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers to manufacturers of **unfired pressure vessels built in accordance with ASME Section VIII, Division 1**. It certifies that the vessel has been **designed, fabricated, inspected, and tested** in full compliance with the code. It is **mandatory** for pressure vessels installed in jurisdictions where ASME codes are adopted by regulation or contract. Other ASME stamps include "S" (power boilers), "A" (assembly), "NB" (nuclear), and "R" (repairs and alterations). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 4]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the hierarchical relationship between Regulations, Codes, and Standards in a chemical process plant project. Provide at least two examples for each tier from Indian practice. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** For a refinery project in India involving (i) an atmospheric storage tank for crude oil, (ii) a shell-and-tube heat exchanger, and (iii) a process piping network, identify the primary construction code and one supporting standard for each. #### Model Solution — Question A **Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Definition of Hierarchy:** Engineering rules in any project follow a **four-tier hierarchy**: $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Tier 1} &\;:\; \text{Statutory Regulations (Government Laws - Highest Authority)} \\ \textbf{Tier 2} &\;:\; \text{Codes (Mandatory Rules - Adopted by law/contract)} \\ \textbf{Tier 3} &\;:\; \text{Standards (Voluntary Consensus Documents)} \\ \textbf{Tier 4} &\;:\; \text{Company / Project Specifications (Proprietary)} \end{aligned} $$ **Indian Examples:** | Tier | Indian Examples | |------|-----------------| | **Regulation** | Factories Act 1948, Indian Electricity Act, OISD Standards, PESO/CCOE | | **Code** | IS 2825 (Unfired Pressure Vessels), National Building Code, ASME Sec VIII (adopted in many Indian EPC contracts) | | **Standard** | IS 2062 (Structural Steel), ASTM A106, ISO 9001 | | **Company Spec** | Indian Oil Corp Spec, BPCL Spec, EIL Spec | **Significance:** - Lower-tier documents **cannot override** higher-tier rules. - In case of conflict, the **most stringent requirement** applies. - This hierarchy is the **legal and contractual backbone** of every EPC contract. > *(Award 7 marks: 2 for hierarchy, 2 for definitions, 3 for Indian examples.)* **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** | Equipment | Primary Construction Code | Supporting Standard | |-----------|---------------------------|---------------------| | **(i) Atmospheric Storage Tank (Crude Oil)** | **API 650** (Welded Tanks for Oil Storage) | **IS 803** (Indian alternative) or **API 620** if low pressure | | **(ii) Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger** | **TEMA** (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) + **ASME Sec VIII Div 1** for pressure shell | **ASME Sec II** (Material), **ASME Sec IX** (Welding) | | **(iii) Process Piping Network** | **ASME B31.3** (Process Piping) | **ASME B16.5** (Flanges), **ASTM A106** (Pipe material) | **Justification (1 line each):** - API 650 → most widely used for **atmospheric & low-pressure** welded storage tanks in oil & gas. - TEMA → defines **mechanical design, fabrication tolerances, and thermal rating** of S&T exchangers. - ASME B31.3 → the **default** process piping code for **refineries, petrochemical, and chemical plants**. > *(Award 7 marks: 1 mark for identifying each code, 1 for each supporting standard, and 1 for overall justification.)* --- > **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** "Codes and Standards are the backbone of safe and legally compliant project execution." Justify the statement with reference to ASME and API codes, citing the role of certification marks. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the procedure for selecting an appropriate construction code for an equipment using a structured 5-step framework. Illustrate with the example of a pressure vessel. #### Model Solution — Question B **Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** **Justification:** - Codes like **ASME** (Section VIII for Pressure Vessels, B31.3 for Process Piping) and **API** (650 for Storage Tanks, 510/570 for Inspection) are developed by experts over decades and are **legally defensible** in any dispute. - **Certification marks** (e.g., ASME "U" stamp, API monogram) certify manufacturer compliance and are essential for **insurance, third-party inspection, and statutory approvals**. - A vessel with the **ASME "U" stamp** is automatically accepted globally, eliminating the need for re-certification — this **de-risks** international EPC projects. > *(Award 7 marks: 3 for justification, 2 for ASME + API examples, 2 for certification marks.)* **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** **5-Step Code Selection Framework** (same as Section 3.1): $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Step 1} &\;:\; \text{Identify statutory jurisdiction (e.g., India - OISD, PESO, IS)} \\ \textbf{Step 2} &\;:\; \text{Identify industry & equipment (e.g., Refinery, Pressure Vessel)} \\ \textbf{Step 3} &\;:\; \text{Map to construction code (e.g., ASME Sec VIII Div 1)} \\ \textbf{Step 4} &\;:\; \text{Cross-reference supporting standards (Material, Welding, NDE)} \\ \textbf{Step 5} &\;:\; \text{Apply company/client specifications overlay} \end{aligned} $$ **Pressure Vessel Example:** - **Step 1** → Indian regulation: OISD-118 (Layouts) + Factory Act. - **Step 2** → Refinery, hydrocarbon service, unfired pressure vessel. - **Step 3** → **ASME Sec VIII Div 1** (or **IS 2825** in India). - **Step 4** → ASTM A516 plate, ASME Sec IX welding, ASME Sec V NDE, ASME B16.5 flanges. - **Step 5** → Apply IOCL / BPCL client specification overlay. > *(Award 7 marks: 3 marks for framework, 4 marks for application to the pressure vessel example.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "Code" with "Standard":** Many students use these terms interchangeably. Always state the **legal status** (mandatory vs voluntary) and provide an **example** for each. > 2. **Forgetting the U Stamp:** When asked "give an example of a certification mark," the **ASME U-Stamp** is the *expected answer*. Saying "ISO certification" is wrong — ISO certifies **systems**, not individual **vessels**. > 3. **Ignoring the Hierarchy:** When asked about regulations vs codes, students often list them in a flat manner. Always emphasize the **pyramid/hierarchy** and the **"most stringent rule applies"** principle. > 4. **No India-specific reference:** KTU is an **Indian university** — always include at least one **Indian reference** (IS, BIS, OISD, PESO, Factory Act) in your answer. > 5. **Missing supporting standards:** When a question asks "which code?" — also mention the **supporting standards** (material, welding, NDE). Examiners expect the full document stack, not just the headline code. > 6. **Memorization-only answers:** Just listing codes without **explaining their applicability** scores only 2-3 out of 7. Always **justify** why a particular code applies. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Codes & Standards (Module 4)** - ☐ **Definitions**: Code = Mandatory rule, Standard = Voluntary guideline, Regulation = Statutory law. - ☐ **Hierarchy**: **Regulations > Codes > Standards > Company Specs** (always). - ☐ **Conflict Rule**: When two codes conflict, **the more stringent provision applies** (unless project spec defines otherwise). - ☐ **ASME U-Stamp**: For **Unfired Pressure Vessels** built to ASME Sec VIII Div 1. - ☐ **ASME Sections** to remember: Sec II (Materials), Sec V (NDE), Sec VIII (Vessels), Sec IX (Welding), B31.3 (Process Piping). - ☐ **API Standards** to remember: API 5L (Line pipe), 510 (Vessel inspection), 570 (Piping inspection), 620/650 (Storage tanks), 653 (Tank repair). - ☐ **TEMA** = Standard for **Shell & Tube Heat Exchangers** (Classes C, B, R). - ☐ **Indian Bodies**: BIS (Standards), OISD (Oil Industry Safety), PESO/CCOE (Statutory). - ☐ **IS 2825** = Indian code for unfired pressure vessels (alternative to ASME Sec VIII). - ☐ **OISD-118** = Layouts, OISD-138 = Piping, OISD-145 = Operations (memorize at least 2-3). - ☐ **NFPA 30** = Flammable & Combustible Liquids Code (most cited fire safety code). - ☐ **PED (2014/68/EU)** = European Pressure Equipment Directive; **ATEX** = Equipment for Explosive Atmospheres (EU). - ☐ **Project Phase Mapping**: Codes apply at *all* phases — Concept → FEED → Detailed → Procurement → Construction → Commissioning → O&M. - ☐ **Always include an Indian reference** (IS/BIS/OISD) — examiners reward it. - ☐ **Construction Code vs Inspection Code** — know the difference (e.g., ASME VIII is *construction*; API 510 is *in-service inspection*). - ☐ **Codes & Standards enable**: Safety, Interoperability, Legal Compliance, Insurance Approval, Cross-border EPC execution. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Code Categories:** > **"MAPS-T"** → **M**echanical (ASME), **A**PI (Tanks/Piping), **P**iping (B31.3), **S**tructural (IS 800), **T**EMA (Exchangers). ---

topics.title: Piping Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT524 - PROJECT ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Piping Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Piping Design** is a specialized branch of chemical/process engineering that involves the systematic planning, sizing, material selection, layout, and specification of piping systems used to transport fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, steam) within a process plant — from one unit operation to another. It integrates the principles of **fluid mechanics, heat transfer, materials science, structural mechanics, and process safety** to ensure safe, reliable, and economical conveyance of process streams.

Per the KTU PECHT524 syllabus under the 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education framework, piping design encompasses the calculation of **pipe diameter, wall thickness, pressure drop, insulation thickness, and supports** while complying with industry codes such as **ASME B31.3 (Process Piping), ASME B31.1 (Power Piping), and ANSI/ASME B36.10M/B36.19M** for dimensional standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Piping design is the **physical nervous system** of a chemical plant. While the P&ID is the *logical* network, the piping isodeline/plot plan and isometric drawings represent the *physical* realization. Roughly **30% of total plant capital cost** in a chemical/refinery project is attributed to piping.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine the **human circulatory system**:

- The **heart** = the pump/compressor that drives the fluid.
- The **arteries and veins** = the process pipes carrying chemicals.
- The **valves** = the control gates regulating flow.
- The **capillaries** = the small branch lines reaching heat exchangers, reactors, and instruments.
- The **blood** = the process fluid (hydrocarbons, water, acids, steam).

Just as a cardiologist must decide the **diameter** of vessels (too small → high pressure drop; too large → wasted cost and stagnant flow), a piping engineer must choose the **optimal pipe diameter**. Too thin a pipe wall may **rupture** (aneurysm); too thick a wall is **economically wasteful** and structurally over-engineered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** In the *Exxon Valdez* oil spill (1989) and several refinery incidents, root-cause analyses often pointed to **inadequate pipe sizing, improper material selection, or missing supports** — reinforcing why KTU examiners emphasize this topic.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are standard in piping design calculations:

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa} = 14.696 \; \text{psi}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)} = 10.7316 \; \text{psia·ft}^3\text{/ (lbmol·°R)}$
- **Roughness factor for commercial steel:** $\varepsilon = 0.00015 \; \text{ft} = 0.046 \; \text{mm} = 45.7 \; \mu\text{m}$
- **Standard pipe schedule numbers:** Sch 5, 10, 20, 40 (Std), 80 (XS), 120, 160, XXS
- **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS):** in inches, defined by **ANSI/ASME B36.10M**

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Trick:** "Schedule = wall thickness *tolerance*; higher schedule = thicker wall = higher pressure rating." Schedule 40 is the *default* for carbon steel process piping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Steps in Piping Design

A piping design project follows this structured workflow:

1. **Process Data Collection** — Gather fluid name, flow rate $Q$ or mass flow $w$, operating pressure $P$, temperature $T$, density $\rho$, viscosity $\mu$, vapor pressure, corrosivity, and solids content.
2. **Selection of Design Velocity** — Choose an *optimum* velocity based on service type. (See table below.)
3. **Preliminary Pipe Sizing** — Compute the inner diameter from the continuity equation.
4. **Standardization** — Round up to the nearest **Nominal Pipe Size (NPS)** from ANSI tables.
5. **Pressure Drop Calculation** — Use the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** with the **Moody friction factor**.
6. **Wall Thickness Calculation** — Apply ASME B31.3 code equation for *pressure design thickness*.
7. **Material Selection** — Based on temperature, pressure, corrosion, and cost.
8. **Support & Expansion Design** — Account for thermal expansion, weight, and vibration.
9. **Insulation Thickness** — Calculate to limit heat loss/gain and ensure personnel safety.
10. **Documentation** — Produce **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**, **Isometric drawings**, **Piping Material Specification (PMS)**, and **Line List**.

### 2.2 Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why a design velocity?** — Velocity too low → solids settle, two-phase flow instabilities. Velocity too high → erosion, water hammer, high pumping cost. Engineering compromise.
- **Why standardize to NPS?** — Pipe fittings, flanges, valves are *off-the-shelf*. Custom diameters are uneconomical.
- **Why Moody chart?** — Friction factor $f$ depends on Reynolds number $Re$ and relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$. It cannot be assumed constant; laminar ($f = 64/Re$) vs. turbulent ($f$ from Colebrook or Swamee-Jain) regimes differ.
- **Why ASME B31.3?** — It is the *de facto* international code for chemical process piping; the bar of legal/insurance acceptance.

### 2.3 Recommended Design Velocities (Industry Standards)

| Service Type | Recommended Velocity (m/s) | Recommended Velocity (ft/s) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Liquid (general) | 1.0 – 3.0 | 3 – 10 |
| Water (pumped) | 1.0 – 2.5 | 3 – 8 |
| Steam (saturated) | 15 – 40 | 50 – 130 |
| Steam (superheated) | 30 – 60 | 100 – 200 |
| Compressed air | 10 – 20 | 30 – 60 |
| Natural gas (high pressure) | 10 – 20 | 30 – 60 |
| Vacuum lines | 15 – 30 | 50 – 100 |
| Slurries | 1.5 – 3.0 | 5 – 10 |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for a piping design KTU question. Pay particular attention to unit conversions — most marks are lost here.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Continuity (Volumetric) | $Q = A \cdot v = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v$ | $Q$ = m³/s; $A$ = m²; $D$ = m; $v$ = m/s |
| 2 | Continuity (Mass) | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | $\dot{m}$ = kg/s; $\rho$ = kg/m³ |
| 3 | Pipe ID (Sizing) | $D_i = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 Q}{\pi v}}$ | All SI units |
| 4 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{D v}{\nu}$ | $\mu$ = Pa·s; $\nu$ = m²/s |
| 5 | Friction Factor (Laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Dimensionless (Fanning) $f_F = 16/Re$ for Fanning |
| 6 | Friction Factor (Turbulent, Colebrook) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$ | Implicit in $f$ |
| 7 | Friction Factor (Swamee-Jain) | $f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$ | Explicit in $Re$, $\varepsilon/D$ |
| 8 | Darcy-Weisbach Head Loss | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | $h_f$ = m; $L$ = m; $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 9 | Darcy-Weisbach Pressure Drop | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $\Delta P_f$ = Pa |
| 10 | Equivalent Length Method | $L_{eq} = \sum K_i \cdot \dfrac{D}{f}$ OR $L_{eq} = L + \sum L_{e,i}$ | Sum of fittings equivalent length |
| 11 | ASME B31.3 Pressure Thickness | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)} + C$ | $t$ = in; $P$ = psig; $D$ = in; $S$ = stress (psi); $E$ = quality factor; $Y$ = temp coeff; $C$ = corrosion allowance (in) |
| 12 | Thermal Expansion | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = 1/°C; $\Delta T$ in °C |
| 13 | Insulation (Cylindrical Heat Loss) | $\dfrac{Q}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi (T_i - T_o)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k}}$ | $k$ = W/(m·K); radii in m |
| 14 | Critical (Sonic) Velocity | $v_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}$ | $\gamma$ = Cp/Cv; $M$ = kg/kmol |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Piping design finds application in:

- **Oil & Gas Refineries** — long-distance cross-country pipelines (e.g., 48-inch crude trunk lines).
- **Petrochemical Plants** — ethylene cracker furnace piping operating at 850 °C.
- **Pharmaceutical & Food** — sanitary stainless-steel piping (SS 304/316L), orbital welding.
- **Power Plants** — high-pressure steam piping (Sch 160, XXS).
- **HVAC & District Cooling** — chilled water networks.

A *good* design balances **safety, cost, and operability**. A *bad* design can lead to rupture, leaks, plant shutdowns, or even fatalities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pipe Sizing Equation (Continuity-Based)

Starting from the principle of **conservation of mass for an incompressible fluid in steady flow**:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v = \text{constant}$$

For a circular pipe of internal diameter $D_i$:

$$A = \frac{\pi D_i^2}{4}$$

Substituting into the volumetric form $Q = A v$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D_i^2}{4} \cdot v$$

Solving for the internal diameter $D_i$:

$$D_i^2 = \frac{4Q}{\pi v}$$

$$\boxed{D_i = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}}}$$

**Interpretation:** The pipe diameter scales with the **square root of the volumetric flow** and **inversely with the square root of the design velocity**. Doubling the flow increases the required diameter by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ times.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Darcy-Weisbach Equation

Consider a horizontal pipe of constant diameter with a fluid flowing at average velocity $v$. Apply the **mechanical energy balance** between sections 1 and 2, assuming no pump or turbine:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

For a horizontal pipe of constant diameter ($z_1 = z_2$ and $v_1 = v_2$ by continuity), the equation reduces to:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h_f$$

The friction head loss $h_f$ is **empirically observed** to be proportional to:
- The pipe length $L$
- The square of the velocity $v^2$
- Inversely proportional to the diameter $D$
- A dimensionless friction factor $f$

This leads to the **Darcy-Weisbach equation**:

$$\boxed{h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\rho g$ gives the **pressure drop form**:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Friction Factor Convention:** The $f$ in Darcy-Weisbach is the **Darcy friction factor** (4× the Fanning friction factor). Always verify which convention the problem uses.

### 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A pump delivers **30,000 kg/h** of cooling water (density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, viscosity $\mu = 1.0$ cP) through **200 m of horizontal commercial steel pipe** with two 90° standard elbows and one fully open gate valve. Calculate the pipe diameter and the pressure drop.

**Given:**
- Mass flow $\dot{m} = 30{,}000$ kg/h $= 8.333$ kg/s
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 1.0 \; \text{cP} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $L = 200$ m
- Roughness $\varepsilon = 0.046$ mm
- Design velocity (water) = $v = 1.5$ m/s

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{8.333 \; \text{kg/s}}{1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Required internal diameter:**

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times 1.5}}$$

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{0.03333}{4.7124}} = \sqrt{7.073 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$D_i = 0.0841 \; \text{m} = 84.1 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Standardize to NPS:**

Looking up ANSI B36.10M, the next standard size is **NPS 3 (DN 80)** with:
- $D_{OD} = 88.9$ mm
- Sch 40 wall thickness = 5.49 mm
- Internal diameter $D_i = 88.9 - 2(5.49) = 77.92$ mm $\approx 0.0779$ m

**Step 4 — Recalculate the actual velocity:**

$$v_{actual} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D_i^2} = \frac{4 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi \times (0.0779)^2}$$

$$v_{actual} = \frac{0.03333}{0.01907} = 1.748 \; \text{m/s}$$

This is within the recommended range (1.0 – 3.0 m/s), so the selection is acceptable.

**Step 5 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D_i}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.748 \times 0.0779}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{136.16}{0.001} = 136{,}160$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 6 — Relative roughness:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D_i} = \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0779} = 5.905 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Friction factor using Swamee-Jain:**

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$

Computing each term:
- $\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} = \dfrac{5.905 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 1.5959 \times 10^{-4}$
- $Re^{0.9} = (136{,}160)^{0.9} = 3.787 \times 10^{4}$ (using calculator)
- $\dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} = \dfrac{5.74}{3.787 \times 10^{4}} = 1.5157 \times 10^{-4}$

Sum inside the log: $1.5959 \times 10^{-4} + 1.5157 \times 10^{-4} = 3.1116 \times 10^{-4}$

$\log_{10}(3.1116 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.5071$

Square: $(-3.5071)^2 = 12.300$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{12.300} = 0.02033$$

**Step 8 — Equivalent length of fittings:**

From standard tables (Crane TP-410):
- 90° standard elbow: $L_e/D = 30$ → $L_{e,elbow} = 30 \times 0.0779 = 2.337$ m
- Fully open gate valve: $L_e/D = 8$ → $L_{e,valve} = 8 \times 0.0779 = 0.623$ m

For 2 elbows: $L_{e,total} = 2(2.337) + 0.623 = 5.297$ m

Total equivalent length: $L_{tot} = 200 + 5.297 = 205.3$ m

**Step 9 — Pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L_{tot}}{D_i} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.02033 \times \frac{205.3}{0.0779} \times \frac{1000 \times (1.748)^2}{2}$$

Step-wise:
- $\dfrac{L_{tot}}{D_i} = \dfrac{205.3}{0.0779} = 2635.4$
- $\dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} = \dfrac{1000 \times 3.0555}{2} = 1527.75$ Pa
- $f \times 2635.4 = 0.02033 \times 2635.4 = 53.578$

$$\Delta P_f = 53.578 \times 1527.75 = 81{,}853 \; \text{Pa} \approx 81.85 \; \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_f \approx 81.9 \; \text{kPa} \; (\approx 0.82 \; \text{bar})}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of ASME B31.3 Wall Thickness

The ASME B31.3 code provides the **pressure design thickness** for straight pipe under internal pressure:

$$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)} + C$$

Where:
- $t$ = pressure design thickness (in)
- $P$ = internal design pressure (psig)
- $D$ = outside diameter of pipe (in)
- $S$ = allowable stress at design temperature (psi)
- $E$ = longitudinal joint quality factor (1.0 for seamless, 0.85 for ERW)
- $Y$ = temperature coefficient (table 304.1.1, e.g., 0.4 for ferritic steel < 900 °F)
- $C$ = corrosion + mechanical allowances (in)

**Why this form?** It comes from the **hoop stress equation** for a thin-walled cylinder:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P D}{2 t}$$

Rearranging for $t$ and applying a safety factor via allowable stress $S$:

$$t = \frac{P D}{2 \sigma_{allowable}} = \frac{P D}{2 (S \cdot E \cdot \text{factor})}$$

The factor $Y$ in the denominator (with a $+ PY$ in the denominator) reflects the **material's strain-hardening behavior at elevated temperature**, derived from the **Lame equation** for thick cylinders.

### 3.5 Derivation of Critical Pipe Diameter (Two-Phase Flow Special Case)

For two-phase flow (gas-liquid), the **Baker chart** or the **Beggs-Brill correlation** is used. A simplified critical diameter to avoid slug flow:

$$D_{c} = \frac{0.00233}{\sqrt{\rho_G \cdot v_G}} \quad \text{(in metres)}$$

Where $\rho_G$ is gas density (kg/m³) and $v_G$ is superficial gas velocity (m/s).

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Piping Design Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Project Inputs] --> B0[Process Data: P, T, Q, rho, mu]
    B0 --> C0{Select Design Velocity v_opt}
    C0 --> D0[Compute Internal Diameter D_i]
    D0 --> E0[Standardize to NPS / DN Size]
    E0 --> F0[Compute Reynolds Number Re]
    F0 --> G0{Flow Regime?}
    G0 --> H0[Laminar: f = 64/Re]
    G0 --> I0[Turbulent: f from Colebrook/Swamee-Jain]
    H0 --> J0[Compute Pressure Drop Delta P_f]
    I0 --> J0
    J0 --> K0[Add Fittings: K-method or L_e method]
    K0 --> L0[Select Pipe Schedule - ASME B31.3]
    L0 --> M0[Material Selection: CS / SS / Alloy]
    M0 --> N0[Insulation Thickness Calculation]
    N0 --> O0[Support Span and Anchors]
    O0 --> P0[Generate Isometric Drawing and Line List]
    P0 --> Q0[Documentation: P&ID, PMS, BOQ]
    Q0 --> R0[Project Complete - Ready for Construction]
```

### 4.2 Pipe Component Identification Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Pipe Segment L_1] --> F1[Flanged Joint]
    F1 --> P2[Pipe Segment L_2]
    P2 --> E1[90 Degree Elbow]
    E1 --> P3[Pipe Segment L_3]
    P3 --> V1[Gate Valve]
    V1 --> P4[Pipe Segment L_4]
    P4 --> T1[Tee Branch]
    T1 --> P5[Pipe Segment L_5]
    P5 --> R1[Reducer]
    R1 --> P6[Final Pipe Run to Equipment]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Piping Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Source Equipment: Pump or Compressor] --> ISO[Inlet Isolating Valve]
    ISO --> STRN[Strainer Y-Type]
    STRN --> CV[Check Valve - NRV]
    CV --> GV[Mainline Gate Valve]
    GV --> D1[Distribution Header A]
    GV --> D2[Distribution Header B]
    D1 --> E1[Branch 1: To Heat Exchanger]
    D1 --> E2[Branch 2: To Reactor]
    D2 --> E3[Branch 3: To Storage Tank]
    D2 --> E4[Branch 4: To Distillation Column]
    E1 --> SENS1[Pressure and Temperature Sensors]
    E2 --> SENS2[Flow Transmitter]
    E3 --> SENS3[Level Switch]
    E4 --> SENS4[Control Valve]
```

### 4.4 Friction Factor Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Compute Re and epsilon over D] --> R1{Re less than 2000?}
    R1 -- Yes --> R2[Laminar Flow: f equals 64 divided by Re]
    R1 -- No --> R3{Re between 2000 and 4000?}
    R3 -- Yes --> R4[Transition: Use Sieder-Tate or empirical chart]
    R3 -- No --> R5{Re greater than 4000: Turbulent}
    R5 --> R6{Use Moody's Chart or Colebrook Equation}
    R6 --> R7[Iterative Solution or Swamee-Jain Explicit]
    R7 --> R8[Final f for Darcy-Weisbach]
```
