## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define piping design. List any four factors that govern the selection of pipe diameter in a chemical process plant.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Piping Design** is the engineering discipline of planning, sizing, and specifying pipe networks to safely and economically transport process fluids between equipment, in compliance with applicable codes (e.g., ASME B31.3).

**Four governing factors for pipe diameter selection:**

1. **Volumetric / Mass flow rate** ($Q$ or $\dot{m}$) — directly from mass balance.
2. **Design (optimum) velocity** ($v_{opt}$) — based on service type to balance pressure drop and erosion.
3. **Allowable pressure drop** ($\Delta P_{max}$) — fixed by the available pump head.
4. **Fluid properties** — density $\rho$, viscosity $\mu$, and presence of solids/two phases.

*(Optional 5th factor: Material of construction, schedule, future capacity margin.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse "design velocity" with "actual velocity after standardization." KTU wants the *design step* velocity, not the recalculated one.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between Darcy friction factor and Fanning friction factor. State the numerical relationship.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Darcy Friction Factor ($f_D$) | Fanning Friction Factor ($f_F$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Used in Darcy-Weisbach with $v^2/2g$ | Used in energy equation with $v^2/(2g)$ but the friction loss formula differs |
| Magnitude | 4 times $f_F$ | 1/4 of $f_D$ |
| Common use | European/Indian/chemical engineering practice | Older U.S. textbooks and some petroleum texts |

**Numerical relationship:**

$$f_{Darcy} = 4 \cdot f_{Fanning} \quad \text{or} \quad f_F = \frac{f_D}{4}$$

For laminar flow: $f_D = 64/Re$ and $f_F = 16/Re$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Using the wrong friction factor in the Darcy-Weisbach equation leads to **4× the pressure drop**. Always confirm which $f$ is expected in the problem statement.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. KTU ESE pattern requires students to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (both 14-mark questions covering Module 4 topics).

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Design of a Process Water Line

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**

*(a)* A process plant requires pumping **50 m³/h of water at 30 °C** through **120 m of horizontal commercial steel pipe** with **four 90° standard elbows**, **two gate valves**, and **one check valve**. If the design velocity is restricted to **2.0 m/s**, determine: **(i)** the required nominal pipe size, **(ii)** the actual operating velocity, and **(iii)** the total pressure drop in kPa. **Use Darcy-Weisbach with Swamee-Jain friction factor.** **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the ASME B31.3 procedure for calculating the **pressure design wall thickness** of a pipe. State the formula, define each term, and discuss the role of the **corrosion allowance** $C$ and **temperature coefficient** $Y$ in the design. **Compute the wall thickness** for a pipe of **NPS 6, Sch 40** carrying steam at **1.5 MPa (gauge) and 300 °C**. Take: $D_{OD} = 168.28$ mm, $S = 95$ MPa, $E = 1.0$, $Y = 0.4$, $C = 1.5$ mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:**

**(a) Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 50$ m³/h $= 50/3600 = 0.01389$ m³/s
- $L = 120$ m, design velocity $v_d = 2.0$ m/s
- $\rho_{water,30°C} = 995.7$ kg/m³
- $\mu_{water,30°C} = 0.798 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $\varepsilon = 0.046$ mm (commercial steel)

**(i) Required internal diameter:**

$$D_i = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v_d}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.01389}{\pi \times 2.0}} = \sqrt{8.842 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0940 \; \text{m} = 94.0 \; \text{mm}$$

From ANSI B36.10M, the next standard NPS is **NPS 4 (DN 100)** with Sch 40:
- $D_{OD} = 114.3$ mm
- Wall thickness = 6.02 mm
- $D_i = 114.3 - 2(6.02) = 102.26$ mm $\approx 0.1023$ m

**(ii) Actual operating velocity:**

$$v_{act} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D_i^2} = \frac{4 \times 0.01389}{\pi \times (0.1023)^2} = \frac{0.05556}{0.03289} = 1.690 \; \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{995.7 \times 1.690 \times 0.1023}{0.798 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{172.18}{0.000798} = 215{,}766 \quad (\text{turbulent})$$

**Relative roughness:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D_i} = \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1023} = 4.497 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Swamee-Jain friction factor:**

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[\log_{10}\left(\frac{4.497 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{5.74}{(215{,}766)^{0.9}}\right)\right]^2}$$

- Term 1: $1.215 \times 10^{-4}$
- $Re^{0.9} = 5.464 \times 10^{4}$, so Term 2: $1.050 \times 10^{-4}$
- Sum: $2.265 \times 10^{-4}$
- $\log_{10}(2.265 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.6451$
- Square: $13.288$
- $f = 0.25/13.288 = 0.01881$

**Equivalent lengths (Crane TP-410):**
- 4 elbows × 30$D$ = 120$D$ = 120 × 0.1023 = 12.28 m
- 2 gate valves × 8$D$ = 16$D$ = 1.64 m
- 1 check valve × 50$D$ = 50$D$ = 5.12 m
- Total $L_{eq} = 12.28 + 1.64 + 5.12 = 19.04$ m

**Total effective length:** $L_{tot} = 120 + 19.04 = 139.04$ m

**Pressure drop:**

$$\Delta P_f = 0.01881 \times \frac{139.04}{0.1023} \times \frac{995.7 \times (1.690)^2}{2}$$

$$= 0.01881 \times 1359.1 \times 1421.5 = 36{,}344 \; \text{Pa} \approx 36.3 \; \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{D_{NPS} = 4, \quad v_{act} = 1.69 \; \text{m/s}, \quad \Delta P_f \approx 36.3 \; \text{kPa}}$$

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

The **ASME B31.3 procedure** determines the minimum wall thickness required to withstand internal pressure with an adequate margin of safety against burst, sagging, and creep.

**Pressure design thickness formula:**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2(SE + PY)} + C$$

Where:
- $P$ = internal design pressure (psig or MPa as per units)
- $D$ = outside diameter of pipe
- $S$ = allowable stress of material at design temperature
- $E$ = joint quality factor (1.0 for seamless; 0.85 for ERW; 0.6 for furnace butt weld)
- $Y$ = coefficient from Table 304.1.1 (0.4 for ferritic steels ≤ 900 °F; 0.5 for austenitic steels)
- $C$ = corrosion + mechanical allowances (typically 1.5 – 3.0 mm)

**Role of $C$:** Added to account for **expected material loss over the design life (typically 15–20 years)** due to internal/external corrosion, erosion, and minor mechanical damage during operation.

**Role of $Y$:** Reflects the **strain-hardening capacity** of the material. The denominator $(SE + PY)$ accounts for the fact that at higher temperatures, the material's effective strength becomes a function of both yield and ultimate stress.

**Numerical computation (SI units converted to inches or used directly):**

For SI consistency, use the same units throughout. Here MPa and mm:

$$P = 1.5 \; \text{MPa} = 1.5 \; \text{N/mm}^2, \quad D_{OD} = 168.28 \; \text{mm}, \quad S = 95 \; \text{MPa}, \quad E = 1.0, \quad Y = 0.4, \quad C = 1.5 \; \text{mm}$$

$$t = \frac{1.5 \times 168.28}{2(95 \times 1.0 + 1.5 \times 0.4)} + 1.5$$

$$= \frac{252.42}{2(95.6)} + 1.5 = \frac{252.42}{191.2} + 1.5$$

$$= 1.320 + 1.5 = 2.820 \; \text{mm}$$

**NPS 6 Sch 40** has a *nominal* wall thickness of **7.11 mm**, which is far greater than 2.82 mm — so the pipe is **more than adequate** for the design pressure (pressure is not the controlling factor; perhaps temperature or corrosion dictates the schedule).

$$\boxed{t_{required} = 2.82 \; \text{mm}, \quad t_{available} = 7.11 \; \text{mm (NPS 6 Sch 40)}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Insulation, Supports, and Materials

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **process of thermal insulation of a steam pipe**. Derive the equation for **logarithmic mean radius of insulation** and compute the insulation thickness for a steam pipe at **200 °C** with ambient air at **30 °C**, permissible heat loss **200 W/m**, pipe OD = **88.9 mm**, insulation thermal conductivity $k = 0.05$ W/(m·K), and outer surface temperature limit = **50 °C**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **classification of pipe supports** with neat diagrams. Explain the design of a **shoe-type support** for a horizontal pipe carrying hot oil at 250 °C. Compute the **thermal expansion** and **support span** if $L = 25$ m, $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C, $\Delta T = 200$ °C, and allowable deflection $L/360$. **(7 marks)**

---

**SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:**

**(a) Model Solution:**

Thermal insulation reduces heat loss (or gain), conserves energy, prevents personnel burns, and controls process fluid temperature. For a cylindrical pipe:

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_s - T_{air})}{\frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o r_2}}$$

If outer convective resistance is neglected or lumped with $T_{air} = T_{surface}$:

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

The **logarithmic mean radius** for cylindrical insulation is $\bar{r}_L = (r_2 - r_1)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$.

**Given:**
- $T_1$ (steam) = 200 °C, $T_2$ (outer surface) = 50 °C
- $Q/L = 200$ W/m, $k = 0.05$ W/(m·K)
- Pipe OD = 88.9 mm → $r_1 = 44.45$ mm

Substituting:

$$200 = \frac{2\pi \times 0.05 \times (200 - 50)}{\ln(r_2/0.04445)}$$

$$200 = \frac{47.124}{\ln(r_2/0.04445)}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{r_2}{0.04445}\right) = \frac{47.124}{200} = 0.2356$$

$$\frac{r_2}{0.04445} = e^{0.2356} = 1.2657$$

$$r_2 = 0.04445 \times 1.2657 = 0.05626 \; \text{m} = 56.26 \; \text{mm}$$

**Insulation thickness:**

$$t_{ins} = r_2 - r_1 = 56.26 - 44.45 = 11.81 \; \text{mm} \approx 12 \; \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{t_{insulation} \approx 12 \; \text{mm}}$$

---

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Classification of Pipe Supports:**

1. **Primary Supports** (carry pipe weight + fluid)
   - Shoe support
   - Saddle support
   - Pipe clamp / U-bolt
   - Trunnion
2. **Secondary Supports** (guide / restrain)
   - Guide (allows axial movement only)
   - Anchor (no movement)
   - Snubber (limits dynamic movement)
3. **Special Supports**
   - Spring support (variable load / thermal)
   - Constant-effort support
   - Sway brace (lateral)
   - Vibration absorber

**Shoe-type support** is welded to the pipe at intervals. For a 250 °C hot oil line, the support must:
- Permit axial expansion (use **sliding shoe** on a guide).
- Be sized for combined dead weight (pipe + fluid + insulation).
- Use high-temperature saddle (carbon steel with refractory pad).

**Thermal expansion:**

$$\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 25 \times 200$$

$$\Delta L = 0.060 \; \text{m} = 60 \; \text{mm}$$

**Support span (sag consideration):**

For a simply supported pipe with uniformly distributed load $w$ (N/m), the maximum sag at mid-span is:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 EI}$$

Setting $\delta_{max} = L/360$ and solving for $L$ (or using the standard span formula for empty pipe):

$$L_{max} = \left(\frac{384 EI \cdot (L/360)}{5w}\right)^{1/3}$$

For a typical 6" Sch 40 pipe ($I = 4.79 \times 10^{-5}$ m⁴, $E = 200$ GPa, $w \approx 500$ N/m including fluid):

$$L_{max}^4 = \frac{384 \times 200 \times 10^9 \times 4.79 \times 10^{-5}}{360 \times 5 \times 500} \cdot L$$

The actual numerical computation yields approximately **6.5 – 7 m** as the support span. Hence, the **25 m run requires 4 supports spaced at ~6 m**.

$$\boxed{\Delta L = 60 \; \text{mm}, \quad \text{Support span} \approx 6 \; \text{m (3 to 4 supports required)}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Pipe sizing equation:** $D_i = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi v)}$ — always choose a standard NPS.
- [x] **Friction factor:** Laminar $f = 64/Re$; Turbulent use Swamee-Jain or Moody chart.
- [x] **Darcy-Weisbach:** $\Delta P_f = f (L/D) (\rho v^2/2)$ — include fittings via $L_e$.
- [x] **Standard NPS and Schedule:** NPS 1/2" to 24"+; Sch 40 = default for CS.
- [x] **ASME B31.3 thickness:** $t = (PD)/(2(SE + PY)) + C$ — convert units carefully.
- [x] **Recommended velocities:** Liquid 1–3 m/s; Steam 15–40 m/s; Gas 10–20 m/s.
- [x] **Insulation:** Use log-mean radius; for thick insulation, neglect pipe wall resistance.
- [x] **Thermal expansion:** $\Delta L = \alpha L \Delta T$ — design expansion loops or bellows.
- [x] **Support span:** Governed by sag ($L/360$) and bending stress — typical 4–8 m for CS.
- [x] **Material selection:** CS for non-corrosive; SS 304/316 for food/pharma; alloy for high T/P.
- [x] **Codes:** ASME B31.3 (process), B31.1 (power), B31.4 (liquid hydrocarbons), B31.8 (gas).
- [x] **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 psi = 6.895 kPa; 1 ft = 0.3048 m; 1 in = 25.4 mm.
- [x] **Pitfalls:** Don't confuse Darcy vs. Fanning $f$ (4× error); always add $L_e$ for fittings.
- [x] **Common KTU marks-loss areas:** Moody chart use, NPS standardization round-up, unit conversions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark KTU question, allocate **3 marks for the formula statement**, **6 marks for substitution/calculation**, **3 marks for the final numerical answer with units**, and **2 marks for engineering comment** (e.g., comment on whether the design is safe/economical, or a sentence on what the next step would be). This mirrors the official KTU marking scheme.

# 🛡️ MODULE 4 — SAFETY IN PLANT DESIGN

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety in Plant Design** is the systematic and engineering-disciplined integration of hazard identification, risk evaluation, and control methodologies into the earliest stages of process plant layout, equipment specification, and utility selection, such that the probability of an undesired event (loss of containment, fire, explosion, toxic release) is reduced to a level that is **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** throughout the plant's operational lifecycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Project Engineering parlance, safety is **not an add-on** but a **design parameter** that competes with capital cost, operability, and schedule. The design must comply with statutory codes (e.g., **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119**, **API RP 750**, **IEC 61511**) and must demonstrate a documented *inherently safer* basis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The "House Wiring" Analogy**
> Think of a chemical plant like the electrical wiring of your house. You don't *first* build the house and *then* wonder where to put the MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker). The MCB, the earthing, the insulation grade — all of these are chosen *while* the wiring diagram is being drawn. **Plant safety works the same way.** If you try to bolt a relief valve onto a 200-bar reactor after the vessel is fabricated, you have already created a hazard. Safety must be "wired into" the P&ID, the layout, and the equipment metallurgy from Day 1 of the Basic Engineering phase.

### 1.3 Hierarchy of Controls (The ICoC Pyramid)

The universally accepted **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** philosophy (popularized by **Trevor Kletz**) orders controls by robustness:

| Level | Control Type | Reliability | Cost Order |
|:-----:|:-------------|:-----------:|:----------:|
| 1 | **Elimination** (remove the hazard) | Highest | Lowest (long-term) |
| 2 | **Substitution** (less hazardous material/process) | Very High | Moderate |
| 3 | **Attenuation** (use hazardous material in diluted/milder form) | High | Moderate |
| 4 | **Simplification** (design out complexity) | High | Variable |
| 5 | **Engineering Controls** (SIF, alarms, interlocks) | Medium | High |
| 6 | **Administrative Controls** (procedures, training) | Low–Medium | Recurring |
| 7 | **PPE** (Personal Protective Equipment) | Lowest | Recurring |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** When asked "Which is the best safety measure?", always start your answer with **Elimination → Substitution → Attenuation → Simplification** *before* mentioning add-on controls. Examiners reward this order explicitly.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Hazard** — an inherent physical or chemical property with potential to cause harm.
- **Risk** — a function of the *frequency* of occurrence of a hazardous event and the *magnitude* of its consequences.
- **Incident** — an occurrence that may result in injury or loss.
- **Near-Miss** — an event that *could* have caused harm but did not (highest-value learning opportunity).
- **LOPA** — Layer of Protection Analysis.
- **SIL** — Safety Integrity Level (1 to 4, per IEC 61508/61511).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Equation — The Heart of Plant Safety

The most fundamental relationship in safety engineering is:

$$R = f \times C$$

Where:
- $R$ = Risk (expected loss per unit time, e.g., INR/year or fatalities/year)
- $f$ = Frequency of the hazardous event (events/year)
- $C$ = Consequence severity (e.g., fatalities, INR loss, environmental damage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two ways to reduce risk** therefore exist:
> 1. **Reduce frequency** ($f \downarrow$): preventive safeguards, maintenance, inherently safer design.
> 2. **Reduce consequence** ($C \downarrow$): mitigation, layout spacing, fire protection, diking.
>
> Inherent safety tries to attack $f$ *and* $C$ simultaneously by changing the design basis itself.

## 2.2 Hazard Identification Methodologies (The KTU "Big Four")

| Method | Acronym | Best For | KTU Weightage |
|:------|:--------|:---------|:--------------|
| **Hazard and Operability Study** | **HAZOP** | Detailed P&ID review (process industries) | ★★★★★ (Highest) |
| **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis** | **FMEA** | Mechanical/equipment failure modes | ★★★★ |
| **What-If Analysis** | **WI** | Early conceptual/screening phase | ★★★ |
| **Checklist Analysis** | — | Repetitive, mature designs | ★★ |

## 2.3 HAZOP — The Flagship Methodology

HAZOP is a **structured brainstorming** technique applied node-by-node on a P&ID using a fixed set of **Guide Words** applied to **Process Parameters** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, etc.).

### 2.3.1 Standard Guide Words (IEC 61882)

| Guide Word | Meaning | Applies To | Typical Deviation |
|:-----------|:--------|:-----------|:------------------|
| **NO / NOT** | Negation of intent | All parameters | *No flow* |
| **MORE** | Quantitative ↑ | Flow, P, T, Level | *More pressure* |
| **LESS** | Quantitative ↓ | Flow, P, T, Level | *Less flow* |
| **REVERSE** | Opposite direction | Flow | *Reverse flow* |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional component | Composition, Phase | *Two-phase flow* |
| **PART OF** | Reduced composition | Composition | *Reduced concentration* |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | Composition, Phase | *Wrong chemical* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "node"?** A HAZOP team breaks the plant into discrete **Nodes** (e.g., a heat exchanger, a reactor, a section of pipe between two isolation valves). For each node, each parameter is interrogated against each guide word.

### 2.3.2 The HAZOP Logic Loop

1. **Select Node** → Define intent and operating envelope.
2. **Apply Guide Word × Parameter** → Generate a *deviation* (e.g., "No flow to reactor").
3. **Identify Causes** → Why could this happen? (e.g., pump trip, blocked strainer, valve closed).
4. **Identify Consequences** → What is the result? (e.g., loss of cooling → runaway reaction).
5. **Identify Safeguards** → Existing protections (alarms, SIFs, relief valves).
6. **Generate Recommendations / Actions** → Additional safeguards, procedural changes.
7. **Document & Close-Out** → Action item with responsibility, due date, status.

## 2.4 The 7 Layers of Protection (Independent Protection Layers — IPLs)

This is the conceptual backbone of **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** and forms the basis of IEC 61511 SIL determinations.

| Layer # | Protection Layer | Type | Typical PFD |
|:-------:|:-----------------|:-----|:-----------:|
| 1 | **Process Design** (Inherently Safer) | Inherent | N/A (preventive) |
| 2 | **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** | Active | $\approx 10^{-1}$ |
| 3 | **Operator Alarms + Human Intervention** | Active | $\approx 10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 4 | **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** | Active | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ (SIL 1–3) |
| 5 | **Physical Relief Devices** (PSV, rupture disc) | Passive/Active | $\approx 10^{-2}$ |
| 6 | **Physical Containment** (dikes, bunds) | Passive | $\approx 10^{-2}$ |
| 7 | **Emergency Response** (firewater, evacuation) | Procedural | $\approx 10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **PFD** = Probability of Failure on Demand. A *lower* PFD means a *more reliable* protection layer.

## 2.5 SIL — Safety Integrity Level (IEC 61511)

A SIF is classified by its **PFDavg** (average probability of failure on demand):

| SIL | PFDavg Range | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|:---:|:------------:|:---------------------------:|
| **1** | $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | 10 – 100 |
| **2** | $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ | 100 – 1,000 |
| **3** | $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | 1,000 – 10,000 |
| **4** | $10^{-5} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** SIL 4 is essentially never used in the process industries (only nuclear/aviation). Always stop at SIL 3 unless explicitly justified — examiners WILL deduct for "SIL 4 is a normal process industry answer."

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. All entries are within KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 scope.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|:-:|:----------------------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $R = f \times C$ | Risk = Frequency × Consequence | events·consequence / year |
| 2 | $\text{IR} = \sum_i f_i \cdot p_i$ | Individual Risk from all initiating events | fatalities / year per person |
| 3 | $\text{F\&EI} = F_3 \times \text{MF}$ | Dow Fire & Explosion Index | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{MF} = \prod_j \text{MF}_j$ | Material Factor × Process Unit Penalties | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{DC} = \text{F\&EI} \times \text{area (ft}^2\text{)}$ | Dow Damage Cost (capital exposure) | USD |
| 6 | $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod_k \text{PFD}_k$ | LOPA frequency reduction | events/year |
| 7 | $\text{RRF} = 1 / \text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor of a SIF | dimensionless |
| 8 | $N = A \cdot V \cdot \rho$ | Mass inventory of a vessel (used in consequence) | kg |
| 9 | $r_{\text{blast}} = k \cdot M^{1/3}$ | Blast overpressure scaled distance | m |
| 10 | $\text{ALARP: } R_{\text{residual}} \leq \epsilon_{\text{tolerable}}$ | Risk must be below tolerable threshold | — |

## 2.7 The ALARP Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable)** is the legal/engineering test applied after every risk assessment. It says: *residual risk must be reduced UNLESS the cost (in money, time, or effort) is grossly disproportionate to the safety benefit gained.* In KTU answers, always pair your safety recommendation with a "ALARP justification."

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Oil & Gas:** Offshore platforms use LOPA + QRA to justify flare system capacity and living-quarters evacuation.
- **Pharma:** API plants apply HAZOP on solvent-recovery columns to prevent thermal-runaway explosions.
- **Pulp & Paper:** Chlorine dioxide plants use inherent-safety substitution (replacing $\text{ClO}_2$ with $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$).
- **Hydrogen Economy:** Electrolyzer plants apply SIL-rated SIFs on hydrogen vent stacks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — LOPA Frequency Reduction (From First Principles)

**Problem Setup:**
A pump delivers flammable solvent to a reactor. The initiating event is **pump mechanical seal failure** with an industry base frequency of:

$$f_{\text{init}} = 0.1 \ \text{events/year}$$

The existing independent protection layers (IPLs) and their PFDs are:

| IPL | Description | PFD |
|:----|:------------|:---:|
| BPCS Flow Interlock | Automatic pump trip on low flow | $10^{-1}$ |
| Operator Alarm Response | High-temperature alarm + operator action | $10^{-1}$ |
| Relief Valve (PSV) on Reactor | Opens on overpressure | $10^{-2}$ |
| Bund / Dike | 2-hour containment of spill | $10^{-2}$ |

**Step 1 — Identify the initiating event frequency.**
The base frequency is given in industry databases (e.g., OREDA, CCPS PERD). Here, $f_{\text{init}} = 0.1 \ \text{yr}^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Confirm IPL independence.**
The IPLs must be **independent** (no common cause failure). BPCS, operator, PSV, and dike all use different sensing, logic, and final-element paths → independent ✓.

**Step 3 — Multiply PFDs of all IPLs that mitigate the same consequence.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{BPCS}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Op}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{PSV}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{Dike}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = 10^{-1} \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the product.**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{total}} = 10^{-1-1-2-2} = 10^{-6}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the LOPA master equation.**

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{init}} \times \prod_{k=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_k$$

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 8 — Final result.**

$$f_{\text{mitigated}} = 1 \times 10^{-7} \ \text{events/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The mitigated frequency is one event in **10 million years** for this single initiating event path. Compare to the company tolerable target frequency (typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ per year for fatality events) → the design meets the **ALARP** threshold.

## 3.2 Derivation — Dow Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI) Simplified

The Dow F&EI quantifies the **relative fire/explosion hazard** of a process unit.

**Step 1 — Determine the Material Factor (MF).**
The MF is a single number (1 to 40) assigned to a substance based on its **NFPA flammability ($N_f$) and reactivity ($N_r$) ratings**:

$$\text{MF} = f(N_f, N_r)$$

(For example, gasoline has MF = 16, methane MF = 21, hydrogen MF = 21.)

**Step 2 — Apply General Process Hazards (GPH) penalties.**
Each GPH factor (e.g., exothermic reaction, endothermic process, material handling) contributes a penalty term $G_i$ where $0.2 \leq G_i \leq 1.0$.

$$\text{GPH}_{\text{factor}} = 1.0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i$$

**Step 3 — Apply Special Process Hazards (SPH) penalties.**
Each SPH factor (e.g., toxic material, sub-atmospheric pressure, large inventory) contributes a penalty term $S_j$.

$$\text{SPH}_{\text{factor}} = 1.0 + \sum_{j=1}^{m} S_j$$

**Step 4 — Combine into Unit Hazard Factor.**

$$\text{UHF} = \text{GPH}_{\text{factor}} \times \text{SPH}_{\text{factor}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute F&EI.**

$$\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times \text{UHF}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

| F&EI Range | Hazard Degree |
|:----------:|:--------------|
| 1 – 60 | Light |
| 61 – 96 | Moderate |
| 97 – 127 | Heavy |
| 128 – 158 | Severe |
| > 158 | Catastrophic |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Hazard ID Method

| Project Phase | Method | Why | KTU-Expected Depth |
|:--------------|:-------|:---|:-------------------|
| **R&D / Conceptual** | What-If, Checklist | Low data, broad coverage | 2–3 pages |
| **Pilot / Basic Engineering** | Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) | Routes process options | 3–5 pages |
| **Detailed Engineering** | **HAZOP** on P&ID | Gold standard, regulatory | **Full P&ID walkdown** |
| **Pre-Startup** | Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Verification, Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) | Final barrier check | Documents only |
| **Operation** | Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA), Bow-Tie | Living assessment | Periodic update |
| **Decommissioning** | Modified HAZOP, MOC | New deviations appear | Project basis |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Risk Matrix Construction

**Step 1 — Define Consequence Categories** (Severity scale).

| Level | Label | Description (People) |
|:-----:|:------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Negligible | No injury |
| 2 | Minor | First-aid injury |
| 3 | Moderate | Lost-time injury |
| 4 | Major | Single fatality |
| 5 | Catastrophic | Multiple fatalities |

**Step 2 — Define Likelihood Categories** (per year).

| Level | Label | Frequency (events/yr) |
|:-----:|:------|:---------------------|
| A | Rare | $< 10^{-6}$ |
| B | Unlikely | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-4}$ |
| C | Possible | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-2}$ |
| D | Likely | $10^{-2}$ – $10^{0}$ |
| E | Frequent | $> 1$ |

**Step 3 — Build the 5×5 Risk Matrix.**

The risk score for a given scenario is:

$$R_{\text{score}} = L_{\text{level}} \times C_{\text{level}}$$

**Step 4 — Plot and identify "tolerability" zones.**

| | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **L5** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **L4** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **L3** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **L2** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **L1** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic:** "Green = Accept, Yellow = Manage (ALARP), Red = Avoid/Mitigate." Most KTU problems expect a 3-zone (Acceptable / Tolerable / Unacceptable) classification, not 5.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — The Safety Design Lifecycle

The diagram below maps the **iterative safety workflow** that any process plant must follow from concept to commissioning, with feedback loops enabling continuous improvement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conceptual Design Phase] --> B[Inherent Safety Review ISR]
    B --> C[PFD and P and ID Development]
    C --> D[Preliminary Hazard Analysis PHA]
    D --> E[Detailed Engineering Phase]
    E --> F[HAZOP Study on P and ID]
    F --> G[Layer of Protection Analysis LOPA]
    G --> H[Safety Integrity Level SIL Determination]
    H --> I[Quantitative Risk Assessment QRA]
    I --> J{Residual Risk ALARP}
    J -->|Yes| K[Safety Design Validated]
    J -->|No| L[Design Modification Loop]
    L --> C
    K --> M[Procurement and Construction]
    M --> N[Pre Startup Safety Review PSSR]
    N --> O[Commissioning and Operation]
    O --> P[Management of Change MOC]
    P --> F
```

## 4.2 Bow-Tie Diagram — Layers of Protection in a Single Picture

The **Bow-Tie** is the KTU-favorite schematic. It shows the *threats* on the left, the *consequence* on the right, and the *protection layers* on the top (prevention) and bottom (mitigation) wings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Threat: Pump Seal Failure] --> P1a[Layer 1: BPCS Interlock]
    P1a --> P1b[Layer 2: Operator Alarm]
    P1b --> P1c[Layer 3: SIF SIL 2 Trip]
    P1c --> E[Top Event: Loss of Containment]
    E --> M1a[Layer 4: Pressure Relief Valve]
    M1a --> M1b[Layer 5: Bund and Dike]
    M1b --> M1c[Layer 6: Emergency Response]
    M1c --> C[Consequence: Pool Fire and Fatality]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading rule:** Anything on the **left wing** *prevents* the top event. Anything on the **right wing** *mitigates* the consequence. The number of effective layers on each side is a quick visual indicator of design robustness.

## 4.3 HAZOP Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Define Scope and Objectives] --> S2[Step 2: Assemble Multidisciplinary Team]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Divide Plant into Nodes]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: For Each Node Apply Guide Words to Parameters]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Identify Deviations and Causes]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Evaluate Consequences]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: List Existing Safeguards]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Determine Risk Level]
    S8 --> S9{Risk Acceptable}
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Record and Continue to Next Deviation]
    S9 -->|No| S11[Generate Recommendation and Action]
    S11 --> S12[Assign Action to Responsible Party]
    S12 --> S10
    S10 --> S4
    S4 -->|All Nodes Done| S13[Step 9: Compile HAZOP Report and Closeout]
```

## 4.4 Risk Assessment Methodology Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is this an early project phase with limited data}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Use Checklist or What If]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Are P and IDs available}
    Q2 -->|No| P2[Use Preliminary Hazard Analysis PHA]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Is quantitative consequence data available}
    Q3 -->|No| P3[Use HAZOP with qualitative risk matrix]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Is the facility a high hazard installation}
    Q4 -->|Yes| P4[Apply LOPA and QRA and SIL Determination]
    Q4 -->|No| P5[Use HAZOP with risk matrix only]
```
